being.name: BEING
being.definition: A state of experience and behavior in which the actor is affected by internal and external stimuli and responds by expressing feelings on an organic, moment-to-moment basis. It is the state where the greatest number of personality facets are available to the actor as a creative artist.
being.details: BEING is the foundational prerequisite to all craft work. It is not just an acting state but a living state that should exist 24 hours a day. The actor who is truly BEING is unique, individual, and unlike any other person. BEING is achieved when internal and external reality flows uninterrupted through expression. The split between what an actor really feels and what he expresses is the core problem BEING addresses. BEING is contrasted with 'natural' acting, which is the imitation of truth rather than truth itself. Conditioning, social taboos, and behavioral restrictions are major obstacles to BEING.

sources.summary: Definition: BEING is a state of experience/behavior wherein actor is affected by stimuli and responds organically moment-to-moment; the nucleus of talent.
Development of BEING concept: originated from observing split between actor's real feelings and their expressed behavior.
BEING is the bridge between theory and application; prerequisite to using the Method and craft techniques.
BEING is a living state, 24 hours a day; conditioning and taboos are major obstacles.

additions.summary: Ensemble acting is the highest form of organic acting; it is based on BEING. Without BEING, ensemble is impossible.
BEING is the basis of organic reality, irreverence, Ultimate Consciousness, and ensemble—the foundation of theatrically memorable performances.

instrumental_preparation.name: Instrumental Preparation
instrumental_preparation.definition: Work specifically designed to free the actor's personal instrument—the actor himself—so that emotional blocks, inhibitions, fears, and conditioning can be eliminated, making the actor receptive to craft techniques.
instrumental_preparation.details: The 'instrument' is the actor himself. Stanislavsky's Method lacked this component, which is why it failed for many actors. Without instrumental preparation, even valid craft techniques are useless for actors with emotional blocks. Instrumental preparation must address the specific problems standing in the way of the actor's personal truth: fear, insecurity, behavioral conditioning, social obligations. Morris's first two books (No Acting Please; Being & Doing) focused almost exclusively on instrumental preparation.

sources.summary: Morris discovered emotional blocks prevent actors from using techniques; devised BEING as prerequisite.
Stanislavsky's system did not recognize need for instrumental preparation—the missing link in the Method.
Dozens of instrumental problems (fear, insecurity, conditioning) stand in the way of the creative process.

missing_link_in_the_method.name: Missing Link in the Method
missing_link_in_the_method.definition: The gap between theory and application in the Stanislavsky system, caused by the system's failure to address instrumental preparation—the need to free the actor's emotional instrument before craft techniques can work.
missing_link_in_the_method.details: Actors who are naturally emotionally free had success with the Method; those with blocks could not apply it. Stanislavsky himself admitted his approach was incomplete. Morris identifies instrumental preparation as the missing link that bridges theory and application. The bridge is achieving BEING: a state of authentic, comfortable life from which the actor can feel and express anything.

sources.summary: The reason the Method failed universally: it did not recognize need for instrumental preparation.
The missing link is the gap between theory and application; BEING is the bridge.

craft.name: Craft
craft.definition: A method of creating realities on stage and in front of the camera. It is the means by which an actor builds a performance, fulfills characterization, identifies obligations and responsibilities of material, and makes choices to accomplish the author's intent.
craft.details: Craft is a process of work. Many actors talk about craft without having one, or practice abstract, intellectual techniques that don't stimulate organic behavior. The key question is not 'what' but 'how.' Knowing what to do without knowing how is useless. Craft without instrumental preparation (BEING) as a foundation is ineffective. This book is dedicated entirely to the craft process, organized into four major categories.

sources.summary: Craft is a process of creating realities on stage/camera; the subject of this book.
Many actors have no real craft or use abstract intellectual techniques; the key word is 'how.'

emotional_point_of_view.name: Emotional Point of View
emotional_point_of_view.definition: The actor's authentic, moment-to-moment feeling-responses to everything in their environment—people, objects, sounds, sensations. Cultivating emotional point of view means becoming aware of how you really feel about all stimuli, not just the 'major' ones.
emotional_point_of_view.details: Most people are only aware of major stimuli; widening the panorama of responsive awareness is the goal. When strung together in an unbroken chain, emotional-point-of-view responses constitute the moment-to-moment impulses of BEING. The Emotional-Point-of-View Inventory is the primary technique for developing this. There is also a nonverbal version where sounds replace words, preventing intellectualization.

sources.summary: Emotional point of view: asking how you feel about everything around you; builds impulse-awareness and BEING.
Emotional-Point-of-View Inventory builds ability to respond and express all impulses, creating organic flow of BEING.
Extended example of running an emotional-point-of-view inventory in detail.

moment_to_moment.name: Moment-to-Moment
moment_to_moment.definition: The unbroken, real-time flow of authentic impulses and responses as they arise in the present, without pre-planning or imposition. The basis of truthful acting.
moment_to_moment.details: On stage there is always a life going on under and through the lines—the inner monologue. Actors often cut off this internal process, replacing reality with awareness of how they are acting, what the next line is, or performance evaluation. Re-establishing moment-to-moment flow through exercises like the Emotional-Point-of-View Inventory eliminates tension and dimensionalizes expression.

sources.summary: Inner life on stage goes on under/through lines; actors cut it off and replace it with performance-monitoring.
Involvement with own flow of reality eliminates tension and makes work unpredictable and compelling.

inclusion.name: Inclusion
inclusion.definition: The practice of recognizing and incorporating ALL impulses—distractions, self-consciousness, confusion, fear, irrelevant thoughts—into the acting, rather than suppressing them. Turning potential liabilities into assets.
inclusion.details: In the midst of a scene, actors experience impulses inconsistent with the emotional life of the play. These are real and must not be disregarded. Refusing to include these impulses creates a short-circuiting that splits the actor from internal reality. Including all impulses makes the actor compelling, unpredictable, and colorful. Inclusion is one of the pillars of the irreverent approach to acting. The stimulus-affect-response-expression chain must not be interrupted. Denying any reality on stage interferes with the natural living process.

sources.summary: The Inclusion technique: using monologue to practice including all impulses, even those unrelated to the scene.
How to practice the Inclusion technique; checking impulses via variation in repeated monologue performances.
Conditioning creates taboos that short-circuit impulse flow; including the short-circuit feelings restores authenticity.

additions.summary: Including all impulses is part of irreverent acting; no creative alternative to inclusion exists.
The test for inclusion in performance: ask 'Is it not possible for the character to behave that way at that moment?' If yes, it is permissible. Almost always the answer is yes.

organic_impulse.name: Organic Impulse
organic_impulse.definition: An authentic, spontaneous feeling or behavioral response that arises naturally from the actor's instrument without imposition or intellectual control.
organic_impulse.details: Organic impulses are the raw material of truthful acting. They can be stimulated by craft choices and approaches but must not be manufactured or imposed. The goal of both BEING preparation and craft work is to stimulate organic impulses appropriate to the material.

sources.summary: Craft techniques stimulate organic behavior; intellectual techniques prevent it.
Instrumental exercises help actors experience and express organic impulses with greater honesty and dimension.

conditioning.name: Conditioning
conditioning.definition: The social, parental, and cultural influences that create habitual patterns of emotional redirection, inhibition, and suppression. A major obstacle to BEING.
conditioning.details: Conditioning is often subtle; parents and peers have enormous impact on emotional development without the actor realizing it. Conditioning causes actors to redirect real feelings and impulses into more socially acceptable areas. When an actor's conditioned taboos are triggered on stage, they short-circuit the flow of impulses. Most people grow up with restrictions they are not even aware of—they don't discuss 'personal' things.

sources.summary: Conditioning—behavioral conditioning and social obligations—is one of dozens of instrumental problems blocking creative process.
Conditioning creates lifetime taboos and private areas that short-circuit organic impulse flow on stage.

fear_of_exposure.name: Fear of Exposure
fear_of_exposure.definition: The terror actors feel about being truly seen—having their authentic feelings, vulnerabilities, and inner life visible to others. A major block to BEING freedom.
fear_of_exposure.details: Fear of exposure operates even when the actor intellectually knows exposure is safe. It keeps actors from BEING in life and on stage. The Intimate Sharing exercise with another actor is a technique specifically designed to address this fear.

sources.summary: Fear of exposure is a major block to BEING freedom; Intimate Sharing exercise addresses it.

irreverence.name: Irreverence
irreverence.definition: Honoring your real feelings and impulses in the moment rather than servicing the material conceptually. Expressing what is real even when it does not fulfill the scene's requirement in that instant. Being reverent to yourself rather than to a concept.
irreverence.details: Irreverence is NOT license for undisciplined rambling—it is part of a specific, disciplined process with rules and structure. It has three distinct states: Philosophical, Rehearsal, and Performance Irreverence. The actor who functions from reality exposes himself uniquely—no two actors can play the same role the same way. Irreverence means the actor must commit to exploring choices wherever they lead, even away from expected results. Being irreverent to the material IS being reverent to yourself. 'False irreverence' is when actors take liberties to cover lack of craft—the real irreverent actor knows what they are doing and why.

sources.summary: Definition: Irreverence = honoring real feelings/impulses rather than servicing material conceptually. Must express real over conceptual.
Irreverence allows actor's unique individuality to emerge; two actors playing same role differ because of unique personalities.
Being irreverent to the material means being reverent to yourself. Must follow choice wherever it leads.
Rehearsal irreverence has structure and rules; it is not license for helter-skelter exploration.

philosophical_irreverence.name: Philosophical Irreverence
philosophical_irreverence.definition: The first state of irreverence—accepting and embracing the concept of irreverence as a way of life and work. Accepting yourself: who you are, what you feel, how you express yourself, and how you experience the world as the most important contribution you have to make.
philosophical_irreverence.details: Requires courage to not run scared under the pressure of a take or an entrance. Means standing your ground when others in the production work differently. Requires communicating your process to the director so they understand and support it. Developing a BEING consciousness instead of a DOING consciousness prevents moment-to-moment reality from being violated for results. Ego preparations are tools for establishing philosophical irreverence.

sources.summary: Philosophical irreverence: first step is accepting the concept. Accepting irreverence = accepting yourself.
Requires standing ground with process even when others resist; communicate approach to director.
BEING consciousness vs. DOING consciousness; result consciousness is the main trap to avoid.

rehearsal_irreverence.name: Rehearsal Irreverence
rehearsal_irreverence.definition: The state of irreverence in rehearsal—exploring choices adventurously, following impulses wherever they lead, experimenting without yet knowing what works, and using the rehearsal period for discovery rather than polishing.
rehearsal_irreverence.details: The most adventurous and experimental stage of the creative process. Actor identifies obligations, selects choices, and uses choice approaches while remaining irreverent in their execution. Actor includes all peripheral life stimulated by choices AND what is happening on stage. Blocking imposed by directors can be creatively adjusted through organic impetus rather than mechanical compliance. Use of special rehearsal techniques (gibberish, sounds, paraphrasing, nursery rhymes, etc.) liberates the actor from concept.

sources.summary: Rehearsal irreverence: most irreverent stage; actor explores and experiments.
Actor uses rehearsal to identify obligations, select choices, explore with choice approaches while being irreverent.
Has structure and rules; depends on identifying responsibilities and exploring techniques that fulfill them. Like following a road map.
Gains from rehearsal irreverence: reality vs representation, discovery, real-life satisfaction, dimensional life.

performance_irreverence.name: Performance Irreverence
performance_irreverence.definition: The state of irreverence in performance—by this stage the actor has found the right choices and approaches, knows when to use each, but remains available to unexpected impulses and includes them as nourishment rather than threats.
performance_irreverence.details: The 'seeking and exploring' of rehearsal has borne fruit—the choices are set, the role is created. Actor knows what choices to use and when to shift from one to another. New, unexpected impulses in performance are 'food' of creative impulses rather than problems. Choices in long runs may lose strength; actor can add fresh sensory elements or adjust choices. Personal Inventory run as an Inner Monologue is the antidote when impulsive flow is interrupted during performance.

sources.summary: Main difference between rehearsal and performance irreverence: in performance the choices are set; exploration is over.
Facilitated emotions have a beginning/middle/end and don't authentically give way to next impulse; real organic choices vary naturally.
When impulsive flow is interrupted in performance, use Personal Inventory as Inner Monologue to climb back to organicity.
When choices lose strength over a run, add fresh sensory elements to the choice rather than abandoning it.

additions.summary: Irreverence in performance story: Joan Hotchkis screamed mid-scene; including this impulse freed her to function organically for the rest of the scene.

result_consciousness.name: Result Consciousness
result_consciousness.definition: The trap of focusing on the desired emotional outcome of a scene rather than on the present moment's reality. When knowing the result of a scene or emotion becomes stronger than the current impulse, the actor leads or manipulates emotional life toward the desired result.
result_consciousness.details: Contrasted with BEING consciousness (moment-to-moment reality) and DOING consciousness (result-focused). Causes a split in consciousness: part of the actor is not fully in the choice but is watching for the resulting emotion. In real life, people experience grief and want not to; actor works for grief because they want to. This reversal is the tell of result consciousness. The antidote is to start from a real state of life, work for a choice that really affects the actor, and allow the natural human response.

sources.summary: Result consciousness: knowing responsibility of scene causes actor to lead emotional life toward result rather than moment.
Split in consciousness when result awareness competes with choice involvement. Real-life experience is the reverse of performing for result.

ensemble.name: Ensemble
ensemble.definition: The highest form of organic acting: two or more actors functioning entirely from BEING, each fully available to the other's moment-to-moment impulses, creating an emotional chain reaction where even the actors don't know where it will lead.
ensemble.details: Ensemble is achieved ultimately when actors are impulsively available to each other on a second-to-second basis. Presenting intellectual concepts of behavior makes real ensemble impossible. Irreverent rehearsal with committed choices by both actors creates the dynamic tension from which ensemble emerges. When one actor has a stronger reality and 'wins,' both actors grow from the experience.

sources.summary: Ensemble: highest form of organic acting; chain of organic responses between actors who are fully available to each other.
BEING is the basis of ensemble; without BEING, ensemble is impossible.
Two committed actors with opposing realities create the dynamic from which ensemble emerges.

eleventh_level_of_consciousness.name: Eleventh Level of Consciousness
eleventh_level_of_consciousness.definition: A concept where on the first ten levels of consciousness the actor is totally involved in the flow of reality, but on the eleventh level the actor retains awareness that they are performing on stage—ensuring craft responsibilities are fulfilled without breaking the reality.
eleventh_level_of_consciousness.details: This is the resolution to the apparent contradiction between full organic immersion and theatrical craft responsibility. The actor says proper words, doesn't actually strangle the leading lady, etc., all from this eleventh level. Morris says he will explore this concept in more depth later in the book.

sources.summary: Eleventh Level: on ten levels actor is in reality; eleventh level knows they are 'acting' and preserves theatrical responsibilities.

preparation.name: Preparation (Craft Preparations)
preparation.definition: The largest part of acting—the work the actor does to become ready to use craft. Preparation falls into instrumental and craft areas. Instrumental preparation brings the actor to a BEING state; craft preparation specifically addresses and changes emotional states in relation to the material.
preparation.details: There are eight major categories of preparation: standard relaxation cluster, involvement preparations, emotional preparations, ego preparations, large expurgatives, relationship preparations, interim preparations, and irreverent preparations. Each category can be used both instrumentally (to eliminate blocks/achieve BEING) and craftually (to stimulate specific emotional states for material). The same exercise used with different emphasis serves either instrumental or craft purposes. Example: Exorcism used instrumentally purges blocked emotion; used as craft preparation, it stimulates specific emotional states (anger, rage, frustration) that can be carried directly into a scene.

sources.summary: Three major craft categories: preparation, obligations/choices/choice-approaches. Eight major preparation categories listed.
Distinction: instrumental preparation eliminates blocks to BEING; craft preparation affects/changes emotional state in relation to material. Same exercise, different emphasis.

relaxation.name: Relaxation (Standard Relaxation Cluster)
relaxation.definition: The starting point of all acting work. Relaxation is the only place from which an artist can create. The cluster includes physical relaxers, personal inventory, and sensitizing.
relaxation.details: Tension is the enemy of creativity. Physical relaxation techniques include weight-and-gravity relaxation, tensing and relaxing, deep breathing, and stretching and yawning. Relaxation can be done before scene work or at any time during rehearsal/performance. In instrumental use, more time, louder sounds, and overall physical approach. In craft use, must be faster and less obtrusive.

sources.summary: Relaxation is the start mark; the only point from which an artist can create.
Physical relaxers: weight-and-gravity, tensing-and-relaxing, deep breathing. Can be done while waiting on set or stage.

involvement.name: Involvement
involvement.definition: The natural enemy of tension and self-consciousness. The capacity to be genuinely absorbed by—selflessly interested in—people, objects, and the environment. Involvement is essential for authentic relationship work.
involvement.details: Threshold of Interest is the principle: start with what genuinely interests you. Never force involvement. The available-stimulus sweep, wonder/perceive/observe, and sensory exploration are the primary involvement techniques. Without being selflessly involved, the actor cannot achieve the kind of relationship that stimulates reality in theater or film.

sources.summary: Involvement is the natural enemy of tension and self-consciousness.
The involvement group: large area of disciplines; without selfless involvement, authentic relationship is impossible.

threshold_of_interest.name: Threshold of Interest
threshold_of_interest.definition: The principle that it is always easier and more productive to relate to things that genuinely interest you than to force involvement with things that don't. Start with what is actually interesting to you—even a small part of an object or place.
threshold_of_interest.details: Applied to involvement work, this principle means: look for the parts of a place, object, or person that actually fascinate you. Applied to choice work, it leads you right into the heart of the choice. Saves time and energy—authentic interest generates real involvement.

sources.summary: Threshold of Interest: look for things that genuinely interest you; apply to all involvement and choice work.

personal_inventory.name: Personal Inventory
personal_inventory.definition: A self-questioning process done before and during a scene to identify and express what the actor is really feeling moment-to-moment. The question is simply: 'How do I feel?' The answers are then expressed or incorporated into the scene.
personal_inventory.details: Part of the Standard Relaxation Cluster. Instrumental use: discovering what you are feeling and getting to a place where you can express impulses moment-to-moment. Craft use: done before the first line and during the scene whenever the actor feels disconnected from internal reality. In performance: run as a silent Inner Monologue under the lines; its responses become the flow of the character's life. The antidote for premeditation, disconnection, and mechanical performance.

sources.summary: Personal Inventory: last part of relaxation cluster; discover what you feel; used instrumentally and craftually.
Craft use: used before first line and during scene when losing connection to internal reality.
Running Personal Inventory as Inner Monologue in performance example; responses get included in scene behavior.

inner_monologue.name: Inner Monologue
inner_monologue.definition: The continuous, silent stream of consciousness the actor maintains under and through the spoken lines of a scene during performance. Unlike the Inner-Outer Monologue exercise (which is spoken), the Inner Monologue remains silent and functions as the actor's internal reality.
inner_monologue.details: In real life, everyone always has some kind of internal monologue going on. Acting cuts this off—the Inner Monologue restores it. If the choice is affecting the actor, the Inner Monologue will become the flow of life of the character—a collaboration between actor and character. This allows the character to inhabit the actor. The actor does NOT become the character; the character becomes the actor. The nature of the Inner Monologue is decided by whatever stimulus is affecting the actor.

sources.summary: Inner Monologue: silent stream of consciousness under the lines; different from Inner-Outer Monologue which is audible.
If choice affects actor, Inner Monologue becomes character's life—character becomes the actor rather than actor becoming character.

disconnected_impulsivity.name: Disconnected Impulsivity
disconnected_impulsivity.definition: A technique for stimulating quick impulsive responses during a scene by encouraging moment-to-moment impulses—internal and external—that don't necessarily relate to each other. Can range from logical responses to completely unrelated thoughts.
disconnected_impulsivity.details: Used to eliminate leadership (premeditation) of any kind. Takes place internally—impulses are activated under lines, between lines, or while listening to the other actor. What it looks like from outside: a person responding naturally to the environment, the other character, and their own thoughts. Very natural; charges low-energy scenes; excites relationships. Should only be used for as long as needed—not indiscriminately.

sources.summary: Disconnected Impulsivity technique: encourage expression of moment-to-moment impulses under/through lines; impulses need not relate to each other.
How it looks from outside: person responding to environment, other actor, and personal thoughts. Natural; charges low-energy scenes.

sensitizing.name: Sensitizing
sensitizing.definition: The practice of heightening the responsiveness of one or more of the five senses through concentrated, directed attention. Can be used instrumentally as part of relaxation or craftually when senses are sluggish during sensory choice work.
sensitizing.details: Part of the Standard Relaxation Cluster when done with all five senses instrumentally. As craft technique: used selectively when a specific sense is unresponsive during choice exploration. 'Live' in the particular sense—concentrate all energy into that sense (auditory: hear every element of the other actor's voice; tactile: center focus in the specific body part). Sensitizing during a scene becomes part of the reality rather than an interruption.

sources.summary: Sensitizing: part of relaxation cluster instrumentally with all senses; craftually used selectively when senses are sluggish during choice work.
How to sensitize: 'live in' the specific sense until it tingles with responsiveness; can be done mid-scene as part of the reality.

emotional_preparation.name: Emotional Preparation
emotional_preparation.definition: A specific category of craft preparations designed to affect the actor's emotional state—to elevate accessibility, change emotional state from one place to another, seduce the instrument into emotional surrender, and manipulate the actor into the emotional reality demanded by the material.
emotional_preparation.details: The entire area of emotional preparations is specifically used to: affect the actor's emotional state, elevate emotional accessibility, change from one emotional state to another, seduce the instrument into emotional surrender, and generally manipulate the actor into the area of emotional reality the material demands. Timing of the preparation matters: how long you want to maintain the emotional pitch and what level you want to attain determines when and which preparation to use. Key preparations include: Vulnerability Workout, Talking to People About Meaningful Things, Coffin Monologues, Sharing and Double Exposure, Emotionally Meaningful Imaginary Monologues, Sense Memory with Emotionally Impacting Objects, Music, Other Meaningful Sounds, Passions Workout.

sources.summary: Emotional preparations: used to affect emotional state, elevate accessibility, change emotional state, seduce the instrument into emotional surrender.
Selection of preparation is as important as selection of choice; sets up entire system of work.

ego_preparation.name: Ego Preparations (Chapter 3 Craft Focus)
ego_preparation.definition: Preparations designed to address and build the actor's ego state in relation to the character's ego state. The first step is identifying what the character's ego state is, then what the actor's current ego state is, and then choosing a preparation to bridge the gap.
ego_preparation.details: The gauge for what craft preparation is needed: examine the character's ego state (how do they feel about themselves generally? In the world? In this scene?) vs. the actor's current state. A heavily damaged or abused ego may require psychotherapy alongside acting study. Key preparations: Superman (for large ego obligations), Counting Your Blessings (gratitude and self-appreciation list), Sensory Self-Endowments (sensorially endowing oneself with desired physical features), Accepting the Academy Award, Fantasies and Self-Aggrandizements, Progress Reports, Magic-Pocket Exercise, Two-Person Validations. Be careful not to over-prepare with a large ego technique for a subtle ego state—use appropriate magnitude.

sources.summary: Ego preparations for craft: first determine character's ego state, then identify your own, then pick the preparation to bridge the gap.
Variations on Superman; use appropriate magnitude for the ego obligation; don't overkill subtle states.

large_expurgatives.name: Large Expurgatives (The Terrific Trio)
large_expurgatives.definition: A cluster of three high-energy exercises (Abandonment, Vesuvius, Exorcism) used to purge tension, stimulate large emotional responses, and build foundations for volatile or emotionally extreme characters. The Terrific Trio.
large_expurgatives.details: Each exercise starts very arbitrarily—the actor jumps in and starts, hoping it will accomplish the desired state. Large expurgatives are designed to produce large expressive emotions; for subtler emotional levels, they are not the right choice. Additional large-expurgative preparations: Confront and Encounter, Go Crazy, Anger-and-Rage Expression. Instrumentally: purge tension and conglomerated impulses. Craftually: stimulate specific emotional states that can be carried into the scene.

sources.summary: The Terrific Trio: Abandonment, Vesuvius, Exorcism. Used with different emphasis as instrumental or craft preparations.
Large expurgatives produce large expressive emotions; not right for subtle emotional levels.

vulnerability.name: Vulnerability
vulnerability.definition: The state of emotional openness and availability to be genuinely affected—a key quality for authentic acting and for achieving BEING. Vulnerability rises in direct proportion to impulsivity.
vulnerability.details: Vulnerability is not weakness; it is the actor's openness to being genuinely affected by stimuli. As vulnerability level climbs, so does impulsivity—a necessary ingredient for organic reality. Fear of exposure is the primary block to vulnerability. The Vulnerability Workout and Intimate Sharing are the primary techniques for building vulnerability.

sources.summary: As vulnerability level climbs, so does impulsivity—necessary ingredient for organic reality.

sensory_self_endowment.name: Sensory Self-Endowment
sensory_self_endowment.definition: Using sensory questions to create the felt experience of having different physical features than one actually has—adding or eliminating any aspect of the body—as an ego or character preparation.
sensory_self_endowment.details: As a craft preparation: used to feel better about oneself and to establish a foundation for how the character feels about themselves. Process is the same as any Sense Memory exercise—asking sensory questions and responding sensorially. Can be used to add or eliminate virtually anything about the body. As a choice approach: could relate to negative endowments (overweight, larger features, etc.).

sources.summary: Sensory Self-Endowments: use sense memory questions to create experience of different physical features; build ego foundation for character.
Example: sensorially creating long, thick hair. Can add or eliminate almost anything about the body.

relationship_preparations.name: Relationship Preparations
relationship_preparations.definition: A category of craft preparations specifically designed to build, deepen, or modify the actor's real relationship with their scene partner so that it approaches the relationship demanded by the material.
relationship_preparations.details: Nonverbal communication techniques remove the obligation to verbalize, transferring energy to other kinds of relating—making the actor more aware, more related, more involved, more emotionally communicative. Key techniques: Nonverbal Communication, A Nonverbal Relating Exercise, Telepathy, Looking for Things in the Other Person You Haven't Seen Before, Wonder/Perceive/Observe, Feedback, Like-and-Dislike Feedback, Intimate Sharing, Endowments. The difference between using Wonder/Perceive/Observe for involvement vs. for relationship: involvement = achieve involvement; relationship = consider ingredients of the relationship and adjust the exercise to promote the desired emotional point of view.

sources.summary: Nonverbal communication removes word-obligation; energy transfers to direct relating—more aware, related, involved, emotionally communicative.
Wonder/Perceive/Observe used differently in relationship preparations: adjust to promote specific relationship feelings demanded by material.

nonverbal_communication.name: Nonverbal Communication
nonverbal_communication.definition: A category of techniques where actors relate without words, which transfers the energy normally used for verbal description into direct emotional contact, deepening involvement and relationship.
nonverbal_communication.details: Words train us to describe feelings rather than express them. We also spend time thinking about what to say next, which interferes with involvement. Removing verbal obligation allows energy to be used for other kinds of relating. Result: more awareness of the other actor, more involvement, more emotional communicativeness. Techniques include: A Nonverbal Relating Exercise, Telepathy.

sources.summary: Nonverbal communication: removing word obligation transfers energy to direct relating; more involvement, more emotional communication.

obligations.name: Obligations
obligations.definition: The elements of the playwright's material that the actor must make equally real for themselves. The realities of the play: place, time, relationships, theme, emotional involvement of each character, and the impact characters have on each other. These are what the actor is responsible for creating organically.
obligations.details: There are seven main obligations: Time-and-Place, Relationship, Emotional, Character, Historic, Thematic, and Subtextual. The order of importance and approach varies with each piece of material. Morris contrasts obligations with Stanislavsky's 'objectives, superobjectives, actions, activities'—calling the latter counter-creative. Obligations defined in terms of the actor's task to create (not just play) allow the actor to use craft (choice approaches) to organically stimulate the needed reality. The seven obligations serve as a blueprint for approaching material.

sources.summary: Obligations are the playwright's stated realities that the actor must make equally real for themselves; the heart of craft.
Seven main obligations listed: Time-and-Place, Emotional, Thematic, Relationship, Character, Subtextual, Historic.

time_and_place_obligation.name: Time-and-Place Obligation
time_and_place_obligation.definition: The obligation to make the place and time of the scene—where and when it occurs—genuinely real to the actor so that it affects them as it would affect the character.
time_and_place_obligation.details: Every scene takes place somewhere at a specific time of day, night, or year. The playwright chooses these for communicative/expressive reasons. The actor must create the time and place so as to be affected just as the character is. Options: use available stimuli (the existing set), add meaningful objects, or create a wholly different place. Standard questions for approaching this obligation: Where does it take place? How does it affect the characters? What do the characters say about it? Why does it take place here? Even if time/place is not the most important obligation in a scene, it must never be disregarded.

sources.summary: Time-and-Place: every scene occurs somewhere at a specific time; playwright's choice is meaningful; actor must make it equally real.
Identifying how place and time affect the character; standard questions to ask when approaching the obligation.
Options for fulfilling time-and-place: use available stimuli, add objects, or create a wholly different place.

relationship_obligation.name: Relationship Obligation
relationship_obligation.definition: The actor's responsibility to make real—through craft—who the other characters are to their character, including relationship responsibility (who is the other person to the character) and the emotional obligation of the relationship (how the character feels toward them).
relationship_obligation.details: Relationship Responsibility: who the other characters are (wife, father, adversary, lover, etc.) and establishing a parallel reality with the actual actor playing the role. The Emotional Obligation of Relationship: how the character feels about each of the other characters. The actor uses endowments and sensory processes to make the other actor feel like the character they are supposed to be. Unless doing a solo dramatic reading, there will always be relationship obligations.

sources.summary: Relationship obligation: who the other characters are and how the character feels about them; must be made equally real.
Actor's responsibility: make the other actor be whatever the script says they are, through craft. Example: making actor feel like father.

emotional_obligation.name: Emotional Obligation
emotional_obligation.definition: What the character feels in every given scene and in every moment throughout the play, and how those emotions change. The emotional life the actor must organically stimulate for themselves.
emotional_obligation.details: The emotional obligation is the most central obligation in most scenes. The actor identifies the emotional obligation, then makes choices to create the realities that promote the emotional experience. Choices must address the full emotional picture—not just stimulate surface frustration without the underlying passion and need that motivate it. Example: If the character desperately wants to become an actor, the actor must first work for choices that create the passionate desire, then find choices that create the obstacles to it.

sources.summary: Emotional obligation: what the character feels in every scene and how those emotions change; actor must stimulate these organically.
Must work for the full emotional pyramid—passion and need plus obstacles—not just surface frustration without underlying drive.

character_obligation.name: Character Obligation
character_obligation.definition: The actor's responsibility to create the character's qualities in four areas: physical, emotional, intellectual, and psychological. Identifying differences AND similarities between actor and character, then finding organic ways to bridge them.
character_obligation.details: Four parts: physical, emotional, intellectual, psychological. Start by identifying the four areas of the character as described by the playwright, then compare to yourself. For differences: find choices and approaches that organically stimulate the character's qualities. For similarities: the actor IS the character in those areas; no additional work is needed. The goal: the character inhabits the actor. Physical character work can involve externals (animals), lifting weights, costumes, Sensory Self-Endowments, etc. When play is cast, actors are usually chosen for similarities to the character—but differences must be addressed.

sources.summary: Character obligation: four parts—physical, emotional, intellectual, psychological; identify differences and similarities.
For physical differences: choices and approaches can organically change movement, relationship to body, and physical personality.
Similarities: actor IS the character in those areas; no work needed. The goal is for the character to inhabit the actor.

character_inhabiting_actor.name: Character Inhabiting the Actor
character_inhabiting_actor.definition: The principle that the actor does not become the character; rather, the character becomes the actor. The character inhabits the actor's authentic organic life rather than the actor imposing a concept of the character from outside.
character_inhabiting_actor.details: This is a fundamental reversal of the common acting trap of 'becoming' a character by imposing external behavior. When organic choices successfully stimulate the character's emotional life, the character's impulses flow through the actor's authentic being. The Inner Monologue becomes the character's inner life—a collaboration between actor and character.

sources.summary: The actor does not become the character; the character becomes the actor through the Inner Monologue and organic choices.
Character inhabiting actor: bridging differences and similarities brings character into the actor's organic life.

historic_obligation.name: Historic Obligation
historic_obligation.definition: The actor's responsibility to create the realities of the historical period of the play—its manner, speech patterns, thought processes, concerns, beliefs, technological realities, dress, and physical movement—so that they are authentically felt rather than merely implied.
historic_obligation.details: All elements of a historical period impact how the character looks at life, relates to people, speaks, thinks, acts, walks, and talks. The actor must find substitutes or parallels in their own life for each historical element. Emotions have been universally the same since the beginning of recorded time; any character's experience can be restimulated by personally meaningful choices. Dress/costume can go a long way in exciting imagination and changing contemporary movement. Truth comes from a place of truth; failing to create the historical foundation makes it impossible to be real in other obligation areas.

sources.summary: Historic obligation: manner, speech, thought process, concerns, beliefs, physical movement must all be organically created.
Emotions are universally the same across time; find personally meaningful parallels. Costume helps with physical movement.

thematic_obligation.name: Thematic Obligation
thematic_obligation.definition: The actor's responsibility to carry the statement of the play or film—what the writer is saying, often why they wrote the piece. Sometimes divided among characters; often assigned to a single character as the playwright's spokesman.
thematic_obligation.details: The theme is what the play says; the emotional life of the play expresses the theme through the characters' behavior. The actor must ask: Does my character have a responsibility to promote the statement of the play? Shaw often singled out one character to espouse his philosophy; many playwrights embed their statement in the emotional journey of the characters.

sources.summary: Thematic obligation: carry the statement of the play; ask whether character is the playwright's spokesman.
Theme is what the play says and why the writer wrote it; character's journey embodies the theme.

subtextual_obligation.name: Subtextual Obligation
subtextual_obligation.definition: The most subtle and usually hardest to identify obligation—the unsaid things in the play, the statements that live underneath the written words, the emotional reality that exists between and beneath the explicit dialogue.
subtextual_obligation.details: Subtext is what is meant but not said. It is the underlying emotional reality that gives the written words their deeper meaning. The actor's responsibility is to make the subtext real to themselves so that it lives in their behavior throughout the scene.

sources.summary: Subtextual obligation: the unsaid things in the play, the most subtle and hardest to identify of all seven obligations.

additions.summary: Subtext: unsaid things and feelings that motivate behavior; the underlife; sometimes the essence, quality, and ambiance of the material. Example: Winterset's quality of despondency and murky hopelessness.
Once subtextual responsibility is identified, actor specifies behavior goals; example of working for Pollyanna-quality choices to serve subtextual fabric.
Glass Menagerie example: dreamlike quality, nostalgia, ethereal texture—the ambiance must manifest throughout. Actress must find choices that stimulate this underlife quality.

interim_preparation.name: Interim Preparation
interim_preparation.definition: A craft preparation that bridges the gap between the actor's current emotional state and the emotional state required by the first scene obligation when that gap is too large to bridge directly. It is the priming emotion that leads to what the character is experiencing.
interim_preparation.details: In a sense, all craft preparations are interim preparations, since they are all designed to get the actor ready to deal with the first emotional responsibility of the material. The specific distinction is 'stretch and difference'—when the obligation is so extreme or distant from where the actor is emotionally that a bridge must be built. The bridge is built in steps—first reaching an intermediate emotional state, then using that as a springboard to the next step, then to the final choice. If the emotional obligation is not a stretch for the actor, no interim preparation is needed. More is often less—actors should not over-prepare out of insecurity.

sources.summary: Interim preparation: space between BEING state and material's obligation; priming emotion when obligation is too distant from current state.
Build a bridge in steps; if obligation is not a stretch, no interim preparation is needed. 'More is less, less is more.'

irreverent_preparations.name: Irreverent Preparations
irreverent_preparations.definition: A category of craft preparations designed to encourage an irreverent behavioral foundation before approaching material, especially in resistant or rigid environments. They open the instrument, expand expression, stimulate courage, and encourage trust in impulsive expression.
irreverent_preparations.details: Most irreverent preparations are large or exaggerated in nature—meant to stretch expression and stimulate courage. Often better done privately in restrictive environments (rigid directors, non-exploratory casts). If in an organically-oriented group, better to do them in front of or with others. Key irreverent preparations: Animation Workout, Freedom Workout, Go Crazy, Outrageous Monologue, Nonverbal Approach, Parallel Improvising.

sources.summary: Irreverent preparations: for resistant/rigid environments; open instrument, expand expression, encourage trust in impulse. Often better done privately.

feedback.name: Feedback
feedback.definition: A relationship preparation technique where two actors ask each other honest questions—about how they perceive each other, what they get from each other in the work, what they find interesting—in a conversational exchange. Builds authentic relationship investment.
feedback.details: Questions should relate to important things, not trivial matters. Two types: general feedback (any important questions) and Like-and-Dislike Feedback (exclusive focus on what is liked and liked least, avoiding the word 'dislike' to keep the exercise positive). If actors go after the nucleus of their curiosity with courage, they build a relationship that stands up to any material obligation.

sources.summary: Feedback: conversational exchange of important questions about each other; builds authentic relationship.
Extended example of feedback exchange covering first impressions, working styles, and mutual appreciation.

endowments.name: Endowments
endowments.definition: A choice approach that involves changing an object or person by endowing it with qualities, elements, or features it does not actually possess. The process is done sensorially—using the same kind of sensory questions as in any Sense Memory work—applied to an already existing object or person with the goal of effecting a change in it.
endowments.details: Virtually no limitations on what can be accomplished: hair color, length, height, weight, complexion, emotional attitudes, physical manifestations of illness or age, architectural features of a room, props, clothing, even gender. The Endowment process: start with the available actor or object and ask sensory questions directed at changing its perceived elements (eyes, skin, posture, sounds, smells, textures). Endowing another actor with the features of someone else: start with similarities, use contrasts, sensorially mold the actor toward the imaginary person's features, mannerisms, rhythms, odors, and sounds. The result is a conglomerate stimulus—the real actor plus the sensory sense of the imaginary person—becoming the living embodiment of the choice. Can also endow inanimate objects (a papier mache prop becomes a priceless objet d'art; a cheap violin becomes a Stradivarius). Can endow a harmless actor with the omnipresence of evil, or a healthy actor with the physical manifestations of terminal illness. One choice and one choice approach can address multiple obligations simultaneously.

sources.summary: Endowments (approach 7): changing object or person by endowing with qualities it doesn't possess; done sensorially.
Vast range: hair, height, illness, room architecture, props, props, gender; endow papier mache prop as objet d'art.
Endowment process example: aging a healthy actor—detailed sensory questions about eyes, skin, hair, posture, voice.
Endowing another actor with features of someone else: use similarities and contrasts; result is conglomerate stimulus.
Full example: actor endows actress with wife's features through hundreds of sensory questions across face, smell, body, voice, rhythm.

sense_memory.name: Sense Memory
sense_memory.definition: The foundational technique of recreating sensory experiences—objects, people, places, events—by asking questions to each of the five senses and responding sensorially, without the actual stimulus present. The cornerstone of choice approach work.
sense_memory.details: Sense Memory is both the first choice approach AND the foundation for many other techniques (Endowments, Sensory Self-Endowments, many emotional preparations). The process: ask sensory questions, respond sensorially to each. Can be done silently and unobtrusively—doesn't call attention to itself. Success depends on sensory expertise, quality of questions, and sensory availability.

sources.summary: Sense Memory can be done silently; doesn't interfere with what actor is doing or saying. Used as emotional preparation.
Sense Memory as choice approach for physical obligation (thirst): ask sensory questions related to thirst; physically and sensorially re-create and exaggerate.

additions.summary: Sense Memory as choice approach: senses can be trained to respond to absent objects as if present. With daily practice, actor can actually see, feel, smell, hear, taste non-present objects for real.
Process: start with first sensory question directed to specific sense; work through multiple senses; can ask fifty or more questions per sense area; go back and forth between senses.
Sense Memory process goes on while scene is in progress—between lines, while other actor speaks. Actor still hears other actor and is affected, but also doing the sensory process.

choices.name: Choices
choices.definition: The specific objects, people, places, events, or experiences the actor selects to create in order to fulfill the obligations of the material. The choice is what stimulates the desired emotional response through the choice approach.
choices.details: A choice must be something that genuinely affects the actor in the way the material requires. 'Wherever there is an impulse, a thought, or a feeling, there exists a choice that will stimulate it.' Choices can be: people (father, lover, enemy), places (a childhood home, a meaningful environment), objects (a photograph, a ring, a letter), events/experiences, sounds, music, time of day, weather conditions, and much more. The actor should be cataloguing, trying, using, and exploring choices all the time—an actor is an actor 24 hours a day. The key to good choices: knowing oneself and knowing what kinds of stimuli do what to you.

sources.summary: The choice is the element that stimulates the desired response—music, a place, a ring, time of day, a room and sound.
List of possible choices: people, places, sounds, music, objects, weather, physical conditions, and much more.
Actor should catalogue and explore choices all the time; knowing what stimulates each kind of feeling is prerequisite to making right choices.

choice_approaches.name: Choice Approaches
choice_approaches.definition: The twenty-two functional tools and practical techniques the actor actually uses when acting to make the chosen object real for themselves. They are the 'pick and shovel of the job'—the means by which the actor works on stage with a choice.
choice_approaches.details: While obligations and choices are intellectual decision/identification areas, choice approaches are what the actor actually uses when acting. There are currently twenty-two choice approaches and craft techniques. Originally there were only four or five. Some choice approaches work far better than others in certain material situations. The twenty-two include: Sense Memory, Available Stimulus, Sensory Suggestion, Evocative Words, Believability, Selective Emphasis, Endowments, Imaginary Monologues, Imaginary Dialogues, Externals (Animals, People, Inanimate Objects, Insects), Objects that come into contact with the body, Affective Memory, and others. A craft technique is a choice approach that can be used in conjunction with other choice approaches.

sources.summary: Choice approaches: 22 functional tools the actor actually uses when acting; the pick and shovel of the job. Grew from 4-5 over the author's teaching career.
Three-part table: Obligation → Choice → Choice Approach; examples showing how they interconnect.

task_inventory.name: Task Inventory
task_inventory.definition: A ten-step process for finding the right choices for a scene when the actor is stuck or can't identify what to use. Includes a Fail-Safe Device for when all ten steps fail.
task_inventory.details: Steps: 1) Identify the obligation clearly; 2) Restate it in complete language; 3) Ask if you have felt that way; 4) Remember when; 5) What was happening; 6) What stimulated the feeling; 7) What was the common denominator; 8) Find the experience most closely mirroring the obligation; 9) Specify the choice; 10) Explore the translation into a choice approach. Fail-Safe Device (Step 11): If all ten steps fail, ask 'What would make me feel that way if it existed in this place right now? How can I create it?' Important footnote: before the Task Inventory, ask: Is there anything in the existing environment that will already make me feel what I need?

sources.summary: Task Inventory: invented in a moment of desperation; 10-step process plus Fail-Safe Device for finding choices when stuck.
Fail-Safe Device (Step 11): when all else fails, ask what would make you feel that way if it existed right now.
Example: two experiences with common denominator of 'something told to me'; select which had most impact in relation to obligation.

daily_inventories.name: Daily Inventories
daily_inventories.definition: The practice of cataloguing and inventorying emotional responses to everyday stimuli—people, encounters, events—in specific, sensory detail for future use as choices.
daily_inventories.details: An actor should always be working—observing and perceiving human behavior, becoming familiar with how people behave and why they respond. During one day we have thousands of stimuli encounters; most pass unnoticed. The inventory process makes stimuli encounters specific and usable: isolate them, take specific sensory inventory, and catalogue them. Can catalogue a minimum of a dozen experiences a day. No one should know the actor is doing it. The Actor is an actor twenty-four hours a day.

sources.summary: Daily Inventories: actor must catalogue emotional responses to everyday stimuli; approach specifically and sensorially for future choice use.
Inventory questions: how did he look, how tall, physical attitude, expression, eye details, voice quality. Both intellectual and sensorial responses.

choice_hunt.name: Choice Hunt
choice_hunt.definition: An exercise done lying down with eyes closed, going back in time through meaningful and emotional events in the actor's life to discover and reconnect with experiences that have strong emotional content and could serve as choices.
choice_hunt.details: Done in solitude, lying on the back with eyes closed. Go back to meaningful events: graduations, holidays, weddings, picnics, family events, significant personal experiences. As memory releases its secrets, the actor begins to remember things spontaneously—even when not doing the exercise. Encourage expression of all emotional impulses stimulated by re-creating the experiences. Generates a surprising amount of impulses and feelings.

sources.summary: Choice Hunt: lying down, eyes closed, going back through meaningful events; encourages expression of stimulated impulses; memory begins to release spontaneously.

affective_memory.name: Affective Memory
affective_memory.definition: A choice approach where the actor recreates a powerful personal experience from their own life—using Sense Memory as the tool—in order to re-stimulate the authentic emotional responses that experience produced.
affective_memory.details: Affective Memory uses Sense Memory as its foundation—it cannot work without Sense Memory. This makes it both a choice approach and a craft technique (one that requires another choice approach). The experience must be recreated component part by component part (complete sensory creation), not just remembered intellectually. Example: recreating the room where aunt nearly died, using odors, visual elements, physical presence, coldness—every sensory component. Affective Memory is powerful but requires the most time and effort to execute properly.

sources.summary: Choice approach for Biff: Affective Memory for a place at a specific time and experience—all component parts recreated sensorially.
Morris's personal example: recreating room where aunt nearly died for final scene in Dark of the Moon; every sensory component used.
Affective Memory requires Sense Memory; must recreate all component parts; an entire experience as choice means creating all components.

additions.detail: Clarification of the process (pages 225-228): must not be confused with remembering or affecting memory or recalling an event. Origin: Stanislavsky called it 'Emotional Recall' or 'Memory of Emotion'; Strasberg modified and renamed it. The key: all sensory questions asked in the present tense ('What are the colors of the walls?' not 'What were the colors?'). Start the process well before the actual moment of the experience—create the entire environmental and relational context through Sense Memory, then 'sneak up on' the unconscious. First exploration may take 2-3 hours; successive explorations take progressively less time as the actor finds the 'triggers.' Acts as a bridge from the conscious into the unconscious; when it works, the actor is 'taken over by the unconscious'—catching fire. Also useful for piquing personality elements beyond which the actor has already evolved (e.g., regaining organic access to youthful insecurities now shed). Use only when a simpler approach won't do—it is a demanding technique reserved for actors with mastery of Sense Memory.

sources.summary: Affective Memory (approach 12): re-creating entire experience through Sense Memory; all questions in present tense.
First exploration may take hours; successive ones faster as triggers are found; builds bridge to unconscious.
Also re-accesses personality elements the actor has evolved past; catches fire experience.
Affective Memory example: Medal of Honor ceremony paralleled by personal college graduation honor.

conglomerate_choice.name: Conglomerate Choice
conglomerate_choice.definition: A choice that combines qualities from several different people or experiences to create a single composite stimulus that produces the desired emotional response that no single person or experience would provide alone.
conglomerate_choice.details: Used when no single person in the actor's life exactly matches the obligation's requirements. The actor combines two or more people who, together, make them feel the way the character feels. The conglomerate is then worked for using a choice approach (Sense Memory, Endowments, Imaginary Monologue, etc.).

sources.summary: Conglomerate choice: when no single person matches all obligation elements, combine several people who together produce the needed feeling.

chronology_of_obligations.name: Chronology of Obligations
chronology_of_obligations.definition: The order in which the actor addresses the various obligations of a piece of material. Determined by which obligation is most impelling and most foundational for the character's behavior in a given scene.
chronology_of_obligations.details: The most important obligation takes precedence and must be addressed first. Example: a character rendered speechless by the power of a holy relationship—the relationship obligation must be addressed before any others. When no clear starting obligation, Time-and-Place is a good default starting point as it builds an environment that takes the actor off the stage. The chronology should become deductive from reading the material.

sources.summary: Chronology of obligations: order in which actor approaches obligations; most important determines where to start.
Example: blacksmith meeting Jesus—relationship obligation must come first before emotional can be addressed.
When chronology is unclear: time-and-place is a good default start; creates environment that takes actor off the stage.

public_privacy.name: Public Privacy
public_privacy.definition: The phenomenon experienced by an audience when watching an actor who has created a genuine environment for themselves—the actor seems truly to be in another place, engaged in a private life, observed but unaware of observation. This is different from theatrical presentation.
public_privacy.details: Comes from an actor who has created a time-and-place so real that their moment-to-moment life is fed by that environment. The audience experiences the feeling of watching someone truly 'in private' while being in public—a paradox that creates authentic theatrical reality.

sources.summary: Public privacy: phenomenon that comes from an actor who has created an environment; audience feels they are watching someone truly in private life.

how_to_identify_obligations.name: How to Identify Obligations in Material
how_to_identify_obligations.definition: A systematic method for identifying the obligations in a piece of material through four primary sources: what the author says, what other characters say about your character, what the character says about himself, and the actions and interaction of the characters.
how_to_identify_obligations.details: 1. What the author says about the play and the characters—descriptions, stage directions, short biographies. 2. What the other characters in the play say about your character—dialogue references to the character's behavior reveal obligations. 3. What the character says about himself—monologues, self-references, opinions expressed. 4. The actions and interaction of the characters—physical behavior descriptions reveal emotional life and character elements.

sources.summary: What the author says: character descriptions, biographies, environment descriptions, thematic statements, writer's self-identification with characters.
What other characters say: dialogue about your character reveals obligations directly. Examples of defensive character identified through others' lines.
Actions and interaction of characters: physical behavior descriptions in stage directions reveal emotional life and character qualities.

available_stimulus.name: Available Stimulus
available_stimulus.definition: A choice approach that uses whatever actually exists in the environment—the other person, the place itself, sounds, odors, objects, energy—as the stimulus for emotional response. The first consideration when approaching scene work because it is the most immediately accessible.
available_stimulus.details: What it means: relating to anything that actually exists in the environment and is really there. Should be the first consideration when preparing to work—if it exists, it is the most available choice approach. Can be almost anything: other people, the place itself, sounds, odors, energy, objects, photographs, how someone is dressed. Mechanics: if you can see it, look at it and relate; same for hearing, smelling, tasting, feeling. Often only a part of an object or element affects you—use Selective Emphasis to focus on that part. Inner Monologues can be run in relation to Available Stimulus to intensify the response.

sources.summary: Available Stimulus: relating to anything actually existing in environment; should be first consideration when preparing to work.
Mechanics: if you can see it, relate to it; same for all senses. Selective Emphasis and Isolation used when only part of an object affects you.
Inner Monologue in relation to Available Stimulus: talk to yourself about how you feel about what you see/hear from the other actor.

selective_emphasis.name: Selective Emphasis
selective_emphasis.definition: The technique of focusing attention on a specific part of an object, person, or stimulus that affects you, rather than the whole, in order to intensify the emotional impact. Also: creatively manipulating the way you perceive something to make it serve the scene's needs.
selective_emphasis.details: Often only part of an object or person affects you; the whole may have a different impact. Used in conjunction with Available Stimulus and many other choice approaches. Creative Manipulation: adjusting how you interpret or relate to an element so it creates the desired response. Example: 'That twinkle in her eyes: every time she looks at me she lights up for me—it's all for me!' Selective Isolation: close focus on a specific element while letting everything else recede. Can be used to intensify any sensory or relational element.

sources.summary: Selective Emphasis: focus on the part of an object that affects you; example of rocking chair reminding actor of grandfather.
Creative Manipulation: adjust/focus element so it affects you in the way you want; example: 'That twinkle is for me!'

creative_manipulation.name: Creative Manipulation
creative_manipulation.definition: The process of intelligently adjusting how one relates to or interprets an available stimulus or choice so that it creates the desired emotional effect. Focusing and directing the element's impact rather than taking it as-is.
creative_manipulation.details: Used when an element in the environment is close to what is needed but needs adjustment to fully serve the scene. The adjustment makes the element affect you in the manner you want. Example: The actress has a slight twinkle in her eyes. Creative Manipulation: 'That twinkle is for me—every time she looks at me, she lights up specifically for me.' Must not distort truth so severely that the adjustment is unbelievable—should build on what is actually there.

sources.summary: Creative Manipulation: focus element so it affects you in the manner you want; example of adjusting a twinkle to make it directed specifically at you.

additions.detail: Creative Manipulation (approach 20) as distinct from leadership: leadership involves itself with the resulting behavior; Creative Manipulation confines itself to the choice and the choice approach—the impetus, not the response. Used with Believability (building the untrue elements), Imaginary Monologues (steering content toward most impacting areas), Evocative Words (selecting words with most impact). Example Imaginary Monologue using Creative Manipulation: focused entirely on hurts, omitting all loving elements, to fuel the emotional life of hurt and self-pity. Important distinction: Creative Manipulation is not leadership—it does not impose a result; it intelligently shapes the input.

sources.summary: Creative Manipulation (approach 20): directing oneself into the desired area; confines itself to the choice and approach, not the resulting behavior.
Difference from leadership: leadership involves resulting behavior; Creative Manipulation involves only the impetus.

sensory_suggestion.name: Sensory Suggestion
sensory_suggestion.definition: A choice approach that combines the suggestive narrative ('He looks angry, he's moving toward me') with sensory questions ('What do I see in his eyes?') to create an imaginary reality faster than pure Sense Memory alone would allow.
sensory_suggestion.details: Combines narrative suggestion with sensory questions. The suggestion sets up the reality; the sensory questions make it specific and real. Can be used for imaginary choices (people not present) OR to change the behavior of the actual actor you're working with. Much faster than pure Sense Memory—establishes a reality that would take ten times longer with Sense Memory alone. Used when time doesn't permit full Sense Memory exploration or when a choice needs to be quickly established.

sources.summary: Sensory Suggestion: combines narrative suggestion with sensory questions; establishes reality faster than pure Sense Memory.
Can be used to change behavior of actual other actor in a scene—create a different relationship reality with the person present.

evocative_words.name: Evocative Words
evocative_words.definition: A choice approach that uses clusters of words, phrases, and associative language from personal experiences or belief structures to evoke the desired emotional state. Works through verbal/linguistic association rather than sensory recreation.
evocative_words.details: Can relate to: experiences from the actor's life (describing the key sensory/emotional elements of the experience in shorthand phrases), or belief structures (things the actor feels very strongly about—beliefs, codes, morality, strong feelings about justice, fascism, love, etc.). Can be done out loud as a preparation, semi-audibly, or silently as an Inner Monologue during scene work. Particularly useful when the actor has many lines and moves quickly—where sensory work would interfere with the behavioral obligation of the scene. Can be used in conjunction with other choice approaches (e.g., Sense Memory for the place + Evocative Words as Inner Monologue during the dialogue). The right cluster of words and phrases must be found through experimentation.

sources.summary: Evocative Words: relates to personal experiences or belief structures; works well when fast-paced dialogue prevents sensory choice work.
Example: loneliness/depression evocative words from specific personal experience; can be done as inner monologue.
Can be used with belief structures (fascism, Holocaust) to stimulate strong emotional responses; versatile combination with other approaches.

believability.name: Believability
believability.definition: A choice approach that mixes truth and untruth in a structured way—starting with threads of real life and adding fictional elements—to create a fabric of total reality. Both actors work together to build a shared fictional reality that serves the scene.
believability.details: A mixture of some truth and a lot of untruth; starts with reality and adds elements to promote the relationship and emotional needs of the scene. Both actors identify obligations and conflict elements, then structure a Believability improvisation together. The deft mixing of truth and untruth makes them indistinguishable—the actors lose track of what is real and what isn't. Selective emphasis is applied to both the truth and the untruth to structure the circumstances. Particularly important as a collaborative tool when both actors need to create a shared relational reality. Also possible for a single actor to do it alone.

sources.summary: Believability: mixture of truth and untruth; both actors identify obligations and structure it together; can be done alone but works especially well collaboratively.
Extended improvisation example using Believability; starts with actual truth about real lives, adds fictional elements.
The untruth in the example listed; when deftly mixed, truth and untruth become indistinguishable. Selectively emphasize both.

additions.detail: Solo Believability: can be done entirely as a private Inner Monologue—the other actor need not know what approach is being used. Can be used as a preparation for getting into a BEING state for a scene, structured to serve any purpose. Trick Believability: one actor launches a real but structured improvisation without telling the other actor, manipulating the situation to stimulate the relationship and emotional life the scene requires. The other actor's authentic responses feed the first actor's reality. Risk: the other actor may not appreciate the manipulation; use common sense and know your collaborators. Believability can also be sustained through an Inner Monologue after the scene dialogue begins.

sources.summary: Believability can be done solo as preparation; can be done without other actor's knowledge.
Solo Believability example: actor uses Inner Monologue during scene to sustain love/attraction toward the actress.
Trick Believability: one actor starts a real improvisation without other knowing; their authentic response feeds the reality.
Caution about Trick Believability—use common sense; if you can get away with it, you may never need to reveal what you were doing.

selective_emphasis_standalone.name: Selective Emphasis (Standalone Choice Approach)
selective_emphasis_standalone.definition: A choice approach and craft technique that can be used in conjunction with almost all other choice approaches. The process of isolating a specific part of any whole—a place, person, object, or thought—and relating only to that isolated element, so that it affects the actor in the manner desired.
selective_emphasis_standalone.details: Can be applied to a real or imaginary object, or to a thought process. With Sense Memory: selectively emphasize the sensory questions that lead in the desired emotional direction. With Available Stimulus: isolate a specific mannerism or physical element of the other actor. With Evocative Words: choosing the loaded phrases that will impel the right response is itself a selectively emphasized use of the approach. With Believability: creatively leading oneself to subjects/events that will promote the desired relational life. With Imaginary Monologues: select the right person AND isolate the specific things to say to that person. With Inner Monologue: direct everything said to oneself with intelligence related to the obligations of the piece. With Affective Memory and Personal Realities: emphasize those elements of a past experience or current reality that will move in the needed direction. Classic example: Goya's execution painting—selectively emphasizing the small patch of sunlit tree and birds produces a totally different response than the whole painting. The mechanics of its application vary from one choice approach to another.

sources.summary: Selective Emphasis (approach 6): isolating a part of any whole; dependent on isolating part of something.
With Sense Memory: selectively emphasize questions that promote the goal.
Goya painting example: selectively emphasizing one corner (sunlit tree) produces different response than the whole.
Application across Available Stimulus, Evocative Words, Believability, Imaginary Monologues, Inner Monologues, Affective Memory.
Personal Realities: selectively emphasize positive reality from earlier in the day to stimulate needed joy for scene.

imaginary_monologues.name: Imaginary Monologues
imaginary_monologues.definition: A choice approach activated by talking to an imaginary person as if that person were present now. The actor selects someone from life who will make him feel the way the scene requires, and talks to that person about the things most likely to stimulate the needed emotional state.
imaginary_monologues.details: One of the most commonly used choice approaches because it is simple to do and unobtrusive. Everyone has volumes of unsaid things they'd like to have told people living and dead—this gives that material a purpose. Can be used as a preparation before the scene begins, or run as an Inner Monologue in between and under the lines of dialogue. Can be combined with Sense Memory: asking sensory questions about the imaginary person in between the words of the monologue dimensionalizes the reality and allows the imaginary person to 'respond.' Creative Manipulation and Selective Emphasis can be applied: pick the right person, talk about the right things, choose impacting words. Can address any of the seven major obligations—relationship, emotional, character, historic, time-and-place, thematic. Freedom: can talk to anyone living or dead, about anything, allowing the monologue to go in any direction.

sources.summary: Imaginary Monologues (approach 8): talking to an imaginary person as if present; pick someone who makes you feel the way scene requires.
Example monologue to father about feeling unappreciated; can be used as preparation or as inner monologue during scene.
Most commonly used approach; simple, unobtrusive; everyone has unsaid things to say.
Combined with Sense Memory: sensory questions interspersed with words; allows imaginary person to 'respond' and creates unpredictability.
Can address any of the seven obligations; freedom to talk to anyone living or dead about anything.

imaginary_dialogues.name: Imaginary Dialogues
imaginary_dialogues.definition: A choice approach in which two actors each do an Imaginary Monologue simultaneously out loud, relating what each says to the other, while each allows the other's words to come from their respective imaginary choices. The result fires each actor's emotional furnace through the dialogue.
imaginary_dialogues.details: Must be done out loud—if done as inner monologue it becomes two actors doing separate Imaginary Monologues, not a Dialogue.
