Value: while both actors speak to their respective imaginary people, they relate those words to each other; accepting what the other says as coming from one's own imaginary choice creates a genuine emotional exchange. Depends on making the right decisions about respective choices—identifying obligations of both characters, then each actor selecting an imaginary person whose parallel reality fits. The Imaginary Dialogue often parallels the written scene almost perfectly while also being 100% accurate to the actors' own personal realities. Actors may start using the character's name and end up using their choice's name as they become fully absorbed. The life stimulated by the Imaginary Dialogue can be carried directly into the scene; actors often do the entire scene from the realities created in the dialogue.

sources.summary: Imaginary Dialogues (approach 9): two actors doing Imaginary Monologues together out loud; each relates what they say to the other.
Odets' Waiting for Lefty example: detailed obligations for Joe and Edna; each actor's choice from personal life parallels the scene's conflict.
Extended dialogue example; often parallels written scene exactly while being 100% accurate to personal realities.
Actors start using character names and end using choice names; the stimulated life carries directly into the scene.

externals.name: Externals
externals.definition: A category of choice approaches (approach 10) that seeks to get a physical sense of an animal, another person, an inanimate object, or an insect through the actor's own instrument. When accomplished and translated into human behavior, the actor is affected on multiple levels—physically, emotionally, intellectually, and vocally.
externals.details: Four separate involvements: Animals, People, Inanimate Objects, Insects. Goal: experiencing a sense of any one of these externals through one's own body. An entire acting approach could be constructed from just this one area (Morris's statement). Keys to the unconscious: approaching inner life from the outside in. The way to pique unconscious responses seems to be to follow this path from the primitive parts of ourselves. Six purposes: (1) fulfill character elements, (2) stimulate emotional impulses/points of view/thought processes/behavior that other choices cannot elicit, (3) pique primal and primitive elements, (4) effect a total change in the physical instrument, (5) effect a change in how the actor relates to the world, (6) address as many of the seven major obligations as possible. Animals: Morris's personal story—spent a year at the gibbon cage at the LA Zoo; eventually used gorilla to transform his playing of Eben in Desire Under the Elms; the gorilla transformed city-boy sophistication into a hulking farm boy with basic animal impulses. People: approach technique similar to animals; same elements—overall rhythm, leading center, secondary center, limitations, mannerisms—but no need for translation from animal to human. A timid actor can become menacing; a brawler can become a cowering jelly. Externals are a powerful tool for prying open personality treasure chests.

sources.summary: Externals (approach 10): four involvements—animals, people, inanimate objects, insects; get physical sense through own instrument.
Richest and most complex choice approach category; entire acting approach could be built on it alone.
Morris's personal story: year at gibbon cage; eventually used gorilla for Eben in Desire Under the Elms.
Six purposes of Externals; keys to the unconscious: approaching inner life from the outside in.
People (Externals B): approach same as animals but no translation needed; observe rhythm, leading center, secondary center, limitations, mannerisms.

additions.detail: People (Externals B): approach technique essentially same as animals—overall rhythm, leading center, secondary center, limitations, centers of weight/balance/power, isolations, corresponding/contrasting rhythms, individual mannerisms—except no translation from animal to human needed. Three types of limitations for humans: self-imposed limitations (a mannered way of moving), personal-characteristic limitations (tight shoulders, rigid neck), and handicaps (a limp). Purpose can be exact parallel to character OR emotional parallel: the outward physical manifestations of a person mirror their inner life. Accomplishing external sense of that person stimulates their inner life inside the actor. Example: Dustin Hoffman using his mother for Tootsie. Inanimate Objects (Externals C): less scientific, more imagination-dependent. Select an object; assume its physical position with your body; sensorially create its weight, mass, and static rhythm (objects have static, not active, rhythm); allow the impulses stimulated by the process to animate into active human behavior. The question 'What must it feel like to be this piano?' combined with the approach process can produce startling emotional and physical results. Playwrights often describe characters using inanimate objects—these descriptions are literal instructions. Insects (Externals D): final and most experimental subcategory. First brought to class by student Bruce Mars using a praying mantis he had captured. Approach: same elements as animals—overall rhythm, tempo, leading center, limitations related to head/thorax/abdomen movements, corresponding and contrasting rhythms—but human limitations are greater (many legs, antennae, speed). A single authentic mannerism of an insect sometimes gives the entire feeling. Example obligation: ominous and stoic individual with lethal essence—Choice: black widow spider.

sources.summary: People (Externals B): same approach as animals; no translation needed; three types of human limitations.
Individual mannerisms for people: bottomless well; actor must include all mannerisms for authentic sense.
Reasons for using people: exact parallel, OR person whose inner life parallels the character.
Inanimate Objects (Externals C): assume physical position; weight/mass/static rhythm; animate impulses into human behavior.
Playwrights use inanimate objects to describe characters—'solid as a rock,' 'like a bulldozer'—literal instructions.
Insects (Externals D): Bruce Mars/praying mantis story; approach similar to animals; single mannerism sometimes sufficient.
Translation of insects: same as animals; retain rhythms, centers, tempo; progressively convert to human behavior.

externals_animals_technique.name: Externals — Animals (Approach Technique)
externals_animals_technique.definition: The specific step-by-step technique for observing an animal, absorbing its elements through one's own instrument, and translating that sense into human behavior for use in a scene or play.
externals_animals_technique.details: Eight-step approach checklist: (1) Overall rhythm—watch the animal, practice its movements, bring that home, repeat. (2) Leading center—the part of the animal that crosses an imaginary line first; identify and apply to own body. (3) Secondary center—the part that closely follows the leading center; put together with rhythm and leading center. (4) Limitations—what the animal cannot do; adapted to own body. (5) Centers of weight, balance, and power—the part that propels the animal; add to exploration. (6) Isolations—practice independent body part movement; break animal down into components and reassemble. (7) Corresponding and contrasting rhythms—parts moving in harmony vs. counterrhythm; practice separately and together. (8) Species mannerisms and individual mannerisms—species-wide behaviors first, then individual peculiarities. The tempo (speed of rhythm) is added after the rhythm is established. Translation to human behavior: slowly move from all-fours to crouching to standing while retaining all elements; make the animal behavior more subtle by degrees until only the inner sense remains. Sounds of an animal: explore where sounds come from in the body; what precipitates them; pitch, depth, duration; can be directly translated into character voice.

sources.summary: Approach technique checklist: overall rhythm, leading center, secondary center, limitations, weight centers, isolations, corresponding/contrasting rhythms, mannerisms.
Practice rhythm at home; leading center; secondary center; put together progressively.
Center of weight/balance/power; isolations for independent body movement and breaking animal into components.
Tempo added after rhythm established; system: observe, assume position, take home, return, systematically build.
Eight-element checklist; species mannerisms more important initially; individual mannerisms refine the specific animal.
Translation to human behavior: all-fours to crouching to standing; retain elements, make progressively more subtle.
Animal sounds: explore source in body, movements accompanying sound, pitch/depth/duration; translate into character voice.

objects_contact_body.name: Objects that Come into Contact with the Body
objects_contact_body.definition: A choice approach that uses clothing, accessories, weapons, or other objects worn or held by the actor to stimulate emotional and behavioral responses. The object creates real physical and psychological changes when worn or held; if the object is not available on stage, it is sensorially created through Sense Memory.
objects_contact_body.details: We all know that what we wear has a definite effect on how we feel and how we relate to the world. Strong emotions can be stimulated by creating a sense of an object attached to or held by the body. When the real object exists and can be worn in the scene: craft technique becomes Available Stimulus. When the real object is not available: approach is Sense Memory—systematically ask sensory questions about the object's presence on the body (weight, pressure, texture, temperature, sound when moving, resistance in fingers). Examples: Western boots (swagger, ego-center, sexuality), a gun in a fast-draw holster (command, superiority, conviction), a tuxedo (formality), high heels (changed gait and attitude), a monocle, a cape. Detailed sensory process: where in the waist do I feel the belt?—the sides, the back? As I breathe, where do I feel resistance? Where is the weight on the right side? What color is the belt from this angle? etc.

sources.summary: Objects that Contact Body (approach 11): clothing and objects worn/held stimulate emotional and behavioral changes.
Boots example: entire rhythm, ego, sexuality changed; can sensorially create the object if not available on stage.
Gun/holster example: commands room, feels superior; audience sees suit, actor has sensorially created gun.
Approach technique: detailed sensory questions about weight, texture, temperature, sound, resistance of object on body.

inner_monologue_choice_approach.name: Inner Monologues (Choice Approach and Craft Technique)
inner_monologue_choice_approach.definition: A choice approach and craft technique (approach 13) that involves talking to oneself internally—creating a structured verbal flow with a beginning, middle, and end—about whatever will promote the emotional life required. When used in conjunction with another choice approach, it becomes a craft technique; when used alone, it is a choice approach.
inner_monologue_choice_approach.details: Three main forms: (1) Moment-to-Moment Inner Monologue: staying in the reality of the moment by including between the words of the play what is going on inside. Antidote for leadership and result-consciousness. Allows impulses to express through dialogue. (2) Character Inner Monologue: structuring an internal monologue that promotes the thinking of the character, using the actor's own personal parallels—not trying to think the character's actual thoughts, but finding personal parallels to the character's thought process. 'Fuel' the scene with the emotional life from the actor's own real belief structures and points of view. (3) Inner Imaginary Monologue: doing an Imaginary Monologue internally while speaking the lines of the scene, so that the words of the play are addressed through the inner monologue to an imaginary person. Can be used with: Evocative Words, Believability, Prior Knowledge, Available Stimulus, Personal Realities, Selective Emphasis, Sub-Personalities, Sensory Speculation, Creative Imagery. When used out loud in rehearsal, it becomes a good exploration exercise—going back and forth between the lines and the inner monologue aloud. The actor must discipline himself to structure the inner monologue; otherwise it becomes just thinking and the focus wanders.

sources.summary: Inner Monologues (approach 13): talking to oneself about whatever promotes emotional life; craft technique when combined with other approaches.
Moment-to-Moment Inner Monologue: include between words of play what is going on inside; antidote for leadership.
Character Inner Monologue: parallel to character's thought process using actor's own realities; find personal parallel, not character's literal thoughts.
Inner Imaginary Monologue: doing Imaginary Monologue internally while speaking the lines.
Inner Monologue can be used with: Evocative Words, Believability, Personal Realities, Selective Emphasis, Sensory Speculation, Creative Imagery, Sub-Personalities.

substituting_other_words.name: Substituting Other Words for the Lines
substituting_other_words.definition: A simple choice approach (approach 14) in which the actor substitutes his own personally meaningful and impacting words for the words of the scene, said as an Inner Monologue under the spoken text. Used when the written lines are archaic, ornate, or otherwise emotionally inert for the actor.
substituting_other_words.details: The actor selects words that come from a personal, real place and that are more emotionally impacting than the written dialogue. These substituted words are run as an Inner Monologue under and in between the actual spoken lines. Classic example: Christopher Fry's archaic language in 'The Lady's Not for Burning'—replaced with simple direct personal language. Content can come from what the actor really feels about the other actor, or it can relate to another person entirely. Should come from a meaningful place and be about something that actually affects the actor.

sources.summary: Substituting Other Words (approach 14): actor's own words as Inner Monologue under the written lines; used when lines are emotionally inert.
Example: Christopher Fry's archaic language replaced with simple personal language in inner monologue.

essences_and_abstracts.name: Essences and Abstracts
essences_and_abstracts.definition: A highly imaginative choice approach (approach 15) that involves getting the physical sense of an object—animate or inanimate—through the body, then translating that sense and its emotional residue into human behavior. Like working for animals or inanimate objects, but more abstract: the actor gets 'the sense of' a hood ornament, a champagne bottle, scrambling eggs, a metronome, popping bacon.
essences_and_abstracts.details: Any animate or inanimate object can be used. The key question: not what the object feels like to touch, but what it would feel like to BE the object. Approach: assume the physical position or attitude of the object; ask sensory questions about its mass, weight, position, texture; translate the sensory responses to your own body. Highly dependent on imagination and the ability to translate imagination into a physical state. The essence retained after translation produces thoughts, feelings, attitudes, and emotional life. Example: actress playing an alcoholic dowager used 'freshly opened bottle of bubbling champagne' as her Essences-and-Abstracts choice—fulfilled a dozen character elements with a single choice. Can address character, relationship, and emotional obligation areas.

sources.summary: Essences and Abstracts (approach 15): get physical sense of an abstract object through imagination and Sense Memory; translate into human behavior.
Example objects: hood ornament, champagne bottle, metronome, scrambling eggs, bacon, bubble gum.
Actress playing alcoholic dowager used champagne bottle—fulfilled dozen character elements with single choice.

illnesses_and_restrictions.name: Illnesses and Restrictions
illnesses_and_restrictions.definition: A choice approach (approach 16) that involves creating a physical sense of illness, pain, or restriction through Sense Memory, so that the resulting bodily experience organically produces the behavior, irritability, or emotional state required by the scene.
illnesses_and_restrictions.details: Encompasses headaches, toothaches, backaches, stiffness, dizziness, faintness, body braces, leg casts, body casts, being confined, being tied up or restricted in any way. The audience doesn't know what the actor is working for—they only experience the resulting behavior. Many actors ignore the necessity for creating real pain, using only communication/indication; but an actor in real pain behaves quite differently—even the voice carries the presence of pain. The base reality (e.g., real pain) is responsible for all the behavior that results. Example of headache creation (Sense Memory process): What do I feel in my forehead?...How high above my eyebrows?...Where do I feel the most?... etc. Illnesses and Restrictions can also create panic and terror: sensorially filling a vessel with water rising to the chin, sensorially being bound and gagged.

sources.summary: Illnesses and Restrictions (approach 16): Sense Memory used to create physical maladies and restrictions that produce character/emotional behavior.
Many actors don't create real pain; but a person in real pain behaves differently—voice carries pain.
Headache creation example: detailed sensory questions about forehead, sides, back, movement, sound sensitivity.
Example: growing panic using sensation of being in a vessel filling with water reaching the chin.

prior_knowledge.name: Prior Knowledge
prior_knowledge.definition: A choice approach (approach 17) that uses what the actor already knows about the people and places in the play—their lives, relationships, background, history—as a stimulus. It is a subset of Available Stimulus but has its own specific elements. Prior Knowledge includes anything the actor has read, heard, or witnessed about the other actor or the place.
prior_knowledge.details: Activated either by directly relating to the object or person (when knowledge functions automatically), or by structuring the knowledge into a focused Inner Monologue. Often the knowledge is surrounded by other personality elements that obscure the relevant nucleus; the actor must selectively emphasize those elements and weave them into an Inner Monologue. Acting companies with long histories together are rich in Prior Knowledge and Personal Realities. Example: knowing that the other actor hurts people, cheats, borrows and never returns money, lies, pits people against each other—this Prior Knowledge can fuel a feeling of revulsion during a scene where that is the obligation. No one need know that the actor is using this; it is for the actor's use alone. The actor doesn't even have to know how he knows what he knows—just that he knows is enough.

sources.summary: Prior Knowledge (approach 17): what actor already knows about people and places in the play; subset of Available Stimulus.
Inner Monologue example using Prior Knowledge to fuel revulsion; activated under and between lines.
Can function automatically through relating to person/place, or must be excavated by selective emphasis and Inner Monologue.

sensory_speculation.name: Sensory Speculation
sensory_speculation.definition: A choice approach (approach 18) that uses the Sense Memory process to speculatively explore an object or person the actor has never come into physical contact with—imagining what it would feel like to touch, smell, taste, or hear that object or person—in order to stimulate the required emotional response.
sensory_speculation.details: Valuable for: objects across the stage that can't be physically touched; people not yet known intimately; objects for which there is no physical access. All sensory responses are speculative—the actor has not actually experienced this object or person before. The approach goes far beyond a 'wonderment approach': it actively excites and piques sensory responses which lead to feelings and needs, impelling the actor to action. Example: actor attracted to woman across room speculatively explores what it would feel like to touch her face, smell her hair, bring his lips near hers. Can create terror: speculating about a sword plunging into the stomach, stepping out of an airplane at altitude, the physical sensation of strangling someone. Sensory Endowments are also speculative when the actor has never seen the other actor express the particular behavior he wishes to endow.

sources.summary: Sensory Speculation (approach 18): speculatively exploring object/person never physically experienced through sensory questions.
Goes beyond wonderment: actively excites sensory apparatus, stimulates feelings and physical response.
Can create terror: speculating about sword plunge, stepping from airplane, strangling.

personal_realities.name: Personal Realities
personal_realities.definition: A choice approach (approach 19) that uses what is actually happening in the actor's own life at this moment—a feeling, a prejudice, something that occurred earlier in the day, an offstage relationship dynamic with another actor—as the impetus for the scene. A subset of Available Stimulus.
personal_realities.details: Can be almost anything real in the actor's current life: a feeling he has at this moment, a prejudice, a love affair, an ongoing conflict with another actor that began in the first week of rehearsals. Can be something that happened at lunch today that makes the actor feel just the way the character does. Can be activated at several levels of intensity: (1) just accepting and jumping in; (2) reminding oneself out loud; (3) doing a focused Inner Monologue; (4) sensorially re-creating an experience. Anything that happens on stage during performance and promotes the play qualifies as Personal Realities. An acting company that has been together for a long time is very rich in Prior Knowledge and Personal Realities. Example: actor doing Lenny who loves telling jokes—his Personal Reality fuels the comedic scene.

sources.summary: Personal Realities (approach 19): using what is real in actor's life right now as impetus for scene; subset of Available Stimulus.
Lenny example: actor loves telling jokes—Personal Reality fuels scene; can be activated at various levels of complexity.
Can be offstage love affair, ongoing conflict, something from lunch; anything on stage that promotes the play.

sub_personalities.name: Sub-Personalities
sub_personalities.definition: A choice approach (approach 21) derived from Jungian psychology and Dr. Hal Stone's Voice Dialogue technique. The concept holds that each person is made up of many sub-persons or sub-personalities, each with their own impulses, points of view, and behavioral patterns. The actor identifies which sub-personality the character runs from and elicits the matching sub-personality within himself.
sub_personalities.details: Origin: Carl Jung's theory of multiple personality facets; further developed by Dr. Hal Stone and Sidra Stone's Voice Dialogue technique; Morris adapted it for acting. Major sub-personality archetypes: the critic/pusher/perfectionist (triumvirate), the good father/bad father, the nurturing mother/critical mother, the wise person, Aphrodite (feminine sexual energy), Don Juan (masculine sexual energy), the pleaser, the child archetypes (vulnerable, frightened, magical, mischievous, rebellious, lonely child), the obedient son/daughter, the judge, the victim, the free spirit, the beach bum, the depressed sub-person, the Martin Luther (moralist), the Apollonian (logical, intellectual), the Dionysian (hedonistic), the demonic/satanic (killer, anger, power, ruthless). If the actor can identify the sub-personality a character runs from and elicit that same sub-personality in himself, the entire scene or play can be done with a single choice and a single choice approach. Key advantage: the sub-personality has its own unique intelligence for the material—it understands and relates to the content in ways that other parts of the actor do not. Morris warns against Voice Dialogue in acting contexts (risk of assuming behavior rather than organic experience). His technique: other approach techniques for reaching and ventilating a specific sub-personality. When in the driver's seat, a sub-personality influences the entire physical, emotional, and behavioral expression of the person—ideal for multi-obligation scene work.

sources.summary: Sub-Personalities (approach 21): from Jungian psychology; multiple personality facets; actor calls forth matching sub-personality.
Adapted from Dr. Hal Stone's Voice Dialogue; modification process for acting use.
Archetypes include: the child archetypes, the critic, Aphrodite, the satanic part, the pusher, the judge, the protector, the killer, the warrior.
Logic: if characters are people with sub-personalities, identifying the one in control enables actor to use single choice for entire play.
The victim sub-personality; the free spirit; the various child archetypes.
Apollonian vs. Dionysian; Aphrodite; demonic/satanic archetypal category; examples from theatrical characters.

additions.detail: Disowning sub-personalities: as we grow up we suppress, deny, or disown certain parts through social pressure, parental disapproval, peer conformity. The antidote: become aware of the denial, then 'talk' to the disowned part and encourage it to express itself. Repeat until it gains confidence and comes forth more easily. Three approach techniques for reaching and piquing sub-personalities: (1) Voice Dialogue—a two-person facilitation process: the facilitator asks to speak to a particular part; the person moves over a foot or two to accommodate the sub-personality and speaks from that place. Facilitator must be non-judgmental. Always return person to ego state after; never leave them in a sub-personality. (2) Personal Voice Dialogue—actor talks to the desired sub-personality alone and encourages it to respond and speak. Move physically to accommodate the sub when it responds. (3) Working to create stimuli, circumstances, and conditions that pique the sub-personality—knowing what specifically calls forth that part and recreating those conditions (e.g., a specific piece of music, a place, an object, a kind of person, a moral situation). Bonus: when in a sub-personality, the actor's choices for the rest of the play are made by that qualified part of himself—it knows what affects it, and makes appropriate scene choices automatically. Even if the actor decides not to use the sub-personality in final performance, using it to discover choices during the process is a real advantage.

sources.summary: Sub-personality archetypes list: guru/messiah, controller/master controller, hero, spiritual/mystical, satanic/demonic.
Disowning sub-personalities: suppression through social pressure creates unavailable parts; antidote is conscious re-contact.
Using sub-personalities as choice approach: encourage the specific sub to take over; think, feel, behave as that part does.
Three approach techniques: Voice Dialogue, Personal Voice Dialogue, creating stimuli that pique the sub.
Voice Dialogue example: facilitator speaking to the 'angry part'; the sub reveals itself, its frustrations, its conflict with the 'pleaser.'
Facilitator rules: non-judgmental; always return person to objective state; never leave them in a sub-personality.
Personal Voice Dialogue: actor calls on sub-personality alone; 'pusher' example—responds immediately.
Creating stimuli: know what conditions call forth each sub; reproduce those conditions. Bonus: sub makes qualified scene choices automatically.

creative_visualization.name: Creative Visualization
creative_visualization.definition: The twenty-second and final choice approach listed, also called Creative Imagery. A highly imaginative process: the actor closes the eyes and guides himself into an environment—real or imagined—supporting the visualization with all five senses. As the unconscious activates, thoughts and impulses emerge and carry over into the scene.
creative_visualization.details: Done by closing the eyes and visualizing a place, guiding oneself through it like moving through a dream. Can be a place visited before or entirely imagined. Support all elements—colors, objects, sounds, smells—with full sensory engagement. Avoid staying in the head: use the sensory apparatus to make the relationship to the place unconscious. Once thoughts and impulses emerge, open eyes and bring the carryover into the scene. Even after opening the eyes, the creative visualization continues to pique unconscious connections. Unlike Affective Memory: Creative Visualization is a different, more imaginative process—less meticulous in Sense Memory terms. Later, with practice: can be done with eyes open through the 'inner eye.' Serves as foundation for time-and-place obligation; can also stimulate emotional life. Can transport to any place—the Himalayas, Auschwitz, Shangri-La.

sources.summary: Creative Visualization (approach 22): close eyes, guide into environment, support with five senses; unconscious produces thoughts/impulses.
Carryover: even after opening eyes, creative visualization piques impulses.
Not Affective Memory—entirely different process; excellent for time-and-place foundation.

circle_of_the_work.name: The Circle of the Work
circle_of_the_work.definition: Morris's integrated model of acting in which instrument work (BEING, irreverence, preparation) and craft work (obligations, choices, choice approaches) form a complete circle—two sides of a single unified practice—joining at the goal of Ultimate Consciousness.
circle_of_the_work.details: The circle diagram: the lower portion represents pragmatic involvements. Left side: the instrument—exercises and techniques that liberate it (all the instrumental preparations, BEING work, irreverence). Right side: the craft—application of techniques that stimulate life on stage (obligations, choices, choice approaches). The pie-shaped wedge at the top: the philosophy and resulting goal—BEING + Irreverence → Ultimate Consciousness. Balance: the actor must balance time between instrumental emphasis and craft learning. Early training: more time on instrument. As emotional availability grows: equal time on both sides. When the work is integrated into life it becomes 'a way of living as well as a way of work.' The join: at the point where the actor's involvement is so complete that there is no separation between actor and approach—the craft produces reality, which triggers the unconscious, which produces the Ultimate Consciousness. Ultimate Consciousness: complete connection with the unconscious flow of life—the highest state of creativity, the ultimate goal of the performing artist. When reached, the actor does nothing but get out of its way and go where it leads.

sources.summary: Chapter 5 — The Circle of the Work: instrument and craft form a complete circle; balance is the goal.
Left side: instrument; right side: craft; top wedge: BEING + Irreverence → Ultimate Consciousness.
When integrated into life: a way of living as well as working. The join: no separation between actor and approach.

selfishness_as_virtue.name: Selfishness as Virtue (The Irreverent Actor)
selfishness_as_virtue.definition: Morris's argument that the actor must be a 'selfish animal' who works for his own authentic reality—demanding the right to do the work his way, refusing to succumb to the concepts of others—because this irreverent self-focus is the only path to genuine reality on the stage, which ultimately benefits both actor and audience.
selfishness_as_virtue.details: The irreverent actor is selfish enough to demand the right to do the work, to work the way he wants, without succumbing to the concepts of others. He uses rehearsal for what it was designed for and makes his own statement in every character he plays. Historical context: innovators (Reich, Freud, Stanislavsky) faced enormous resistance; the conventional norm suppresses the individual. Imitating success stifles the very part of the actor that might have had impact. The metamorphosis from rebellious to unique is recognition—all it takes is for one successful person to hear your statement. The actor must ask every day: Who am I? What do I feel? How do I see that? What do I want to say? He must have the courage to tell his truth every time. Irreverence is the road to personal and individual statement.

sources.summary: The Virtue and Necessity of Selfishness: actor must be selfish animal who works for own benefit and demands right to work his way.
The Maverick/Rebel/Irreverent: historical innovators faced resistance; imitating success stifles individual impact.
Ask every day: Who am I? What do I feel? Courage to tell truth. 'Irreverence is the road to personal and individual statement.'

emotional_point_of_view_inventory.name: Emotional-Point-of-View Inventory
emotional_point_of_view_inventory.description: A self-questioning practice done out loud where the actor asks how they feel about every object, person, sound, and sensation in their environment, then expresses those responses verbally and physically.
emotional_point_of_view_inventory.instrumental_use: Builds awareness of real impulses, trains moment-to-moment responsiveness, and develops the habit of authentic emotional expression. Helps identify obstacles to BEING.
emotional_point_of_view_inventory.craft_use: Can be used as a pre-scene BEING preparation to establish an organic baseline before approaching material.
emotional_point_of_view_inventory.when_to_use: Daily practice; also as warm-up before rehearsal or performance. Whenever connection to authentic emotional flow is needed.

sources.summary: Described as gathering awareness of how objects affect you, building ability to respond and express all impulses.
Extended example of the inventory in action, moving through room, sounds, people, feelings.

emotional_point_of_view_nonverbal.name: Emotional Point of View, Without Words (Nonverbal)
emotional_point_of_view_nonverbal.description: A variation of the Emotional-Point-of-View exercise where all responses to the environment are expressed as unintelligible sounds rather than words, preventing intellectualization.
emotional_point_of_view_nonverbal.instrumental_use: Establishes connection with real organic impulses by bypassing verbal/intellectual processing. Produces greater vocal variety than word-based expression. Promotes BEING state.
emotional_point_of_view_nonverbal.craft_use: Foundation practice for achieving authentic expression; feeds into the craft process.
emotional_point_of_view_nonverbal.when_to_use: Daily practice; especially useful when an actor tends to describe feelings rather than express them.

sources.summary: Nonverbal version: translate impulses into unintelligible sounds while scanning environment. Avoids intellectualization; words often describe rather than express feelings.

inclusion_technique.name: Inclusion
inclusion_technique.description: The practice of allowing all impulses—whether relevant to the scene or not—to express themselves through the actor's behavior and lines, rather than suppressing them.
inclusion_technique.instrumental_use: Trains the actor to trust all impulses and maintain an unbroken flow of organic life. Eliminates the short-circuiting caused by conditioned taboos.
inclusion_technique.craft_use: Used during scene work to maintain reality even when distracting, irrelevant, or conflicting impulses arise. Prevents the split between internal reality and performed behavior.
inclusion_technique.when_to_use: During rehearsals, scene work, and performance whenever the actor notices impulses that seem inconsistent with the material.

sources.summary: Inclusion technique: using monologue to practice allowing all impulses to express through the words.
How to practice: recite known monologue, allow everything felt to express through the words regardless of its consistency with the material.
Including short-circuit feelings at taboo moments restores authentic impulse flow.

inner_outer_monologue.name: Inner-Outer Monologue
inner_outer_monologue.description: The actor alternates between spoken lines of a memorized monologue and verbalized inner stream-of-consciousness, going back and forth. All content—written and personal—is spoken aloud.
inner_outer_monologue.instrumental_use: Connects the flow of real impulses with the material. Builds the habit of maintaining inner life while speaking scripted text.
inner_outer_monologue.craft_use: Used in rehearsal and practice to bridge the gap between personal organic life and the playwright's words. Good rehearsal tool when the scene feels disconnected.
inner_outer_monologue.when_to_use: Practice with well-memorized monologue; also in rehearsal when inner life disconnects from the material.

sources.summary: Description of Inner-Outer Monologue: alternating written lines and personal inner monologue spoken aloud.
Extended example with Gettysburg Address. Key: carry over the inner life into the written monologue. Good rehearsal tool.

being_workout.name: BEING Workout
being_workout.description: Two actors face each other and relate moment-to-moment, conversationally expressing all obstacles (fears, distractions, tensions, concerns) as they arise. Done before rehearsing a scene together.
being_workout.instrumental_use: Achieves a threshold of real life and authentic impulse flow between two actors. When fears and concerns are exposed openly, the need to hide them is eliminated. Creates permission to be distracted or tense as part of the reality.
being_workout.craft_use: Establishes the organic baseline needed for authentic scene work with a partner. The choices explored from that place stimulate true emotional responses.
being_workout.when_to_use: At the start of any rehearsal with another actor. Especially important when both actors feel disconnected or perform artificially.

sources.summary: BEING Workout: two actors face each other, express all obstacles moment-to-moment; including obstacles creates unbroken reality flow.
By starting rehearsal this way, actors achieve threshold of real life from which authentic choices can be explored.

intimate_sharing.name: Intimate Sharing with Another Actor
intimate_sharing.description: Two actors share deeply personal material with each other to address fear of exposure and break through the barriers that prevent authentic vulnerability on stage.
intimate_sharing.instrumental_use: Directly addresses fear of exposure, which is a major block to BEING. Creates a safe container for practicing genuine vulnerability and openness with a scene partner.
intimate_sharing.craft_use: Prepares actors for scenes requiring emotional vulnerability and genuine intimacy.
intimate_sharing.when_to_use: When fear of exposure is a significant block; as part of building trust with a scene partner.

sources.summary: Intimate Sharing addresses fear of exposure—a major block to BEING freedom. Never obligation to expose against will, but exercise provides safe practice.

ego_preparations.name: Ego Preparations
ego_preparations.description: A variety of exercises designed to establish in the actor a state of self-permission and confidence—the sense that they have the right to BE who they are and take the risks that produce exciting work on stage.
ego_preparations.instrumental_use: Builds the philosophical foundation of irreverence. Gives the actor permission to explore and experiment without fear of judgment. Promotes self-confidence and the courage to risk.
ego_preparations.craft_use: Used before rehearsal or performance to establish an irreverent state that allows full exploration of choices.
ego_preparations.when_to_use: Before rehearsals or scenes; whenever the actor feels inhibited, self-conscious, or dominated by others' expectations.

sources.summary: Ego preparations promote irreverent state by establishing the actor's right to BE who they are and take risks.

making_my_statement.name: Making My Statement
making_my_statement.description: An ego preparation where the actor largely expresses their personal point of view about everything they see, hear, and feel, including personal statements about life, values, and how they want to work.
making_my_statement.instrumental_use: Establishes a strong personal center; promotes the philosophical acceptance of irreverence. Roots the actor in their own perspective before approaching material.
making_my_statement.craft_use: Pre-rehearsal preparation that liberates the actor to bring their individual voice to the role.
making_my_statement.when_to_use: Before rehearsal, especially when feeling intimidated, subordinated to concept, or lacking self-permission.

sources.summary: Making My Statement: express personal point of view about all things seen, heard, felt around you.

due_taking_preparation.name: Due-Taking Preparation
due_taking_preparation.description: Acknowledging one's right to work creatively—done silently or audibly before or during rehearsal. The actor affirms their right to take time, explore, fail, and demand a supportive environment.
due_taking_preparation.instrumental_use: Establishes the actor's sense of entitlement to the creative process. Can be done 'on the record' so others hear it, which increases permission to risk.
due_taking_preparation.craft_use: Prepares the actor to explore choices freely and adventurously without social anxiety about the process.
due_taking_preparation.when_to_use: Before rehearsals; whenever the actor feels pressured, rushed, or denied the creative space needed.

sources.summary: Due-Taking: acknowledge right to work creatively; can be done silently or audibly; going 'on record' frees actor to risk more.

feeling_special.name: Feeling Special
feeling_special.description: An ego preparation where the actor focuses on their own uniqueness—that no one else in the world is exactly like them—as the foundation for irreverent self-expression.
feeling_special.instrumental_use: Achieves a sense of specialness that allows courageous expression of all impulses and opens doors to individual emotional facets. Empowers the actor to dimensionalize the character beyond the author's concept.
feeling_special.craft_use: Helps the actor bring their unique personal qualities to a role rather than playing a generic version of the character.
feeling_special.when_to_use: Before rehearsal or performance; when the actor feels they must conform to a standard or generic interpretation.

sources.summary: Feeling Special: focusing on uniqueness—no one exactly like you—allows courageous expression and brings unique individuality to the role.

sounds_with_a_choice.name: Sounds with a Choice
sounds_with_a_choice.description: A rehearsal irreverence technique where actors replace spoken dialogue with pure sounds while working for their scene choices. They relate to each other and respond to impulses entirely through non-verbal sound.
sounds_with_a_choice.instrumental_use: Eliminates concept and premeditation. Allows the unexpected and fills scenes with emotional colors. Forces real moment-to-moment responsiveness.
sounds_with_a_choice.craft_use: Used in rehearsal to explore emotional content of a scene without being tied to the meaning or logic of the written words. Helps actors discover impulses they wouldn't find through the text.
sounds_with_a_choice.when_to_use: In rehearsal when concept is taking over; when actors feel locked into line readings or predetermined emotional responses.

sources.summary: Sounds with a Choice: actors replace dialogue with sounds while working for choices; eliminates concept and premeditation.

gibberish_with_choice.name: Gibberish with a Choice
gibberish_with_choice.description: Two actors relate using gibberish words (abstract, meaningless syllables) instead of written dialogue while working for their scene choices and encouraging moment-to-moment reality.
gibberish_with_choice.instrumental_use: Eliminates concept and premeditation, like sounds, but is more like talking. Allows abstract word-formation without semantic meaning.
gibberish_with_choice.craft_use: Good intermediate step between pure sounds and returning to written dialogue. Preserves sentence structure without content, keeping emotional reality active.
gibberish_with_choice.when_to_use: In rehearsal as a step between sounds and scripted text; when actors need to free themselves from the conceptual burden of the written words.

sources.summary: Gibberish with Choice: like sounds but more talk-like; eliminates concept while preserving sentence-structure feeling. Good interim technique.

paraphrasing_improvisation.name: Moment-to-Moment Paraphrasing Improvisation
paraphrasing_improvisation.description: Actors paraphrase the scene—not verbatim but in their own words—paralleling the content and achieving the essence of the material while expressing their own personal feelings and impulses.
paraphrasing_improvisation.instrumental_use: Frees actors to allow, permit, and include all impulses without being tied to the logic of the words. Prevents premeditation and head-trapping.
paraphrasing_improvisation.craft_use: Explores the emotional and relational content of the scene while building authentic impulse-based behavior. When actors return to the written text, they carry the irreverent impulse quality with them.
paraphrasing_improvisation.when_to_use: In rehearsal when actors are trapped in their heads by the written lines; when emotional life isn't connecting to the text.

sources.summary: Paraphrasing improvisation: non-verbatim paraphrasing frees actor to include all impulses without word-logic trap.
Extended example showing paraphrasing that stays with scene circumstances while using personal emotional life.

nursery_rhymes.name: Nursery Rhymes
nursery_rhymes.description: Actors replace the written dialogue with nursery rhymes while working for their choices, taking care to avoid falling into the meter of the rhyme, which would reproduce the original trap of servicing the text.
nursery_rhymes.instrumental_use: Transfers emphasis from the meaning of words to impulses. Encourages irreverence and a wider emotional palette.
nursery_rhymes.craft_use: When actors return to the script after nursery rhymes, they typically feel more impulsive and irreverent in the scene.
nursery_rhymes.when_to_use: In rehearsal when concept is taking over or actors are too tied to line readings.

sources.summary: Nursery rhymes replace written lines; must avoid servicing the meter. Transfers emphasis from word-meaning to impulses; promotes irreverence.

choice_irreverence.name: Choice Irreverence
choice_irreverence.description: Being adventurous and experimental with the execution of the choice approach itself—using unexpected or even outrageous 'trick questions' or directions within the choice approach to stimulate accelerated impulsivity.
choice_irreverence.instrumental_use: Promotes a greater thrust in the process of exploring choices; prevents routine execution of choice approaches.
choice_irreverence.craft_use: Applies to any choice approach—Sense Memory, Imaginary Monologue, Believability, etc. The irreverent element exists in the direction the actor takes within the approach technique.
choice_irreverence.when_to_use: In rehearsal, when the choice approach is becoming mechanical or routine.

sources.summary: Choice irreverence: be adventurous in the execution of the choice approach; use unexpected trick questions within Sense Memory or other approaches.

antithetical_explorations.name: Antithetical Explorations
antithetical_explorations.description: Exploring the opposite of what the character seems to be—finding the loving side of an angry character, the vulnerable side of a tough one—to discover unusual behavioral facets and fragment concepts while encouraging irreverence.
antithetical_explorations.instrumental_use: Fragments concepts, promotes irreverence, and discovers the 'underlife' that motivates a character to behave in an opposite manner from the obvious.
antithetical_explorations.craft_use: Builds strong organic foundation for complex characters. Helps avoid superficial behavior by creating the impetus from which the character's emotional life comes rather than just expressing the emotion.
antithetical_explorations.when_to_use: In rehearsal, when character seems one-dimensional or when the actor is playing the result of the character's emotion rather than the cause.

sources.summary: Antithetical Explorations: explore opposite of character's obvious quality to discover underlife and fragment concept.
Actor creates loving impulses for angry character, then finds ways to be rejected, building resentments organically.

switch_trick_exploration.name: Switch-Trick Exploration
switch_trick_exploration.description: A technique where suggestions are rapidly fired at the actor during scene work, throwing them off balance so quickly that it creates a state of high impulsive irreverence, which then carries over when they return to the scripted lines.
switch_trick_exploration.instrumental_use: Creates accelerated impulsivity by overwhelming the actor's intellectual planning mechanisms. When actor returns to scene, they are more in touch with real impulsive inner life.
switch_trick_exploration.craft_use: Used exclusively in rehearsals to shake loose premeditation and result consciousness.
switch_trick_exploration.when_to_use: In rehearsal only; when actor is stuck in concept or premeditation and needs a radical reset.

sources.summary: Switch-Trick: rapid-fire suggestions throw actor off balance, creating high impulsive irreverence that transfers back to scene.

outrageous_exaggerations.name: Outrageous Exaggerations
outrageous_exaggerations.description: Actor exaggerates all behavior—vocally, physically, and verbally—to an outrageous degree, turning the scene into something like a ballet. The point is to break free of seriousness and concept, then carry the liberated impulsive quality back to the scene.
outrageous_exaggerations.instrumental_use: Brings flamboyance and music to lines; breaks the grip of concept and result consciousness. Creates impulsive freedom.
outrageous_exaggerations.craft_use: After the exaggeration phase, the actor returns to their choice with the impulsive freedom gained.
outrageous_exaggerations.when_to_use: In rehearsal when the actor feels stifled or trapped by the seriousness of the material.

sources.summary: Outrageous Exaggerations: exaggerate all behavior to break free of concept; carry impulsive freedom back to the written scene.

personal_inventory_technique.name: Personal Inventory
personal_inventory_technique.description: A self-questioning process asking 'How do I feel?' at any moment in the creative process. Responses are expressed—verbally or through behavior—or run silently as an Inner Monologue under the scene's lines.
personal_inventory_technique.instrumental_use: Stimulates organic state by discovering what is actually felt and expressing those impulses. Part of the Standard Relaxation Cluster.
personal_inventory_technique.craft_use: Used before the first line and during a scene whenever connection to internal reality is lost. Becomes an ongoing Inner Monologue that restimulates impulses and prevents premeditation.
personal_inventory_technique.when_to_use: Before scene work; whenever feeling disconnected, wooden, or caught in premeditation during a scene or performance.

sources.summary: Personal Inventory as relaxation cluster technique; instrumental and craft uses distinguished.
Example of Personal Inventory run as Inner Monologue mid-scene when feeling disconnected.

disconnected_impulsivity_technique.name: Disconnected Impulsivity
disconnected_impulsivity_technique.description: Encouraging a rapid stream of moment-to-moment impulses—internal thoughts, environmental responses, and unrelated observations—run silently under the lines of a scene to eliminate premeditation and restore organic responsiveness.
disconnected_impulsivity_technique.instrumental_use: Eliminates leadership (premeditation); stimulates quick impulsive responses; charges low-energy scenes.
disconnected_impulsivity_technique.craft_use: Used in a scene when other antidotes (Inner Monologue, Personal Inventory) haven't worked to restore impulsive flow.
disconnected_impulsivity_technique.when_to_use: In scenes or performances when lapsing into repetitive, led behavior that other techniques have not corrected.

sources.summary: Disconnected Impulsivity: last resort when other impulse-restoring techniques fail.
Eliminates premeditation; charges low-energy scenes; looks like natural environmental responsiveness from outside.

two_people_trick_each_other.name: Two People Trick Each Other
two_people_trick_each_other.description: During a scene (especially in a long run), one or both actors do unexpected but scene-appropriate things—unexpected physical touches, exaggerated moments, staring at the wall, not relating to force the other to work for attention—to keep each other on their toes and prevent predictability.
two_people_trick_each_other.instrumental_use: Stimulates fresh impulsive responsiveness and prevents the flatness that comes from repetition.
two_people_trick_each_other.craft_use: Promotes performance irreverence; keeps the play fresh and impulsive through the run.
two_people_trick_each_other.when_to_use: In a long run when scenes become stale and predictable; when impulsive responsiveness between actors has diminished.

sources.summary: Two People Trick Each Other: actors do unexpected scene-appropriate things to maintain freshness; not a game—a creative approach to prevent predictability.
Story of Lunt and Fontanne tricking each other with unexpected telephone ring; created fresh impulsiveness.

weight_gravity_relaxation.name: Weight-and-Gravity Relaxation
weight_gravity_relaxation.description: Slowly exaggerating the felt weight of the body while gently moving arms and body parts upward, then allowing them to fall with the exaggerated gravity. Done with deep breathing.
weight_gravity_relaxation.instrumental_use: Relaxes the body by using the sensation of exaggerated weight and gravity to release tension. Can be done unobtrusively on set while waiting.
weight_gravity_relaxation.craft_use: Physical relaxer used to reach the ready state before craft work begins.
weight_gravity_relaxation.when_to_use: Before scenes; whenever tension needs to be released quickly and unobtrusively.

sources.summary: Weight-and-gravity relaxation: exaggerate body weight, move arms up and let fall with gravity. Can be done while waiting on set.

tensing_relaxing.name: Tensing and Relaxing
tensing_relaxing.description: Tiring muscles by tensing/constricting the body to extreme, then slowly relaxing. Can be done whole-body at once or progressively from head to feet.
tensing_relaxing.instrumental_use: Forces physical tension to dissipate by exhausting the muscles. More extreme than weight-and-gravity relaxation; works quickly when actor is under immediate pressure.
tensing_relaxing.craft_use: Physical relaxer that can be used quickly on the spot when tension accumulates during a scene.
tensing_relaxing.when_to_use: When under immediate pressure and fast tension relief is needed; when body tension is extreme.

sources.summary: Tensing and Relaxing: tense whole body or progressively head-to-feet, then release. More extreme but effective on the spot.

deep_breathing.name: Deep Breathing
deep_breathing.description: Progressively encouraging deeper intake of air, filling from bottom up (feet to top), exhaling evenly. Awareness of hyperventilation tendency.
deep_breathing.instrumental_use: Relaxes the actor, oxygenates the blood, provides a burst of energy. Supports all physical relaxation exercises.
deep_breathing.craft_use: Used in conjunction with other relaxation techniques as part of the Standard Relaxation Cluster.
deep_breathing.when_to_use: Whenever tension or low energy needs to be addressed; before or during scene work.

sources.summary: Deep breathing: fill body from bottom up, exhale evenly. Relaxes and oxygenates; gives energy burst.

stretching_yawning.name: Stretching and Yawning
stretching_yawning.description: Natural physical activities that serve important functions: yawning takes in oxygen and opens the voice box, releasing throat tension; stretching eliminates kinks from fatigue and tension.
stretching_yawning.instrumental_use: Improves voice quality (richer, lower after yawning), releases physical tension, oxygenates.
stretching_yawning.craft_use: Can be used as quick preparation before lines; yawning specifically improves vocal quality immediately.
stretching_yawning.when_to_use: Before scenes; as a quick vocal preparation; whenever physical fatigue or throat tension is present.

sources.summary: Stretching and yawning: yawn opens voice box, releases throat tension, lowers voice several octaves. Natural physical functions.

sensitizing_technique.name: Sensitizing
sensitizing_technique.description: Heightening responsiveness of specific senses by 'living in' that sense with concentrated attention. Used instrumentally with all five senses; craftually when a specific sense is sluggish during choice work.
sensitizing_technique.instrumental_use: Opens and heightens all sensory responsiveness as part of overall relaxation and BEING preparation.
sensitizing_technique.craft_use: When senses are sluggish during sensory choice exploration—isolate the unresponsive sense and concentrate entirely in it until it responds, then reinvest in the original choice.
sensitizing_technique.when_to_use: Instrumentally as part of relaxation cluster; craftually during sensory choice work when a sense is unresponsive.

sources.summary: Sensitizing as craft technique: used when senses are sluggish during choice exploration; intersperse while working for choice.
'Live' in the specific sense until it tingles with responsiveness; same process for all five senses with different concentrations.

available_stimulus_sweep.name: Available-Stimulus Sweep
available_stimulus_sweep.description: Letting the eyes sweep the room or stage, stopping at objects that appeal to genuine interest, exploring them with as many senses as possible, then moving on.
available_stimulus_sweep.instrumental_use: Gets the actor out of themselves and into the environment through genuine curiosity. Natural enemy of tension and self-consciousness.
available_stimulus_sweep.craft_use: Builds involvement and relationship with the environment and other actors; useful as pre-scene engagement.
available_stimulus_sweep.when_to_use: Before scenes; whenever tension or self-consciousness is present and involvement needs to be built.

sources.summary: Available-Stimulus Sweep: eyes sweep room, stop at interesting objects, explore with senses. Works when you allow interest, not force it. Can be done silently.

wonder_perceive_observe.name: Wonder, Perceive, and Observe
wonder_perceive_observe.description: A three-part involvement exercise for getting into the other actor. Observe (look for obvious things), Perceive (look deeper with more complex attention), Wonder (encourage curious exploration). Done as an inner monologue or out loud with the other actor.
wonder_perceive_observe.instrumental_use: Gets the actor into the other actor through genuine curiosity; establishes the foundation of a real relationship.
wonder_perceive_observe.craft_use: Used before or during scene work to build authentic relationship involvement with the scene partner. Can be run silently throughout an entire scene.
wonder_perceive_observe.when_to_use: Before scene work with a partner; when relationship feels artificial or the actor is not genuinely engaged with the partner.

sources.summary: Wonder, Perceive, Observe: three-part involvement exercise for the other actor. Observe = obvious; Perceive = deeper; Wonder = curious exploration.
Extended example of perceiving into the other actor: reading physical tension, eye movement, emotional state. Silent inner monologue.

sensory_exploration_involvement.name: Sensory Exploration (Involvement)
sensory_exploration_involvement.description: Using sensory questions about the other actor or objects on stage to heighten involvement. Asking 'What do I see in her eyes?' and responding sensorially. Used as an involvement technique distinct from sensory choice-creation.
sensory_exploration_involvement.instrumental_use: Heightens level of involvement through genuine sensory engagement with the environment and partner.
sensory_exploration_involvement.craft_use: Used as a prelude to exploring the first choice in a scene—sensory exploration aligned with the first emotional obligation warms up the actor's responsiveness.
sensory_exploration_involvement.when_to_use: In the minutes before a rehearsal or scene; when involvement needs to be heightened; when transitioning from instrumental preparation to craft preparation.

sources.summary: Sensory Exploration for involvement: use sensory questions about the other actor or objects to heighten involvement before exploring choices.
Example: sensory exploration about actress aligned with first emotional obligation of concern and empathy.

imaginary_monologue_preparation.name: Emotionally Meaningful Imaginary Monologues
imaginary_monologue_preparation.description: Talking to an absent person as if they were present—saying things that are affecting and meaningful to stimulate desired emotional responses. The imagined person's responses are allowed for (unlike Coffin Monologue).
imaginary_monologue_preparation.instrumental_use: Stimulates strong emotional responses by addressing significant people in the actor's life about meaningful matters.
imaginary_monologue_preparation.craft_use: Used as an emotional preparation to create a specific emotional state before a scene. The content is chosen to stimulate the desired emotional foundation.
imaginary_monologue_preparation.when_to_use: As emotional preparation before a scene; when specific emotional states need to be reached and sense memory or music haven't worked.

sources.summary: Imaginary Monologue: talk to absent person as if present; allow imagined responses; intelligently manipulate content to stimulate strong emotional responses.

sense_memory_with_impacting_objects.name: Sense Memory with Emotionally Impacting Objects
sense_memory_with_impacting_objects.description: Using Sense Memory to recreate a meaningful object—a person, pet, place, photograph, letter, sound, music—in order to elevate emotional state. The object is chosen for its emotional impact.
sense_memory_with_impacting_objects.instrumental_use: Can be used silently without calling attention to itself; doesn't interfere with what the actor is doing or saying.
sense_memory_with_impacting_objects.craft_use: A silent, unobtrusive way to prepare emotionally before or during a scene. Can be done while waiting on set; preparation anytime and anywhere.
sense_memory_with_impacting_objects.when_to_use: As emotional preparation; before scenes or during a long run to prime the instrument; when emotional accessibility needs to be elevated.

sources.summary: Sense Memory with impacting objects: can be done silently; doesn't call attention to itself; choice can be anything meaningful—person, dog, place, music.
Example: creating father sensorially using multiple senses—visual, tactile, olfactory. Continue until emotional responses arise naturally.

music_preparation.name: Music (Emotional Preparation)
music_preparation.description: Using a meaningful piece of music to prepare emotionally. Can be listened to, hummed internally, or recreated sensorially. Music is described as a 'very rich choice area.'
music_preparation.instrumental_use: Stimulates emotional responses by evoking memories, feelings, and associations tied to specific music.
music_preparation.craft_use: Prime the instrument with music related to the scene's emotional needs; catalogue music and its effects to have it available when needed. Humming is also valid—'if it works and is not intrusive, use it.'
music_preparation.when_to_use: As emotional preparation before or during a scene; especially effective when the emotional obligation involves nostalgia, past experiences, or strong feeling-associations.

sources.summary: Music: a rich choice area; catalogue music and responses to it; humming is not cheating—use whatever works organically.
Example: using nostalgic music to prime instrument before a scene about past times.

passions_workout.name: Passions Workout
passions_workout.description: An energizing exercise where the actor explores and expresses their deepest passions—the things they feel most strongly about—with large vocal and physical commitment.
passions_workout.instrumental_use: Gets adrenaline going; elevates energy level; makes the actor more emotionally available even if the character isn't passionate.
passions_workout.craft_use: A craft preparation to raise the overall emotional threshold so that the subsequent choice work is easier to access.
passions_workout.when_to_use: When emotional availability is low; when the actor needs to elevate their overall expressive range before dealing with material.

sources.summary: Passions Workout: instrumentally gets adrenaline going; craftually elevates emotional accessibility and makes actor more available.

joy_euphoria.name: Joy and Euphoria
joy_euphoria.description: An exaggerated state of large experience and flamboyant expression where the actor commits to expressing joy and euphoria with full physical and vocal commitment.
joy_euphoria.instrumental_use: Stretches the instrument to expressive extreme. Makes the actor feel good, that anything is possible, and that no task is too difficult. Acting should be fun.
joy_euphoria.craft_use: Creates internal excitement and external expression that is visually compelling. Good preparation for scenes requiring high positive energy or for loosening tight instruments.
joy_euphoria.when_to_use: When the actor is feeling restricted, low-energy, or overly serious about their work; as a craft preparation for high-energy characters.

sources.summary: Joy and Euphoria: exaggerated state, full physical/vocal commitment; stretches instrument to expressive extreme; feeling that anything is possible.

superman_exercise.name: Superman
superman_exercise.description: An ego preparation where the actor declares their absolute superiority, power, and capability in all areas—'I have always done what I set out to do! I am a Superman!'—with full commitment.
superman_exercise.instrumental_use: Builds ego strength and self-confidence; instills belief in one's own abilities.
superman_exercise.craft_use: Meets the challenge of large ego obligations in characters. Used when the character has a much larger or more confident ego than the actor currently has.
superman_exercise.when_to_use: When the character has a powerful, dominant, or supremely confident ego state and the actor does not match this currently.

sources.summary: Superman: ego preparation for large ego obligations; commit to declaration of total capability and superiority.
Can continue for many minutes until in a very different ego state, hopefully closer to the character's.

counting_blessings.name: Counting Your Blessings
counting_blessings.description: Listing all the good things in the actor's life and about themselves—health, relationships, accomplishments, talents, growth—done audibly or silently as an inner monologue.
counting_blessings.instrumental_use: Helps put ego into proper perspective; a good tool for actors who tend to be hard on themselves.
counting_blessings.craft_use: Good craft preparation when the character has a positive self-view that the actor doesn't currently match.
counting_blessings.when_to_use: Before rehearsal or performance; anytime the actor is being overly self-critical or when the character's ego state is more positive than the actor's.

sources.summary: Counting Your Blessings: list good things about yourself and your life; can be done audibly or as inner monologue; helps actors hard on themselves.

sensory_self_endowment_technique.name: Sensory Self-Endowments
sensory_self_endowment_technique.description: Using Sense Memory questions to create the felt experience of different physical features on the body—adding or eliminating hair, weight, body size, attractiveness—to build ego state or character physicality.
sensory_self_endowment_technique.instrumental_use: Helps the actor feel better about themselves by creating a sensory experience of desired physical features.
sensory_self_endowment_technique.craft_use: Establishes a foundation for how the character feels about themselves, especially when the character's physical self-perception differs from the actor's. Has limitless possibilities.
sensory_self_endowment_technique.when_to_use: When the character's physical self-image (attractive, unattractive, physically powerful, weak, etc.) differs from the actor's, creating a dichotomy that would interfere with fulfillment of the piece.

sources.summary: Sensory Self-Endowments: create sensory experience of different physical features; used to build ego state matching character.
Example: creating sensory experience of long, thick hair using weight, texture, sound questions. Limitless possibilities.

accepting_academy_award.name: Accepting the Academy Award
accepting_academy_award.description: Fantasizing in detail about receiving an Academy Award—the event, the speech, the reactions of others—as an ego-building exercise.
accepting_academy_award.instrumental_use: Builds ego and sense of specialness; practically universal appeal as a fantasy for actors.
accepting_academy_award.craft_use: Can be adapted as a craft preparation for character ego states requiring strong public validation.
accepting_academy_award.when_to_use: As an ego preparation; whenever the actor needs to build a strong positive self-image.

sources.summary: Accepting the Academy Award: widely appealing ego fantasy preparation for actors.

fantasies_self_aggrandizements.name: Fantasies and Self-Aggrandizements
fantasies_self_aggrandizements.description: Creating elaborate personal fantasies of success, admiration, achievement, and specialness—as an inner monologue, a sharing exercise with another actor, or by 'walking through it.'
fantasies_self_aggrandizements.instrumental_use: Elevates ego state; generates a pleasant, confident sense of self that maintains itself for hours. Can be practiced as entertainment and then becomes easy to deploy when needed.
fantasies_self_aggrandizements.craft_use: Elevates the ego states of both actors when done as an improvisation before rehearsal. Creates sense of reality and fun.
fantasies_self_aggrandizements.when_to_use: Before rehearsal or performance; as an antidote to insecurity or low self-confidence. Can also be done 'for fun' to make it easier to access later.

sources.summary: Fantasies: best done by sneaking into them; personal appeal works best. Can be running inner monologue while doing other things.
Morris's airplane fantasy example; nice sense of self maintains for hours after; can be done as improvisation with other actor.

progress_reports.name: Progress Reports
progress_reports.description: Acknowledging and articulating specific positive changes and growth in oneself over a defined period of time. Must not embellish reality—must be genuinely believed.
progress_reports.instrumental_use: Puts ego into proper perspective; helps actors who tend to be self-critical by building evidence-based self-appreciation.
progress_reports.craft_use: Good craft preparation when the character has achieved positive growth or change that the actor currently doesn't feel about themselves.
progress_reports.when_to_use: Anytime; especially when the actor is being self-critical or when the character's self-awareness of growth differs from the actor's.

sources.summary: Progress Reports: acknowledge specific real growth; cannot embellish; must be believed. Good for actors hard on themselves.

magic_pocket_exercise.name: Magic-Pocket Exercise
magic_pocket_exercise.description: Imagining that one has extraordinary items in their pocket—a Hollywood contract, a ten-million-dollar check, car keys to a Ferrari—things that would create a state of great power, security, and specialness.
magic_pocket_exercise.instrumental_use: Stretches imagination while building ego. Creates a feeling of power, security, and specialness.
magic_pocket_exercise.craft_use: Can be used as a craft preparation when a character is in a very powerful, secure, or privileged position.
magic_pocket_exercise.when_to_use: As an ego-building or imagination-stretching exercise; before scenes requiring characters of great power or privilege.

sources.summary: Magic-Pocket Exercise: imagine having extraordinary items in your pocket; stretches imagination while building ego.

two_person_validations.name: Two-Person Validations
two_person_validations.description: Two actors, before rehearsing, sit together and honestly share specific positive things they see and appreciate in each other. Done conversationally, based on truth, using selective emphasis on the good things.
two_person_validations.instrumental_use: Helps both actors feel good about themselves and each other.
two_person_validations.craft_use: Works even better when the positive observations relate to the material's obligations. Especially good for scenes requiring mutual affection, respect, or appreciation.
two_person_validations.when_to_use: Before rehearsal or performance; especially valuable for scenes requiring genuine appreciation, affection, or mutual admiration between characters.

sources.summary: Two-Person Validations: actors share honest positive observations about each other; based on truth; can be done while making up or getting dressed.

abandonment.name: Abandonment
abandonment.description: Lying on the floor and having an all-out tantrum—moving the body with abandonment in all directions with no physical interference or leadership, while screaming and making huge uninterrupted vocal expurgations.
abandonment.instrumental_use: Purges tension and conglomerated impulses. Done until tension is purged.
abandonment.craft_use: Used with specific content to build a foundation for volatile, irrational character behavior. Must have a specific goal: what emotional state to stimulate and what content the Abandonment must have.
abandonment.when_to_use: When the character is highly volatile and irrational; as an instrumental tension-purger. Not appropriate for subtle emotional states.

sources.summary: Abandonment: all-out tantrum on floor with body and voice; instrumental = purge tension; craft = specific goal to build foundation for volatile character.

vesuvius.name: Vesuvius
vesuvius.description: A vocal and verbal expurgation starting with here-and-now realities, then allowing creative leadership to direct the content toward a specific emotional threshold needed for the material.
vesuvius.instrumental_use: Purges conglomerated tension and feelings; a very good instrumental expurgative preparation.
vesuvius.craft_use: Content of the Vesuvius is adjusted to build the specific emotional threshold required by the role. Starts with personal real feelings, then shifts toward content that produces the needed emotional life.
vesuvius.when_to_use: When large emotional preparation is needed; as a foundation-builder for strong emotional states. Not appropriate for subtle work.

sources.summary: Vesuvius: vocal/verbal expurgation starting with here-and-now, then creatively led to build specific emotional threshold. Content depends on emotional obligation.

confront_encounter.name: Confront and Encounter
confront_encounter.description: Aggressively confronting real people—present or imaginary—with unfinished business, grievances, indictments, or strong impulses. Done as an imaginary monologue with absent people or conversationally with present ones.
confront_encounter.instrumental_use: Purges suppressed feelings of grievance, resentment, and unfinished relational business.
confront_encounter.craft_use: Stimulates strong confrontational or aggressive emotional states needed for specific scenes. The content of the imaginary monologue is chosen to produce the desired emotional quality.
confront_encounter.when_to_use: When the scene requires strong confrontational impulses; when the character has a history of grievance or unresolved conflict.

sources.summary: Confront and Encounter: confront real or imaginary people with unfinished business; usually done as imaginary monologue since real people not present at needed times.

go_crazy.name: Go Crazy
go_crazy.description: A large, exaggerated exercise where the actor goes from moment to moment with every impulse—making sounds, saying words, jumping, rolling, mixing external and internal stimuli—going from serious to absurd in seconds, with no required logic.
go_crazy.instrumental_use: Frees the actor from restrictions; encourages irreverence in everything that follows; creates unbroken impulse flow.
go_crazy.craft_use: Sets up foundation of flamboyance and colorful behavior; good preparation for free-flowing, expressive, uninhibited characters.
go_crazy.when_to_use: Before scenes requiring high impulsive expressiveness; when restricted or inhibited; as a pre-scene preparation for volatile or exuberant characters.

sources.summary: Go Crazy: all-out exaggerated impulsive expression; frees restrictions; encourages irreverence; creates unbroken impulse flow and flamboyance.

anger_rage_expression.name: Anger-and-Rage Expression
anger_rage_expression.description: Expressing anger and rage with large vocal and physical commitment. The craft use requires intelligent choice of content—what to express anger about—to ventilate the right kind of emotional life.
anger_rage_expression.instrumental_use: Purges anger and tension instrumentally with any content.
anger_rage_expression.craft_use: Used to stimulate underlying anger that a character may be hiding with rational control—creates the underlife before the controlled surface is added. Content must be chosen to produce the right emotional quality.
anger_rage_expression.when_to_use: When the character's underlife is anger/rage that is covered by a composed exterior; when anger preparation is needed for a scene. Not appropriate for subtle material.

sources.summary: Anger-and-Rage Expression as craft technique: must choose content that ventilates the right emotional life; good for characters who are seething inside but appear composed.

nonverbal_relating_exercise.name: A Nonverbal Relating Exercise
nonverbal_relating_exercise.description: Two actors sit facing each other and relate without words, gestures, or mime—simply allowing whatever is happening in the moment to happen between them, responding to each other in an unbroken chain of emotional responses.
nonverbal_relating_exercise.instrumental_use: Deepens involvement by removing verbal description; transfers energy to direct emotional contact.
nonverbal_relating_exercise.craft_use: Establishes a real emotional relationship with the scene partner based on non-verbal emotional communication. The relationship deepens as the exercise proceeds.
