nonverbal_relating_exercise.when_to_use: As a relationship preparation before scenes; when the emotional relationship between scene partners needs to be deepened before work on text.

sources.summary: Nonverbal Relating Exercise: no words, no gestures, no mime; allow what happens to happen; unbroken chain of emotional responses.

looking_for_new_things_in_partner.name: Looking for Things in the Other Person You Haven't Seen Before
looking_for_new_things_in_partner.description: Deliberately observing and discovering specific features and qualities in the other actor that have previously been taken for granted—physical features, behavioral patterns, emotional qualities.
looking_for_new_things_in_partner.instrumental_use: Trains genuine curiosity and involvement with other people. The actor as student of life and behavior must be genuinely interested in people.
looking_for_new_things_in_partner.craft_use: Builds authentic relationship investment in the scene partner; promotes genuine caring and interest.
looking_for_new_things_in_partner.when_to_use: As a relationship preparation; when the relationship with the scene partner has become habitual, taken-for-granted, or routine.

sources.summary: Looking for new things in the partner: deliberately discover previously overlooked features. Actor as student of life must find everything interesting.

feedback_technique.name: Feedback
feedback_technique.description: Two actors ask each other honest, important questions in a conversational framework to build authentic relationship investment. Goes beyond pleasantries to genuine curiosity about each other.
feedback_technique.instrumental_use: Builds genuine curiosity and relationship between actors.
feedback_technique.craft_use: Relationship preparation specifically designed to build the authentic relationship needed for material obligations.
feedback_technique.when_to_use: Before scenes requiring deep relationship investment; especially when actors don't know each other well.

sources.summary: Feedback: conversational exchange of important questions; no rigid structure but structured; builds real relationship.

like_dislike_feedback.name: Like-and-Dislike Feedback
like_dislike_feedback.description: A version of Feedback where actors exclusively share what they like about each other and what they 'like least'—never using the word 'dislike' to keep the exercise positive and productive.
like_dislike_feedback.instrumental_use: Builds mutual appreciation while addressing genuine concerns in a positive framework.
like_dislike_feedback.craft_use: Relationship preparation that creates authentic mutual regard while acknowledging real differences.
like_dislike_feedback.when_to_use: When relationship needs to be both honest and positive; as variation of Feedback.

sources.summary: Like-and-Dislike Feedback: exclusive focus on what is liked and liked least; 'dislike' word avoided to keep exercise positive.

intimate_sharing_relationship.name: Intimate Sharing (Relationship Use)
intimate_sharing_relationship.description: Two actors share genuinely intimate and meaningful personal material with each other specifically to build closeness and shared understanding rather than to purge fear of exposure.
intimate_sharing_relationship.instrumental_use: Builds genuine intimacy between actors through authentic personal sharing.
intimate_sharing_relationship.craft_use: Creates a foundation of closeness and real relationship investment needed for scenes requiring deep intimacy. Different from the emotional preparation use—goal is closeness with the other person, not elevation of vulnerability state.
intimate_sharing_relationship.when_to_use: Before scenes requiring genuine intimacy, closeness, or deep personal familiarity between characters.

sources.summary: Intimate Sharing for relationship: goal is establishing intimacy/involvement with another person (vs. emotional preparation goal of elevating vulnerability).

endowments_technique.name: Endowments
endowments_technique.description: Sensorially adding or substituting qualities in the other actor or an object through the Sense Memory process—making them have features, qualities, or characteristics they don't actually have.
endowments_technique.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental; occasionally used to increase comfort with a scene partner.
endowments_technique.craft_use: Changes the other actor into the character they are supposed to be (relationship obligation) or creates specific relational feelings—attraction, repulsion, love, fear. Core technique for relationship fulfillment.
endowments_technique.when_to_use: When the scene partner doesn't naturally evoke the needed relationship feelings; when character relationship obligations are not being felt.

sources.summary: Endowments: totally dependent on Sense Memory; cannot be done any other way effectively.
Like sculpting with Sense Memory: add/subtract until the needed reality is created.

animation_workout.name: Animation Workout
animation_workout.description: An irreverent preparation where the actor expresses everything they feel in a highly animated, exaggerated manner—large vocal and physical expression—over an extended period.
animation_workout.instrumental_use: Sets up freedom to allow whatever is felt to come out; encourages emotional abandonment of expression; creates a permission state that carries into rehearsal.
animation_workout.craft_use: Freeing quality promotes irreverence in all that follows; impulses flow into the choice, choice stimulates new impulses, those impulses express through the lines.
animation_workout.when_to_use: Before rehearsal in a restrictive environment; when the actor needs to establish permission for large, uninhibited expression.

sources.summary: Animation Workout: express everything in highly animated manner; freeing quality carries into rehearsal.
Can start humorous and move to heart-wrenching grief; chain reaction of impulse flow follows.

freedom_workout.name: Freedom Workout
freedom_workout.description: Similar to Animation Workout but with more physical freedom and less requirement for organic expression—can look like ballet, leaping and twirling, or animal-like movement. The freedom of the body liberates the instrument.
freedom_workout.instrumental_use: Creates physical and expressive freedom; liberates the instrument from physical and emotional restrictions.
freedom_workout.craft_use: Used as an irreverent preparation before scenes requiring physical freedom and expressive range.
freedom_workout.when_to_use: Before rehearsal when physical and expressive restriction are problems; when the body is tight and movement is constrained.

sources.summary: Freedom Workout: similar to Animation but more physical freedom; less organically required; can be ballet-like or animal-like.

outrageous_monologue.name: Outrageous Monologue
outrageous_monologue.description: Taking a monologue the actor is currently working on and approaching it with complete disregard for meaning, content, or logic—distorting words, approaching characteristically in bizarre ways (like a punch-drunk fighter), treating it with theatrical blasphemy.
outrageous_monologue.instrumental_use: Cuts the cords of logic, expectations, and preconceived notions. Creates a superstructure of trust in impulsive expression.
outrageous_monologue.craft_use: Used as an irreverent preparation to free the actor from their own concepts about the material before doing it authentically.
outrageous_monologue.when_to_use: Before rehearsing a monologue or scene when concept and expectation are locking the actor into fixed interpretation.

sources.summary: Outrageous Monologue: blasphemous approach to material; cuts cords of logic and preconceived notions; creates trust in impulsive expression.

nonverbal_approach.name: Nonverbal Approach (Irreverent Preparation)
nonverbal_approach.description: Doing a scene without words—replacing the dialogue with movement, physical expression, or silence—to liberate the actor from the predictable emotional expectations carried by the words.
nonverbal_approach.instrumental_use: Removes predictable emotional associations with specific words; creates new variety of expression and colors of life.
nonverbal_approach.craft_use: Frees the actor to be as expressive without words, then carry that freedom back to the scene with words. Words should not be the governor of emotional flow.
nonverbal_approach.when_to_use: When the written words are triggering predictable emotional responses rather than authentic impulse-based behavior.

sources.summary: Nonverbal Approach: strange freedom replaces words; infinite more colors and kinds of life emerge without verbal expectations.

parallel_improvisation.name: Parallel Improvising
parallel_improvisation.description: The actor chooses a circumstance from their own life that parallels the emotional elements of the scene and improvises that parallel situation rather than the written scene.
parallel_improvisation.instrumental_use: Allows other impulses to surface in behavior; creates authentic personal engagement with the emotional content.
parallel_improvisation.craft_use: Used as an irreverent preparation or rehearsal technique to find authentic personal connections to the material's emotional content before returning to the written text.
parallel_improvisation.when_to_use: In rehearsal when the written scene feels foreign or disconnected; when finding personal parallels helps build organic connection to material.

sources.summary: Parallel Improvising: improvise personal parallel situation to scene's emotional elements; allows other impulses to surface.

sense_memory_choice_approach.name: Sense Memory (Choice Approach)
sense_memory_choice_approach.description: The systematic use of sensory questions—directed to each of the five senses—to create absent objects, people, places, or events so that the actor can actually experience their presence. The foundational choice approach.
sense_memory_choice_approach.instrumental_use: With sufficient daily practice, the senses can be trained to respond to absent objects as if present.
sense_memory_choice_approach.craft_use: The primary choice approach for creating any object, person, place, or event that will fulfill an obligation. Process continues during scene work—between lines, while listening to other actor. Can be done silently and unobtrusively.
sense_memory_choice_approach.when_to_use: As the primary approach for most obligations; whenever a specific absent object needs to be made real for the actor during scene work.

sources.summary: Sense Memory as choice approach: explorative process using all five senses; creates absent objects so actor can actually experience them.
Process: directed sensory questions to specific senses; can ask 50+ questions per sense area; go back and forth between senses.
Process continues during scene—between lines, while listening; actor still hears and is affected by other actor.

task_inventory_technique.name: Task Inventory
task_inventory_technique.description: A ten-step systematic process for finding the right choice when stuck, concluding with a Fail-Safe Device. Identifies obligation, finds personal parallel experience, specifies common denominator, selects choice, determines approach.
task_inventory_technique.craft_use: Eliminates confusion when trying to find the right choice. Can be done quickly with experience; even usable in the limited time of film work.
task_inventory_technique.when_to_use: When the actor cannot identify a choice for an obligation; when all other approaches to finding a choice have failed; as preventive practice even when not needed.

sources.summary: Task Inventory: 10-step process plus Fail-Safe Device; practice it even when not needed to become facile.
Fail-Safe Device: when all else fails, ask what would create that feeling right now.

daily_inventories_technique.name: Daily Inventories
daily_inventories_technique.description: The disciplined daily practice of cataloguing emotional responses to everyday stimuli in specific sensory detail, building a personal library of choices for future use.
daily_inventories_technique.instrumental_use: Trains the actor to be alive and responsive to all stimuli; develops the habit of awareness and specific emotional responsiveness.
daily_inventories_technique.craft_use: Builds a rich, personally relevant library of choices that can be used in scene work. The actor who catalogues daily will always have choices available.
daily_inventories_technique.when_to_use: Every day, throughout the day—a discipline of living as an actor 24 hours a day.

sources.summary: Daily Inventories: catalogue emotional responses to everyday stimuli specifically and sensorially for future use.
Inventory questions for a specific encounter: sensory details of every element.

choice_hunt_technique.name: Choice Hunt
choice_hunt_technique.description: A lying-down, eyes-closed exercise where the actor goes back in time through meaningful personal experiences, allowing memories to surface and cataloguing experiences with strong emotional potential.
choice_hunt_technique.instrumental_use: Opens access to buried memories and emotional experiences; encourages expression of stimulated impulses.
choice_hunt_technique.craft_use: Discovers potential choices from personal history that haven't been considered before; expands the actor's choice repertoire.
choice_hunt_technique.when_to_use: In solitude; when the actor's choice repertoire feels limited; as a regular practice to continually expand available choices.

sources.summary: Choice Hunt: lying down, eyes closed, revisiting meaningful events; generates unexpected impulses; memory begins releasing spontaneously with regular practice.

available_stimulus_approach.name: Available Stimulus (Choice Approach)
available_stimulus_approach.description: Using what is actually present in the environment—the other actor, the place, sounds, objects, energy—as the stimulus to fulfill scene obligations. First consideration before any other choice approach.
available_stimulus_approach.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental—but can be used instrumentally when the environment itself stimulates involvement.
available_stimulus_approach.craft_use: Most efficient choice approach when the environment contains elements that naturally stimulate the needed emotional response. Use Selective Emphasis to focus on the most impelling element. Use Inner Monologue to intensify the available stimulus response.
available_stimulus_approach.when_to_use: Always check first—before considering other choice approaches. If Available Stimulus can do the job, use it; it requires the least additional work.

sources.summary: Available Stimulus: should be first consideration; most available if it exists.
Checklist of questions for Available Stimulus exploration: environment, objects, people, sounds, light, words in the play.

sensory_suggestion_approach.name: Sensory Suggestion (Choice Approach)
sensory_suggestion_approach.description: Combining narrative description ('He looks enraged and moves toward me') with targeted sensory questions to quickly build an imaginary reality that pure Sense Memory would take much longer to establish.
sensory_suggestion_approach.craft_use: Used when time is limited and a specific emotional response must be quickly established; when changing the perceived reality of the other actor in the scene; when the full Sense Memory process would take too long relative to the scene's demands.
sensory_suggestion_approach.when_to_use: When speed of establishment matters; when working with an imaginary person or trying to change the perceived reality of the actual scene partner.

sources.summary: Sensory Suggestion: narrative + sensory questions combined; ten times faster than pure Sense Memory.
Can change perceived behavior/reality of actual other actor in scene using Sensory Suggestion.

evocative_words_approach.name: Evocative Words (Choice Approach)
evocative_words_approach.description: Using carefully selected clusters of words, phrases, and associative language—from personal experiences or belief structures—to evoke the desired emotional state, run as an inner monologue or audible preparation.
evocative_words_approach.craft_use: Particularly effective in scenes with many lines and rapid dialogue where sensory work would interfere. Run as inner monologue under the scene's dialogue. Also excellent in combination with other approaches (Sense Memory for environment + Evocative Words for emotional sustaining).
evocative_words_approach.when_to_use: When rapid dialogue makes sensory choice approaches impractical; when working from strong personal belief structures; when other approaches have not produced the needed emotional state.

sources.summary: Evocative Words: good when rapid dialogue prevents sensory work; relates to experiences or belief structures.
Can use belief structures (Holocaust, fascism) to fuel character obligations throughout play.

believability_approach.name: Believability (Choice Approach)
believability_approach.description: Both actors (or one alone) build a shared fictional reality by starting with threads of actual truth and progressively adding untrue elements—until truth and untruth become indistinguishable. Selectively emphasize both.
believability_approach.instrumental_use: Can be used instrumentally to build genuine relationship and trust between actors.
believability_approach.craft_use: One of the most enjoyable choice approaches. Particularly powerful as a collaborative technique—both actors structure the Believability together before or during rehearsal to create shared relational reality for the scene.
believability_approach.when_to_use: When the relationship obligation requires a history or emotional depth that doesn't exist between the actual actors; when building shared circumstantial reality for complex relational scenes.

sources.summary: Believability: enjoyable; can be done alone or collaboratively; mixture of truth and untruth.
Deftly mix truth and untruth until indistinguishable; selectively emphasize both.

trick_believability.name: Trick Believability
trick_believability.description: Launching a real improvisation with the other actor, without revealing that it is a structured Believability approach, in order to stimulate the emotional life and relationship dynamics the scene requires. The other actor's authentic responses feed the initiating actor's reality.
trick_believability.instrumental_use: Can be instrumental in generating genuine irritation, conflict, or other emotional states between actors.
trick_believability.craft_use: Used when the scene requires a real, charged relationship reality and conventional preparation has not produced it; particularly effective when actors know the material's obligations and can calibrate the improvisation toward those ends.
trick_believability.when_to_use: When working with actors who would welcome such an approach (must know your collaborators); when conventional approaches have not generated the required relational life; use common sense about when to reveal what was done.

sources.summary: Trick Believability: one actor launches real improvisation without the other knowing; their response feeds the reality.
Extended example: actor criticizes actress's lateness to stimulate irritation; she doesn't know it's a crafted approach.
Possible consequences; use with actors who would welcome it; use common sense.

endowments_approach.name: Endowments (Choice Approach)
endowments_approach.description: Sensorially changing an available object or person by endowing it with qualities it does not possess—starting with the existing reality and asking sensory questions that progressively transform perception of it.
endowments_approach.craft_use: Used when: the scene partner needs to evoke a specific emotional relationship (endow with features of someone important); the character is older/sicker/different from the actual actor; a prop needs to feel like a priceless object; a room must feel like a different architectural space; one obligation must be addressed through another actor's perceived reality.
endowments_approach.when_to_use: When available stimulus doesn't produce the needed relationship or emotional response; when endowing the other actor with the features of someone from the actor's own life who produces the right emotional reality.

sources.summary: Endowments: sensorially changing object or person with qualities it doesn't have.
Extended aging endowment process example; one choice addressing two obligations simultaneously.
Endowing other actor with features of someone else: start with similarities, use contrasts, endow sensorially.

imaginary_monologue_approach.name: Imaginary Monologues (Choice Approach)
imaginary_monologue_approach.description: Talking out loud (or as inner monologue) to an imaginary person as if they were present, selecting the right person and the right things to say in order to stimulate the emotional state required by the scene.
imaginary_monologue_approach.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental—but can ventilate deep personal material.
imaginary_monologue_approach.craft_use: Most common use: as preparation before a scene or as inner monologue under dialogue. Can be combined with Sense Memory (sensory questions interspersed with words) to dimensionalize the imaginary person's presence and create unpredictability. Can be used to address any of the seven major obligations.
imaginary_monologue_approach.when_to_use: When a specific emotional state or relationship dynamic is required and it can be paralleled by a real person from the actor's life; when the actor has much to say to someone living or dead; when fast dialogue makes sensory approaches difficult (run as inner monologue).

sources.summary: Imaginary Monologues: talk to an imaginary person as if present; pick right person and right content.
Combined with Sense Memory for dimensionality; sensory questions interspersed; allows imaginary person to 'respond.'

imaginary_dialogue_approach.name: Imaginary Dialogues (Choice Approach)
imaginary_dialogue_approach.description: Two actors simultaneously doing Imaginary Monologues out loud to their respective imaginary choices, relating what they say to each other, and allowing the other's words to come from their respective imaginary person.
imaginary_dialogue_approach.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental—though it fires the emotional furnace of both actors.
imaginary_dialogue_approach.craft_use: Highly collaborative; requires agreement on choices. Both actors identify obligations, each selects an imaginary person from their own life who parallels the scene's dynamics. Done best before or during rehearsal; the stimulated life carries directly into the scene text.
imaginary_dialogue_approach.when_to_use: When the scene requires a deeply charged relational reality between two characters; when the relationship between the actual actors is insufficiently charged; when both actors work in this manner and can collaborate on choices.

sources.summary: Imaginary Dialogues: two actors doing Imaginary Monologues together out loud; each allows the other's words to come from their choice.
Waiting for Lefty example: detailed choices and obligations for both characters; each actor picks from personal life.

externals_approach.name: Externals (Choice Approach)
externals_approach.description: Getting a physical sense of an animal, another person, an inanimate object, or an insect through one's own instrument, then translating that sense into human behavior for use in a scene. One of the richest and most complex choice approach categories.
externals_approach.instrumental_use: Strongly instrumental—can pique primal and primitive parts of the actor's instrument that no other technique can reach, effecting total physical, emotional, intellectual, and vocal change.
externals_approach.craft_use: Animals: observe and absorb through the eight-element approach technique; translate to human behavior by degrees; use to fulfill character, emotional, historic, and time-and-place obligations. People: same approach, no translation needed. Inanimate Objects and Insects covered separately.
externals_approach.when_to_use: When the character requires physical attitudes, thought patterns, or behavior the actor cannot reach through any other means; when character obligations involve primal/primitive qualities; when the role explicitly invokes animal-like qualities; when conventional choices have not produced the needed transformation.

sources.summary: Externals: four involvements; richest category; can be used for almost all obligation areas.
Six purposes; keys to the unconscious: approaching inner life from outside in.
Using an animal as a choice: can address historic, character, and emotional obligations; examples from Morris's own career.

objects_contact_body_approach.name: Objects that Come into Contact with the Body (Choice Approach)
objects_contact_body_approach.description: Using clothing, accessories, weapons, or props worn or held by the actor—real or sensorially created—to stimulate changes in emotional state, physical rhythm, ego, and behavior.
objects_contact_body_approach.instrumental_use: Can have instrumental dimension: putting on boots or a weapon may access physical confidence, ego, or aggression that opens the actor's instrument.
objects_contact_body_approach.craft_use: When the real article is available in the scene: it becomes Available Stimulus—just relate to it. When not available: use Sense Memory to systematically create the object's presence through sensory questions about weight, pressure, texture, temperature, movement, sound.
objects_contact_body_approach.when_to_use: When character obligation involves specific physical attitudes or emotional states that an object would naturally produce; when the character's world includes meaningful objects not available in performance.

sources.summary: Objects that Contact Body: clothing and objects worn/held stimulate emotional and behavioral state changes.
When real object unavailable: Sense Memory creates it; when available: becomes Available Stimulus.

moment_to_moment_inner_monologue.name: Moment-to-Moment Inner Monologue
moment_to_moment_inner_monologue.description: Including between the words of a scene, as an internal verbal flow, what is actually going on inside the actor right now—allowing those impulses to express through and affect the dialogue.
moment_to_moment_inner_monologue.instrumental_use: Strongly instrumental: prevents result-consciousness and leadership by anchoring the actor in the present moment's actual experience.
moment_to_moment_inner_monologue.craft_use: Primary antidote for leadership and result-consciousness. Keeps the actor in contact with his real impulsive reality. Also used to take advantage of Available Reality—when the actor feels things similar to what the character feels, ventilating those through the lines promotes scene reality.
moment_to_moment_inner_monologue.when_to_use: When the actor has lost touch with internal impulsive reality; when leadership or result-consciousness has been identified; when what the actor actually feels in the moment parallels the scene's requirements.

sources.summary: Moment-to-Moment Inner Monologue: include what is going on inside between words; antidote for leadership.
Nursery rhyme example showing internal monologue between words; impulses encouraged to express through dialogue.

character_inner_monologue.name: Character Inner Monologue
character_inner_monologue.description: Structuring an internal monologue that parallels the character's thought process by using the actor's own personal realities—not thinking the character's literal thoughts, but finding personal parallels that create an emotionally equivalent inner life.
character_inner_monologue.craft_use: Run under the lines throughout a scene or play to fuel the desired emotional life. The actor identifies how the character thinks—his points of view, prejudices, emotional biases—and finds personal equivalents from his own life and belief structures. The monologue varies somewhat from performance to performance.
character_inner_monologue.when_to_use: When the character's inner thought process must be sustained beneath ongoing dialogue; when the character has strong prejudices, beliefs, or biases for which the actor can find personal parallels.

sources.summary: Character Inner Monologue: parallel the character's thought process using actor's own realities.
'Chickenshit colonel' example: Morris uses his own unbending commitment to craft as parallel to the colonel's rigidity.

sensory_speculation_approach.name: Sensory Speculation (Choice Approach)
sensory_speculation_approach.description: Using the Sense Memory process speculatively—exploring through imagined sensory questions what it would feel like to touch, smell, taste, or hear an object or person the actor has never physically experienced—in order to stimulate the required emotional state.
sensory_speculation_approach.craft_use: Used when a needed object or person is inaccessible to physical contact; when an emotional state must be stimulated through sensory engagement with something distant or unreachable; when endowing someone with behavior never previously witnessed.
sensory_speculation_approach.when_to_use: When the object of the scene's emotion is across the stage and can't be touched; when the actor must feel attraction, terror, or desire toward something not yet experienced; when sensory endowment requires speculation about unfamiliar behavior.

sources.summary: Sensory Speculation: speculatively exploring through Sense Memory what it would feel like to experience an inaccessible object or person.

sub_personality_approach.name: Sub-Personalities (Choice Approach)
sub_personality_approach.description: Identifying which sub-personality controls the character in the material, then eliciting the matching sub-personality from within the actor's own instrument, so that the entire scene or play can be done from that authentic part of the actor.
sub_personality_approach.instrumental_use: Strongly instrumental: calls forth a part of the actor's actual personality that may not normally be accessible or expressed.
sub_personality_approach.craft_use: Can address multiple obligations simultaneously with a single choice. The sub-personality has its own unique intelligence for the material. Particularly powerful for complex characters controlled by a single dominant personality facet.
sub_personality_approach.when_to_use: When the character's behavior can be identified as emanating from a specific sub-personality (the victim, the warrior, the free spirit, the moralist, the demonic, etc.); when conventional approaches have not produced the authentic dimensional reality required.

sources.summary: Sub-Personalities: identify the sub-personality the character runs from; elicit matching one in self; can address all obligations with single choice.
Examples: the victim sub-personality for victim characters; the free spirit for impulsive, irresponsible characters.

creative_visualization_approach.name: Creative Visualization (Choice Approach)
creative_visualization_approach.description: Guiding oneself—eyes closed—into a real or imagined environment supported by all five senses, allowing the unconscious to produce thoughts and impulses that then carry over into the scene.
creative_visualization_approach.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental—but can pique unconscious connections deeply.
creative_visualization_approach.craft_use: Excellent foundation for time-and-place obligation. Can address emotional and other obligations. Later can be done with eyes open ('inner eye'). Always support with sensory engagement—avoid staying in the head.
creative_visualization_approach.when_to_use: When the scene requires a specific place reality (time-and-place obligation) that the actual stage environment cannot provide; when other approaches have not reached the needed emotional state; when the actor wants to transport himself into a vivid imaginary or remembered environment.

sources.summary: Creative Visualization: twenty-second and final choice approach; close eyes, guide into environment, support with five senses.
Addresses all seven major obligations; not to be confused with Affective Memory—entirely different process.

voice_dialogue_sub_personality.name: Voice Dialogue (Sub-Personality Technique)
voice_dialogue_sub_personality.description: A two-person facilitation process in which one actor (the facilitator) asks to speak to specific sub-personalities of the other, who physically moves to accommodate each sub-personality and speaks from that part of himself.
voice_dialogue_sub_personality.instrumental_use: Strongly instrumental: makes accessible previously disowned or suppressed sub-personalities, expanding the range of available emotional and behavioral life.
voice_dialogue_sub_personality.craft_use: Used to identify and call forth the sub-personality that matches the character's controlling personality structure. Facilitator must be non-judgmental and always return the person to an objective ego state after the facilitation.
voice_dialogue_sub_personality.when_to_use: When preparing for a role that requires a personality facet the actor has difficulty accessing; when exploring the range of one's own sub-personalities; when another actor can serve as skilled facilitator.

sources.summary: Voice Dialogue: facilitator talks to specific sub-personalities; person moves to accommodate new personality.
Example facilitation: the angry sub-personality reveals its conflict with the pleaser sub-personality.
Facilitator rules: non-judgmental; always return to ego state; never leave in sub-personality.

emotional_pov_inventory_exercise.name: Emotional-Point-of-View Inventory
emotional_pov_inventory_exercise.goal: Build awareness of real moment-to-moment impulses and train authentic emotional responsiveness to all stimuli.
emotional_pov_inventory_exercise.how_to: Do it out loud—whenever and wherever possible. Ask yourself how you feel about the place, each object, sound, person, and sensation. Answer with real responses—verbal, physical, or both. Move through the environment systematically. Allow whatever responses arise, including 'I don't know.' Express emotions inherent in the responses.
emotional_pov_inventory_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors, especially those who feel emotionally disconnected or who tend to monitor their performance rather than experience it.
emotional_pov_inventory_exercise.why_it_works: Widening the panorama of stimuli actors respond to builds a richer inner life. Stringing unbroken responses together creates the moment-to-moment flow of BEING.
emotional_pov_inventory_exercise.additional_info: Differs from the Emotional-Point-of-View Workout (which is a stream-of-consciousness flow) in that the Inventory is more questioning/cataloguing in nature.

sources.summary: Two purposes: makes actor aware of how objects affect them; builds ability to respond and express all impulses.
Full example of running the inventory in a room, moving through objects, sounds, people.

emotional_pov_nonverbal_exercise.name: Emotional Point of View, Without Words
emotional_pov_nonverbal_exercise.goal: Connect with real organic impulses without the intellectualizing filter of words.
emotional_pov_nonverbal_exercise.how_to: Start with the immediate environment. Let eyes slowly sweep the place. Instead of responding with words, make sounds that come from direct response to objects. Be aware of tendency to be arbitrary with sounds or slide into musical activity unrelated to real feelings. Do for at least 5 minutes.
emotional_pov_nonverbal_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who tend to describe rather than express feelings; those who get overly intellectual with emotional work.
emotional_pov_nonverbal_exercise.why_it_works: Words often take the place of the real expressive fabric of feelings. Non-verbal expression produces greater vocal variety and a more direct connection to impulses.
emotional_pov_nonverbal_exercise.additional_info: Practice daily to establish a bond with real impulses, thereby accomplishing the BEING state.

sources.summary: Translate impulses into unintelligible sounds while scanning environment; avoids intellectualization.

inclusion_exercise.name: Inclusion Exercise
inclusion_exercise.goal: Train the actor to trust and include all impulses during scene work rather than suppressing non-material-consistent ones.
inclusion_exercise.how_to: Use a well-memorized monologue. Recite it out loud and be aware of all impulses. Allow everything thought and felt to express through the words, regardless of whether it fits the material's meaning. Don't concern yourself with fulfilling the meaning of the piece—use it only to practice inclusion. Allow even outrageous impulses to ventilate through the words. Do the monologue several times and check: if it sounds different each time, you're working from real impulses; if sections sound the same, you're servicing a concept.
inclusion_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who suppress impulses during scenes; actors who feel split between their real feelings and what they perform.
inclusion_exercise.why_it_works: Including all impulses creates an unbroken flow of authentic life. Turning liabilities into assets produces an unpredictable, colorful, compelling performance.
inclusion_exercise.additional_info: After practice phase, can use during scene work while also obligating oneself to fulfill the material's emotional obligation.

sources.summary: Use monologue to practice including all impulses through the words; check variation across repetitions.
After practice, apply during scene work while working toward emotional obligation of the material.

inner_outer_monologue_exercise.name: Inner-Outer Monologue
inner_outer_monologue_exercise.goal: Connect the actor's inner organic life with scripted text by alternating between written lines and personal inner monologue.
inner_outer_monologue_exercise.how_to: Use a well-memorized monologue (knowing words well is critical). Say 3-4 lines as written out loud, then switch to personal inner stream of consciousness for equal time. Go back and forth. All content is spoken audibly. Most important: carry over the life from the inner monologue into the written monologue—they must connect.
inner_outer_monologue_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who feel disconnected from lines; actors whose inner life disconnects from the material during performance.
inner_outer_monologue_exercise.why_it_works: Very soon the words someone else wrote start coming from the actor's own inner organic life. Bridges gap between personal reality and scripted text.
inner_outer_monologue_exercise.additional_info: Also a good rehearsal tool—when a scene feels dead, employing this technique almost immediately bridges the gap.

sources.summary: Described in detail; alternating written and personal lines, all spoken aloud.
Example with Gettysburg Address. Key: inner life must carry over into written text. Good rehearsal tool.

being_workout_exercise.name: BEING Workout
being_workout_exercise.goal: Achieve an authentic, organic threshold of life with a scene partner before rehearsing.
being_workout_exercise.how_to: Two actors face each other (standing, sitting, or any position). Start by acknowledging how you feel at this moment. Relate to each other conversationally on a moment-to-moment basis. Express all obstacles (fears, tensions, distractions, concerns) as they arise. Continue until both actors feel comfortable expressing no more or less than they feel.
being_workout_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any two actors preparing to rehearse or perform together.
being_workout_exercise.why_it_works: When fears and concerns are brought into the open, they no longer need to be hidden. Including obstacles creates an unbroken flow of reality that becomes part of the BEING state.
being_workout_exercise.additional_info: Can be done in any position. Facing each other is helpful for addressing obstacles between the two actors directly.

sources.summary: Full description with extended dialogue example showing two actors working through fears, self-consciousness, and connection.

intimate_sharing_exercise.name: Intimate Sharing with Another Actor
intimate_sharing_exercise.goal: Overcome fear of exposure and break through barriers to authentic vulnerability with a scene partner.
intimate_sharing_exercise.how_to: Start by expressing reluctance to say anything. Then slowly verbalize innermost feelings starting from here-and-now. Can be conversational between the two actors or done as a solo declaration. Take emotional responsibility for everything expressed. Continue for as long as an hour or more until impulses flow with much less restriction.
intimate_sharing_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors with strong fear of exposure; any scene partners building trust before intimate or vulnerable scene work.
intimate_sharing_exercise.why_it_works: As vulnerability level climbs, so does impulsivity—a necessary ingredient for organic reality. Bringing private fears into the open eliminates the need to protect them.
intimate_sharing_exercise.additional_info: End result: a level of availability where almost anything the actor works for will easily affect them. Privacy is respected—never force true private material.

sources.summary: How to do Intimate Sharing; example of stream-of-consciousness sharing including fears, loneliness, need for validation.
End result: availability where almost anything will easily affect you; vulnerability and impulsivity rise together.

sounds_with_choice_exercise.name: Sounds with a Choice
sounds_with_choice_exercise.goal: Explore scene choices free from conceptual burden of written dialogue; stimulate fresh emotional colors.
sounds_with_choice_exercise.how_to: Both actors work for their scene choices. Instead of speaking the written lines, communicate through sounds only. Relate to each other on a moment-to-moment basis, responding to impulses through sound. Allow unexpected responses to emerge.
sounds_with_choice_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any pair of actors in rehearsal; especially useful when concept is dominating or emotional life is flat.
sounds_with_choice_exercise.why_it_works: Eliminates concept and premeditation; allows unexpected impulses to fill the scene with emotional colors not accessible through text.

sources.summary: Sounds with Choice: replace dialogue with sounds while working for choices; eliminates concept and premeditation.

gibberish_exercise.name: Gibberish with a Choice
gibberish_exercise.goal: Free actors from written-word constraints while maintaining sentence-like expression and moment-to-moment reality.
gibberish_exercise.how_to: Both actors work for their scene choices and relate using gibberish—abstract syllables that sound like an unknown language. Respond to each other's gibberish on a moment-to-moment basis.
gibberish_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any pair of actors in rehearsal; particularly as a middle step between sounds and scripted dialogue.
gibberish_exercise.why_it_works: More like talking than making sounds; preserves the feeling of language/sentence structure while removing semantic meaning. Eliminates concept without removing the communicative feeling.

sources.summary: Gibberish with Choice: abstract words/sentences with no meaning; good interim step between sounds and written dialogue.

paraphrasing_exercise.name: Moment-to-Moment Paraphrasing Improvisation
paraphrasing_exercise.goal: Explore scene's emotional and relational content using personal words rather than scripted text, building impulse-based behavior.
paraphrasing_exercise.how_to: Actors do whatever preparations they would ordinarily do (choices, etc.). Then paraphrase the scene using own words—NOT verbatim, but paralleling the circumstance and essence of the material. Express all personal feelings within the framework of the material. After a satisfying exploration, return to the written script carrying the irreverent quality.
paraphrasing_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any pair of actors in rehearsal who feel trapped in their heads or disconnected from the scene's emotional life.
paraphrasing_exercise.why_it_works: Non-verbatim paraphrasing prevents premeditation of both words and behavior. Allows actors to express personal feelings in the framework of the material.
paraphrasing_exercise.additional_info: Verbatim paraphrasing creates another trap—thinking of each line then coming up with suitable paraphrase. Must be non-verbatim.

sources.summary: How to paraphrase and why it must be non-verbatim.
Extended paraphrasing example showing actors staying with scene circumstances while using personal emotional life.

nursery_rhyme_exercise.name: Nursery Rhymes Exercise
nursery_rhyme_exercise.goal: Free actors from concept and line-reading habits by replacing dialogue with rhymes; transfer resulting impulsive freedom to the written scene.
nursery_rhyme_exercise.how_to: Choose any nursery rhyme or poem. Both actors use the nursery rhyme lines instead of written dialogue while working for their choices. Critically: avoid falling into the meter of the rhyme, which would reproduce the original trap of servicing text. Allow impulses to express through the rhyme freely. Return to the script after.
nursery_rhyme_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who are too closely tied to line readings or specific emotional concepts of a scene.
nursery_rhyme_exercise.why_it_works: Transfers emphasis from word-meaning to impulses; encourages irreverence and wider emotional palette. When actors return to script they feel more impulsive.
nursery_rhyme_exercise.additional_info: Must avoid falling into rhyme's meter—that would again service text rather than impulses.

sources.summary: Nursery rhymes replace dialogue; avoid meter trap; transfers emphasis from word-meaning to impulses.

antithetical_exploration_exercise.name: Antithetical Explorations
antithetical_exploration_exercise.goal: Discover complex, multi-faceted character behavior by exploring the opposite of what the character overtly seems to be.
antithetical_exploration_exercise.how_to: Identify the character's most obvious quality (e.g., anger, violence). For a rehearsal period, explore the opposite: the loving, tender, vulnerable impulses. Work for choices that stimulate those antithetical qualities. Then find organic reasons within the play's circumstances to be rejected or discounted, building the character's actual behavior from that organic frustration.
antithetical_exploration_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors playing one-dimensional or obviously typed characters; anyone whose character work feels superficial.
antithetical_exploration_exercise.why_it_works: Builds organic foundation for the character's emotional life rather than expressing the emotion directly. Creates impetus from which the behavior comes.
antithetical_exploration_exercise.additional_info: Not just about exploring opposite behavior—about finding the internal impetus that generates the character's overt behavior.

sources.summary: Antithetical exploration for angry character: explore loving impulses first.
Actor then finds organic reasons to be rejected, building resentments organically from the stimulated loving impulses.

personal_inventory_exercise_craft.name: Personal Inventory (Craft Use)
personal_inventory_exercise_craft.goal: Reconnect to internal reality during a scene or performance when feeling wooden, disconnected, or mechanically reciting lines.
personal_inventory_exercise_craft.how_to: In performance or rehearsal: silently ask 'How do I feel?' and respond. Include the responses—express them through the behavior and words of the scene or run them as a silent Inner Monologue. Continue the Personal Inventory as an ongoing Inner Monologue until the connection to internal reality is restored. Then reinvest in the original choice.
personal_inventory_exercise_craft.who_should_do_it: Any actor who feels disconnected, mechanical, or premeditative during scene work or performance.
personal_inventory_exercise_craft.why_it_works: Asking 'How do I feel?' and responding honestly restimulates impulses that premeditation has silenced. Including the real responses—even uncomfortable ones—establishes authentic emotional point of view.
personal_inventory_exercise_craft.additional_info: When used in performance, responses are silent or expressed through the scene; in exercise/practice, responses are audible.

sources.summary: Craft use: done before first line and during scene when losing connection to internal reality.
Example of Personal Inventory as Inner Monologue mid-scene; including uncomfortable feelings restores authentic connection.

disconnected_impulsivity_exercise.name: Disconnected Impulsivity Exercise
disconnected_impulsivity_exercise.goal: Eliminate premeditation and restore organic impulsive responsiveness in a scene when other techniques have failed.
disconnected_impulsivity_exercise.how_to: While working in a scene, become aware of real moment-to-moment impulses. Encourage their expression under the lines, between lines, or while listening to the other actor. The impulses can range from logical responses to completely unrelated thoughts—they don't need to connect. Run internally; the expression shows subtly in behavior and response quality. Use only for as long as needed.
disconnected_impulsivity_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who have repeatedly lapsed into led/premeditated behavior despite trying Inner Monologue and Personal Inventory.
disconnected_impulsivity_exercise.why_it_works: The disconnected, non-logical nature of the impulse stream prevents the intellect from grabbing onto any thread and premediting. Generates openness and energy that excites relationships.
disconnected_impulsivity_exercise.additional_info: Should not be used indiscriminately—it's a problem solver. Goal is to reach ensemble-level involvement with the other character.

sources.summary: Disconnected Impulsivity: encourage unrelated impulses under/through lines.
Example of impulse stream in action; from outside looks like natural environmental responsiveness.

wonder_perceive_observe_exercise.name: Wonder, Perceive, and Observe Exercise
wonder_perceive_observe_exercise.goal: Build genuine involvement with and curiosity about the scene partner, establishing the foundation of authentic relationship.
wonder_perceive_observe_exercise.how_to: Observe: look for obvious things about the other actor—how they're dressed, what they're holding, how they seem to feel. Go deeper in observing: features, eye colors, hair, way of moving head, eye shadow, ear coverage, etc. Perceive: look even deeper, challenge powers of observation, read emotional states, behavioral patterns, underlife. Wonder: encourage curious questions about the other person—personal life, thoughts, desires. Go back and forth between observing, perceiving, and wondering. Can be done silently (as inner monologue) or out loud with the other actor.
wonder_perceive_observe_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any pair of actors before scene work; especially when relationship feels artificial or curiosity about the partner is low.
wonder_perceive_observe_exercise.why_it_works: Genuine curiosity creates real involvement; can be run silently throughout an entire scene without anyone knowing. Establishes the foundation of authentic relationship.
wonder_perceive_observe_exercise.additional_info: Doing it silently sometimes excites more real curiosity than doing it out loud with the partner.

sources.summary: Three-part exercise: observe, perceive, wonder about the other actor.
Extended example of perceiving the other actor.

sense_memory_impacting_objects_exercise.name: Sense Memory with Emotionally Impacting Objects
sense_memory_impacting_objects_exercise.goal: Elevate emotional state to a desired level by sensorially recreating a meaningful object or person.
sense_memory_impacting_objects_exercise.how_to: Choose an emotionally impacting object (person, pet, photograph, letter, music, sound, place). Ask sensory questions about the object across all relevant senses—visual, tactile, olfactory, auditory, taste. Respond sensorially to each question. Continue through multiple senses. As emotional responses arise naturally during the sensory creation, encourage and express them. Do this before a scene or silently while waiting on set.
sense_memory_impacting_objects_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor needing emotional preparation before a scene; especially useful when silence is required.
sense_memory_impacting_objects_exercise.why_it_works: Sense Memory can be done silently without calling attention to itself. The sensory recreation triggers authentic emotional associations.
sense_memory_impacting_objects_exercise.additional_info: Can be done while waiting for a crew setup or while on stage before the curtain rises. The more expertise with Sense Memory, the faster the results.

sources.summary: Sense Memory with impacting objects is silent, unobtrusive; can be done while waiting on set.
Detailed example: creating father in chair using visual, tactile, olfactory questions.

abandonment_exercise.name: Abandonment
abandonment_exercise.goal: Purge tension instrumentally or build a foundation of volatile emotional life for a character.
abandonment_exercise.how_to: Lie on the floor. Have an all-out tantrum—move body in all directions with no physical interference or leadership. Scream and make huge uninterrupted vocal expurgations simultaneously. Instrumental use: continue until tension is purged. Craft use: choose specific content for the tantrum that relates to the emotional state needed; continue until the desired emotional state is stimulated.
abandonment_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who carry significant physical tension; actors playing highly volatile, irrational characters.
abandonment_exercise.why_it_works: The unrestricted physical and vocal expression purges conglomerated blocked emotion and stimulates the free flow of impulses.
abandonment_exercise.additional_info: Do it however long it takes. For craft use, must have specific goal: what emotional state to stimulate and what content to use.

sources.summary: Abandonment: all-out tantrum on floor with body and voice; dual instrumental/craft use.

go_crazy_exercise.name: Go Crazy
go_crazy_exercise.goal: Free the actor from restrictions, stimulate unbroken impulse flow, and build a foundation of flamboyant, colorful behavior.
go_crazy_exercise.how_to: Start with large commitment. Go from moment to moment with every impulse. Make sounds, say words, jump around, roll on the ground. Encourage all outrageous thoughts and impulses toward expression. Relate to things and people in the room—mix external and internal stimuli. Go from serious to absurd rapidly. Continue for 2-5 minutes or until on a good roll.
go_crazy_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who feel restricted or inhibited; actors preparing for free-flowing, expressive, or uninhibited characters.
go_crazy_exercise.why_it_works: The unstructured, exaggerated nature frees all restrictions and creates an unbroken impulse flow that carries into subsequent work.
go_crazy_exercise.additional_info: Must be large and exaggerated. The exercise doesn't have to make any sense.

sources.summary: Go Crazy: all-out exaggerated impulsive expression; 2-5 minutes; frees restrictions and creates unbroken impulse flow.

two_person_validations_exercise.name: Two-Person Validations
two_person_validations_exercise.goal: Build mutual positive regard and ego support between scene partners before rehearsing or performing together.
two_person_validations_exercise.how_to: Two actors sit together (or do it while making up or getting dressed). Conversationally share honest positive observations about each other. Selectively emphasize good things—not forced or structured. Be authentic; don't exaggerate or make things up. Continue for as long as needed.
two_person_validations_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any scene partners before a rehearsal or performance; especially useful when the relationship has become distant, competitive, or fraught.
two_person_validations_exercise.why_it_works: Based on truth with selective emphasis; genuine positive regard is stimulated by authentic positive observation. Works even better when observations relate to the material.
two_person_validations_exercise.additional_info: Can be done while making up or getting dressed—does not require special time allocation.

sources.summary: Two-Person Validations: honest positive observations; based on truth; conversational; do as long as needed.

feedback_exercise.name: Feedback
feedback_exercise.goal: Build authentic mutual relationship investment between scene partners through honest, important questioning.
feedback_exercise.how_to: Two actors sit together and ask each other important questions conversationally. Questions should relate to genuinely meaningful things: first impressions, what you find interesting about the other person, what you get from them in the work, what you wish were different. If courageous, go after the nucleus of your curiosity. The exercise can continue for as long as needed.
feedback_exercise.who_should_do_it: Scene partners who don't know each other well; actors whose relationship to each other is not generating the needed relational feelings.
feedback_exercise.why_it_works: Genuine courageous curiosity builds real relationship. The authenticity of the exchange creates investment that stands up to material obligations.
feedback_exercise.additional_info: Don't waste time asking for unimportant feedback. Courage to go to the nucleus of curiosity determines the quality of the relationship built.

sources.summary: Feedback: conversational exchange of important questions; extended example shows depth and honesty possible.

parallel_improvising_exercise.name: Parallel Improvising
parallel_improvising_exercise.goal: Find authentic personal connection to material's emotional content by improvising a personally parallel situation.
parallel_improvising_exercise.how_to: Identify the emotional elements of the scene. Choose a parallel circumstance from your own life with similar emotional content. The parallel doesn't have to involve the same person as the scene (e.g., don't have to use your mother—use anyone with a similar emotional relationship). Improvise the parallel situation with complete freedom and irreverence, going wherever it leads. Include all inner monologue impulses during the improvisation. After the improvisation, return to the written scene with the emotional connection established.
parallel_improvising_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who feel disconnected from the emotional content of a scene; actors who can't find personal relevance in the material.
parallel_improvising_exercise.why_it_works: Personal parallels stimulate authentic emotional responses that can then be carried into the written text.
parallel_improvising_exercise.additional_info: Encourage total freedom to go wherever the improvisation takes you; complete irreverence to the written material.

sources.summary: Parallel improvising: personal parallel to scene's emotional elements; example from Glass Menagerie.

task_inventory_exercise.name: Task Inventory
task_inventory_exercise.goal: Find the right choice for a specific obligation when stuck or unable to identify what to use.
task_inventory_exercise.how_to: 1. Identify the obligation, making it very clear to yourself. 2. Restate the obligation in complete descriptive language. 3. Ask yourself if you have ever felt that way. 4. Remember when you felt that way. 5. What was happening when you felt that way? 6. What specifically stimulated the feeling? 7. What was the common denominator between various experiences of that feeling? 8. Find the experience that most closely mirrors what you need. 9. Specify the choice. 10. Explore the translation of that choice into a choice approach. FAIL-SAFE (Step 11): If all else fails, ask: 'What would make me feel that way if it existed here right now? How can I create it?' FOOTNOTE: First ask if anything in the existing environment will already make you feel what you need.
task_inventory_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor who is stuck when trying to find choices for a scene obligation. Practice it even when not needed to become facile with it.
task_inventory_exercise.why_it_works: Systematic approach eliminates confusion and guesswork; identifies common denominators that point directly to the most effective choices and approaches.
task_inventory_exercise.additional_info: With experience, can be done quickly even in the limited time of film work. The Fail-Safe Device opens the entire world—both reality and fantasy.

sources.summary: Ten steps plus Fail-Safe Device.
Fail-Safe Device.
Extended example with two experiences and common denominator identification.

choice_hunt_exercise.name: Choice Hunt
choice_hunt_exercise.goal: Discover meaningful personal experiences from one's history that have strong emotional potential for use as choices.
choice_hunt_exercise.how_to: Lie down on your back in solitude with eyes closed. Go back in time to meaningful and emotional events: graduations, holidays, weddings, picnics, family events, significant encounters. Allow memories to surface naturally. Encourage the expression of all emotional impulses stimulated by re-creating the experiences. With regular practice, you will begin to remember things spontaneously even when not doing the exercise.
choice_hunt_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who feel their choice repertoire is limited; actors who have difficulty finding personally relevant choices for obligations.
choice_hunt_exercise.why_it_works: Memory releases its secrets gradually with regular practice; generates surprising impulses and emotional responses to experiences not consciously remembered.
choice_hunt_exercise.additional_info: Regular practice causes memory to release spontaneously even outside the exercise.

sources.summary: Choice Hunt: lying down in solitude; revisit meaningful events; surprising impulses generated; memory begins releasing spontaneously.

sense_memory_exercise_detailed.name: Sense Memory (Full Process)
sense_memory_exercise_detailed.goal: Create an absent object, person, or place so fully that the actor can actually see, hear, smell, feel, and taste it—making it real enough to stimulate authentic emotional responses.
sense_memory_exercise_detailed.how_to: Begin with the first sensory question directed to a specific part of one specific sense. For example (visual): 'What color are the walls of this place? How does the light affect the color?' Respond through the sense—actually attempt to see the answer, not just think it. Ask many questions per sense area (can be 50+). Leave and return to a sense as needed. Work all five senses: visual, tactile, olfactory, auditory, gustatory. Go back and forth between senses while sustaining previously created sensory impressions. The process continues during scene work—between lines and while listening to other actor.
sense_memory_exercise_detailed.who_should_do_it: All actors as a daily practice; anyone using Sense Memory as a choice approach in scene work.
sense_memory_exercise_detailed.why_it_works: The senses can be trained through daily practice to respond to absent objects as if present. Not an intellectual but a response process.
sense_memory_exercise_detailed.additional_info: Daily practice is essential—the ability to truly see/feel/smell absent objects comes with sustained practice over time, not immediately.

sources.summary: Sense Memory as choice approach; senses trained to respond to absent objects.
Detailed process with example questions.
Further example with olfactory and visual questions.
Process continues during scene; actor still hears and is affected by other actor.

available_stimulus_sweep_exercise_craft.name: Available Stimulus Exploration (Craft Use)
available_stimulus_sweep_exercise_craft.goal: Identify which elements in the actual environment can be used to fulfill scene obligations before resorting to other choice approaches.
available_stimulus_sweep_exercise_craft.how_to: Go through a systematic checklist of questions: How am I affected by the whole place? As I scan the environment, what affects or changes my emotional point of view? Are there sounds or odors that mean anything to me? What about the furnishings, colors, arrangement, light? How do I feel about individual people collectively? Individually? Do any lines or words in the play mean something to me? Does anything in the play, the actors, or the place remind me of something in my life? Use Inner Monologue to intensify whatever Available Stimulus affects you.
available_stimulus_sweep_exercise_craft.who_should_do_it: Any actor approaching a scene, before selecting other choice approaches.
available_stimulus_sweep_exercise_craft.why_it_works: Available Stimulus is the most efficiently accessible approach—when it works, it requires the least additional craft work. What is already there and real doesn't need to be created.
available_stimulus_sweep_exercise_craft.additional_info: Checklist can be expanded as the actor works with the approach. Sometimes a specific element needs Creative Manipulation to be fully useful.

sources.summary: Systematic checklist for Available Stimulus exploration.
Creative Manipulation of available elements to make them serve the scene.

believability_improvisation_exercise.name: Believability Improvisation
believability_improvisation_exercise.goal: Create a fabric of total reality between two actors by structuring a shared improvisation that mixes truth and appropriately chosen untruth.
believability_improvisation_exercise.how_to: Both actors identify the obligations and conflict elements of the scene. Begin an improvisation—possibly in a parallel circumstance—starting with actual truths from both actors' lives. Selectively emphasize the truths most relevant to the scene's needs. Progressively add untrue elements that serve the scene's obligations. Mix truth and untruth deftly until they become indistinguishable. Continue until both actors have created the emotional reality the scene requires.
believability_improvisation_exercise.who_should_do_it: Scene partners preparing for a scene where the actual relationship between them doesn't yet have the emotional depth required by the material.
believability_improvisation_exercise.why_it_works: When truth and untruth are skillfully mixed, the actors cannot distinguish between them—creating authentic emotional engagement with fictional circumstances.
believability_improvisation_exercise.additional_info: One of the most enjoyable choice approaches. The improvisational framework allows organic exploration before returning to the written text.

sources.summary: Both actors identify obligations, structure Believability improvisation together.
Extended example: two actresses mixing real life truths with carefully chosen untruths.

endowment_process_exercise.name: The Endowment Process (Sensory Exercise)
endowment_process_exercise.goal: Transform the perceived qualities of the other actor or an object sensorially, so that the actor responds to a changed stimulus that fulfills the scene's obligations.
endowment_process_exercise.how_to: Begin with the available actor or object as it really is. Ask sensory questions directed at the desired change, responding with the appropriate sense: eyes (color, clouding, blood vessels), skin (texture, color, temperature), hair (texture, hairline), posture (tremor, head position, shoulders), voice (sound, placement, word formation). For endowing another actor with a specific person's features: first identify similarities between the actress's face/body and the imaginary person's, then identify differences and sensorially begin to mold the actress's features toward the imaginary person. Continue asking hundreds of sensory questions across all sensory modalities until the imaginary person's features begin to overlay the real actor. As you succeed and become emotionally affected by the changes, the nature of the sensory questions changes organically.
endowment_process_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor who needs to feel a fundamentally different relationship to the scene partner than what the actual person provides; or who needs to relate to a prop or set element as something it isn't.
endowment_process_exercise.why_it_works: The Sense Memory process, when applied to an already-existing object or person, begins to shift the actor's perception. The conglomerate stimulus (real actor + sensory endowment) becomes the living embodiment of the choice.
endowment_process_exercise.additional_info: One choice and one choice approach can address two obligations simultaneously (relationship + emotional). The process can go on as long as it takes to complete the transformation.

sources.summary: Endowment process: detailed sensory questions to age a healthy actor.
As success comes, the nature of sensory questions changes; can address two obligations with one choice.
Endowing actress with wife's features: start with similarities, use contrasts, build sensorially across face, smell, body, voice, rhythm.

animal_externals_exercise.name: Animal Externals (Approach Exercise)
animal_externals_exercise.goal: Absorb the sense of a specific animal through the actor's own physical instrument, then translate that sense into human behavior for use in a role.
animal_externals_exercise.how_to: Find a place where you can observe a specific animal for long periods (zoo, wildlife, video study). Step 1—Overall rhythm: watch and practice the animal's rhythm; take it home and repeat; return and refine. Step 2—Leading center: identify what part of the animal crosses an imaginary line first; apply to your own body. Step 3—Secondary center: find the part that closely follows the leading center; put all three together. Step 4—Limitations: study and add what the animal cannot do; adapt to your body. Step 5—Centers of weight, balance, and power: identify and add to exploration. Step 6—Isolations: practice independent movement of body parts; break animal down and reassemble. Step 7—Corresponding and contrasting rhythms: identify parts moving in harmony vs. counterrhythm; work separately, then together. Step 8—Species and individual mannerisms: species mannerisms first; then individual. Add tempo (speed of rhythm) once secure with rhythms. Translation to human behavior: from all-fours to crouching to standing, retaining all elements, making them progressively more subtle. Sounds: explore where sounds originate in the animal's body; reproduce them from the same body location.
animal_externals_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor confronting a character requiring physical attitudes, primal impulses, or behavioral qualities not accessible through other means.
animal_externals_exercise.why_it_works: Approaching inner life from the outside in piques the unconscious. The body leads, and the thoughts, impulses, and emotional life follow the physical instrument's transformation.
animal_externals_exercise.additional_info: Time frame is entirely up to the actor. In public rehearsals, bring only the translated (human) form; in private work, use the full animal position. 'The work or process of a master craftsman is undetectable except to the eyes of an expert.'

sources.summary: Approach technique checklist: eight elements.
Step-by-step: rhythm home; add leading center; add secondary center; practice systematically.
Summary of full system; tempo added last; time frame up to actor.
Full checklist; species mannerisms first; individual mannerisms refine.
Translation to human behavior: degrees from all-fours to standing; inner sense goal.

imaginary_monologue_combined_exercise.name: Imaginary Monologue Combined with Sense Memory
imaginary_monologue_combined_exercise.goal: Dimensionalize an Imaginary Monologue by adding sensory questions about the imaginary person in between the words, allowing the person to appear to respond and creating unpredictability in the expression.
imaginary_monologue_combined_exercise.how_to: Begin establishing the imaginary person sensorially: shape of face, color of eyes, distance, posture. Then begin the monologue, pausing at natural points to ask sensory questions about what the person is doing or showing in response to what was just said. Example sequence: 'How far away is he standing?...How tall is he?...What is the shape of his face?...' then begin words, then mid-sentence ask 'What is his mouth like?...How is he looking at me as I talk to him?' then continue words. The sensory questions can begin before the monologue words and continue after the monologue ends. Allow the imaginary person's responses to genuinely affect what is said next.
imaginary_monologue_combined_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor using Imaginary Monologue as a choice approach who wants to deepen the reality and create genuine unpredictability.
imaginary_monologue_combined_exercise.why_it_works: Seeing the person you are talking to allows for their responses to be perceived—the imaginary person becomes more real and more reactive, producing less predictable and more organic emotional expression.
imaginary_monologue_combined_exercise.additional_info: A very natural combination: the two approaches support each other rather than interfering. Sense Memory part can start before the monologue and continue after it ends.

sources.summary: Combined Imaginary Monologue + Sense Memory: sensory questions interspersed; allows imaginary person to 'respond.'
Example showing alternating pattern of sensory questions and monologue words; both done simultaneously.

illnesses_restrictions_exercise.name: Illnesses and Restrictions — Creating a Headache
illnesses_restrictions_exercise.goal: Sensorially create a physical sensation (headache, bodily pain, or restriction) that organically produces the character behavior required by the scene.
illnesses_restrictions_exercise.how_to: Direct sensory attention to the affected body part and ask sensory questions responded to in that area: What do I feel in my forehead?...How high above my eyebrows?...Where do I feel the most?...How far does that feeling reach?...The right side?...The left?...How high up the head?...Around the sides?...At the back?...As I slowly move my head, what do I experience?...When I close my eyes, what happens?...Where does the pain seem to come from?...How does it change as it moves away from that point?...As I move, what happens in my head?...How do the sounds in this room affect the feeling?...What sounds particularly affect the pain?...As I bend forward, backward, what happens? For restrictions (body brace, handcuffs, neck brace): ask questions about the presence of the object—where does it touch the body, what is the texture and temperature, how much movement is prevented, what does resistance feel like.
illnesses_restrictions_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor required to play illness, pain, physical restriction, or any emotional state that a physical restriction would organically produce (irritability, fear, panic, helplessness).
illnesses_restrictions_exercise.why_it_works: The base reality (real sensory experience of pain or restriction) produces all the organic behavior that results. The actor doesn't communicate pain—he experiences it, and the behavior follows authentically.
illnesses_restrictions_exercise.additional_info: Works for any kind of physical restriction. A person in real pain behaves differently even in voice quality. The sensory process is the same as any Sense Memory work—the questions aim at the relevant body part.

sources.summary: Headache Sense Memory process: detailed questions about forehead, sides, back of head, movement, sounds.
Creating a neck brace: questions about pressure, texture, temperature, range of motion restriction.

creative_visualization_exercise.name: Creative Visualization Exercise
creative_visualization_exercise.goal: Establish a time-and-place reality and/or emotional state by guiding oneself into a specific imagined or remembered environment, allowing the unconscious to produce life that carries into the scene.
creative_visualization_exercise.how_to: Identify the scene's obligation (especially time-and-place and emotional obligations). Select a place—real or imagined—designed to stimulate the needed feelings and relationship to place. Close your eyes. Begin to visualize that environment: its colors, objects, sounds, smells. Guide yourself through the visualization like moving through a dream, from one element or event to another. Support the visualization at every point with all five senses—avoid intellectual processing. Allow yourself to get the sense of being in that place. As the unconscious begins to function, let thoughts and impulses emerge. When you feel ready, open your eyes and begin the scene, relating to the other actor and saying the lines from whatever emotional space you are in. The carryover is usually substantial. If needed, supplement with additional choices related to the environment. Advanced practice: attempt the process with eyes open, through the 'inner eye.'
creative_visualization_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor confronting a time-and-place obligation where the stage environment is insufficient; any actor who needs to transport themselves to a specific emotional reality through place.
creative_visualization_exercise.why_it_works: When the unconscious is engaged through vivid sensory visualization, it produces authentic thoughts and impulses that carry into the scene—not manufactured emotions but organic responses stimulated by the actor's genuine connection to the imagined environment.
creative_visualization_exercise.additional_info: When we were children we did this for play. It is also very relaxing and fulfilling as an adventure in its own right. Do not confuse with Affective Memory—it is an entirely different, more imaginative process.

sources.summary: Creative Visualization: guide through environment like a dream; allow unconscious to produce thoughts/impulses; carry over into scene.
Support with all five senses; never stay in the head; can do with inner eye once facile.

personal_voice_dialogue_exercise.name: Personal Voice Dialogue (Self-Facilitated Sub-Personality)
personal_voice_dialogue_exercise.goal: Call forth a specific sub-personality by speaking to it directly, allowing it to come forward and take over the actor's behavior for the scene.
personal_voice_dialogue_exercise.how_to: From a clear, objective, ego-state place of consciousness, identify the sub-personality you wish to reach. Speak to that part directly: 'I would like to hear from my [pusher/victim/free spirit/moralist, etc.]...Are you around?' Physically move over a foot or two to accommodate that part of yourself. Allow the sub-personality to respond and speak from its own point of view. Listen to what it has to say. If it does not respond immediately, continue trying—more coaxing or manipulation may be needed. Once you feel the sub-personality is present and in position—that it has taken control—begin the rehearsal or performance. Alternatively: identify the specific circumstances, stimuli, or conditions that naturally pique that sub-personality in your life, and create or relate to those conditions.
personal_voice_dialogue_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor who wants to call forth a specific sub-personality for a role and does not have a partner to facilitate Voice Dialogue.
