personal_voice_dialogue_exercise.why_it_works: Speaking directly to the sub-personality from an objective place, and physically accommodating its response, creates a genuine internal shift rather than an external imitation. The sub-personality has its own unique intelligence for the material.
personal_voice_dialogue_exercise.additional_info: Success depends on approaching from an objective, conscious place. If another sub-personality in conflict with the desired one is in the driver's seat, the attempt may fail—resolve that conflict first. 'Be courageous in your exploration of this choice approach.'

sources.summary: Personal Voice Dialogue: actor calls on sub-personality alone; moves to accommodate when it responds.
Pusher example: responds immediately when called.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels disconnected from real feelings or is 'naturally' acting—imitating truth rather than experiencing it
recommendations.recommendation: Begin daily practice of the Emotional-Point-of-View Inventory to build genuine moment-to-moment responsiveness and reconnect with authentic impulses.

sources.summary: Daily use of inventory promotes BEING and is the starting place for the craft process.

recommendations.condition: Actor intellectualizes emotions or describes feelings rather than expressing them
recommendations.recommendation: Practice the Emotional Point of View, Without Words (Nonverbal) exercise daily. Bypass verbal processing entirely to access real impulse fabric.

sources.summary: Nonverbal version prevents intellectualization; words often describe rather than express feelings.

recommendations.condition: Actor notices split between what they feel and what they perform; suppresses non-scene-consistent impulses
recommendations.recommendation: Practice the Inclusion Exercise with a memorized monologue. Allow all impulses to ventilate through the text without concern for fulfilling meaning.

sources.summary: Refusing to include real impulses creates short-circuiting; inclusion creates compelling, unpredictable performance.

recommendations.condition: Actor's inner life disconnects from scripted text during scene work
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Inner-Outer Monologue technique in rehearsal: alternate between written lines and personal inner monologue, ensuring the inner life carries over into the written text.

sources.summary: Inner-Outer Monologue bridges gap between personal reality and scripted text; good rehearsal tool.

recommendations.condition: Two actors preparing to rehearse a scene together; both feel artificial or disconnected
recommendations.recommendation: Begin with the BEING Workout: face each other and express all obstacles, fears, and concerns on a moment-to-moment basis until both feel genuinely comfortable.

sources.summary: BEING Workout is recommended first step when rehearsing with another actor; achieves threshold of real life.

recommendations.condition: Actor struggles with fear of exposure or inability to be genuinely vulnerable on stage
recommendations.recommendation: Practice Intimate Sharing with another actor. Sharing personal material addresses the fear of exposure that blocks BEING freedom.

sources.summary: Fear of exposure is major BEING block; Intimate Sharing exercise directly addresses it.

recommendations.condition: Actor is falling into result consciousness—going for the emotion of a scene rather than working from the moment
recommendations.recommendation: Switch from DOING consciousness to BEING consciousness. Start from a real state of life, work for a choice that genuinely affects you, and irreverently follow impulses wherever they lead. Trust that the natural human response will occur.

sources.summary: Antidote to result consciousness: start from real state of life, work for a real choice, allow natural response.

recommendations.condition: Actor resists the irreverent approach because of fear of judgment from directors, peers, or the conventional acting community
recommendations.recommendation: Use ego preparations (Making My Statement, Due-Taking, Feeling Special) before rehearsal. Communicate your process to the director. Stand your ground—process is worth fighting for.

sources.summary: Stand your ground with process even under criticism; communicate approach to director.
Ego preparations help establish right to work irreverently.

recommendations.condition: Actor is too tied to line readings or the conceptual meaning of the written text in rehearsal
recommendations.recommendation: Use rehearsal irreverence techniques: Sounds with a Choice, Gibberish, Paraphrasing Improvisation, or Nursery Rhymes. These techniques break the grip of text-concept and allow impulse-based exploration.

sources.summary: These techniques eliminate concept and premeditation during rehearsal exploration.

recommendations.condition: Actor's impulsive flow is interrupted during a long run or performance; self-consciousness creeping in
recommendations.recommendation: Immediately use Personal Inventory as an Inner Monologue under the lines of the scene. Once organic groove is restored, reinvest in the original choice.

sources.summary: Personal Inventory run as Inner Monologue is the antidote when impulsive flow is interrupted in performance.
After regaining organic groove, reinvest in original choice.

recommendations.condition: A choice has lost its strength after repeated use in a long run
recommendations.recommendation: Add fresh sensory elements to the existing choice: new, emotionally and sensorially exciting questions. This can be done during performance itself since the basic choice remains the same.

sources.summary: When choices lose strength, add fresh sensory elements; can be done during performance.

recommendations.condition: Actor is playing a one-dimensional character or character work feels superficial—expressing the emotion rather than creating the impetus
recommendations.recommendation: Use Antithetical Explorations: spend rehearsal time exploring the character's opposite qualities, stimulate organic loving/tender impulses, then find organic reasons within the scene to be rejected so resentments and the character's behavior build authentically.

sources.summary: Antithetical Explorations discover underlife and fragment concepts; avoid superficial behavior.
Build character's actual behavior from organic frustration of antithetical impulses.

recommendations.condition: Actor questions whether including an impulse in performance is appropriate or misinterprets the material
recommendations.recommendation: Apply the irreverence test: 'Is it not possible for the character to behave or respond in that way at that moment?' If yes, it is permissible. It is an extremely rare occurrence when the answer would be no.

sources.summary: The test for inclusion in performance: ask whether the character could possibly behave that way.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels tension or self-consciousness before or during a scene
recommendations.recommendation: Begin with the Standard Relaxation Cluster: Weight-and-Gravity Relaxation, Deep Breathing, Stretching and Yawning. Follow with Personal Inventory. Involvement is the natural enemy of tension—use Available-Stimulus Sweep or Wonder/Perceive/Observe.

sources.summary: Relaxation is the starting point; the only place from which an artist can create.
Involvement is the natural enemy of tension and self-consciousness.

recommendations.condition: Actor is disconnected from their scene partner; relationship feels artificial or the actor is not genuinely curious about them
recommendations.recommendation: Use Wonder, Perceive, and Observe—ideally before the scene, but it can be run silently throughout. Also consider Sensory Exploration aligned with the first emotional obligation of the scene.

sources.summary: Wonder/Perceive/Observe takes actor into the other person and establishes foundation of relationship.
Sensory Exploration used as involvement prelude to first choice in scene.

recommendations.condition: Actor's senses are sluggish and unresponsive during sensory choice work
recommendations.recommendation: Intersperse Sensitizing while working for the choice in the scene. 'Live' in the unresponsive sense with concentrated attention until it tingles with responsiveness, then reinvest in the sensory questions for the original choice.

sources.summary: Sensitizing used when senses are sluggish during choice exploration; intersperse it while actively working for the choice.

recommendations.condition: Actor's ego state is significantly lower than the character's—character is supremely confident, powerful, or dominant
recommendations.recommendation: Use Superman or Joy-and-Euphoria as a craft preparation. Commit to the exercise at the extreme. Continue until in a significantly different ego state closer to the character's.

sources.summary: Superman for large ego obligations; Joy and Euphoria stretches instrument to expressive extreme.

recommendations.condition: Actor is hard on themselves and struggling with self-criticism; needs to build self-appreciation to match character's positive self-view
recommendations.recommendation: Use Counting Your Blessings or Progress Reports as a craft preparation. Must be based on genuine truth—don't embellish. Follow with Two-Person Validations with scene partner.

sources.summary: Counting Your Blessings: good for actors hard on themselves.
Progress Reports: must not embellish reality; must be genuinely believed.

recommendations.condition: The character is emotionally extreme (volatile, irrational, rageful) and the actor needs a large emotional foundation
recommendations.recommendation: Use large expurgatives: Abandonment (with specific content for the character's emotional life), Vesuvius (with content aimed at building needed threshold), or Go Crazy (to free all restrictions). For underlying anger covered by controlled exterior, use Anger-and-Rage Expression first, then find organic reason to cover it.

sources.summary: Large expurgatives for large emotional obligations; not for subtle levels.
Anger-and-Rage: create underlife of anger first, then find organic justification to cover it.

recommendations.condition: Actor's relationship with scene partner feels artificial, uninvested, or routine; the emotional relationship demanded by material is not being felt
recommendations.recommendation: Use relationship preparations: Nonverbal Relating Exercise, Wonder/Perceive/Observe (adjusted for the specific relationship quality needed), Looking for New Things in the Partner, or Two-Person Validations. Choose based on what specific relational quality is missing.

sources.summary: Nonverbal communication removes word-barrier and deepens direct emotional contact.
Wonder/Perceive/Observe for relationship: adjust to promote specific relationship emotional point of view needed.

recommendations.condition: Actor is working on a scene and needs to identify what to work on—which obligations are most pressing
recommendations.recommendation: Systematically check all seven obligations: Time-and-Place, Relationship, Emotional, Character, Historic, Thematic, Subtextual. Identify which ones are not yet being felt organically. Prioritize based on what the material's most impelling element is.

sources.summary: Seven obligations as a blueprint; importance and order of approach vary with each piece of material.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to fulfill the emotional obligation but keeps playing surface emotion without the underlying need and passion
recommendations.recommendation: Build the emotional pyramid from the bottom: first work for choices that stimulate the CHARACTER'S DESIRE (the passion and need), then find choices that create the OBSTACLES to that desire. The conflict between these creates the emotional experience naturally.

sources.summary: Must work for passion and need first, then obstacles; the conflict between them produces the emotional life organically.
Without the passionate desire, frustration and anger lack the depth of the author's intent.

recommendations.condition: Actor is in a rigid or resistant environment (conservative director, non-exploratory cast) and needs to establish irreverent freedom before scene work
recommendations.recommendation: Do irreverent preparations privately: Animation Workout, Freedom Workout, Outrageous Monologue, or Go Crazy in the dressing room or at home. Use discretion about when to do them publicly vs. privately.

sources.summary: Irreverent preparations in rigid environments: often better done privately; use discretion.

recommendations.condition: The emotional gap between where the actor is and what the scene requires is too large to bridge directly
recommendations.recommendation: Build an interim preparation bridge in steps. First reach a nearby intermediate emotional state, use it as a springboard to the next step, then approach the major choice. Remember: if the obligation is not a stretch, no interim preparation is needed. Avoid over-preparing.

sources.summary: Interim preparation: build bridge in steps; 'more is less, less is more'; if it isn't broken, don't fix it.

recommendations.condition: Actor cannot find a choice for a scene obligation after trying the obvious options
recommendations.recommendation: Use Task Inventory systematically: identify and restate the obligation, find when you've felt that way, identify what stimulated it, find the common denominator, select the best matching experience, specify it as a choice, then determine the choice approach. If all 10 steps fail, use the Fail-Safe Device.

sources.summary: Task Inventory eliminates confusion; practice it even when not needed.

recommendations.condition: Actor's choice repertoire feels limited; they use the same choices repeatedly
recommendations.recommendation: Implement Daily Inventories and regular Choice Hunt practice. Catalogue everyday stimuli encounters specifically and sensorially. Do Choice Hunt lying down to discover emotional experiences not recently accessed.

sources.summary: Actor is actor 24 hours a day; must catalogue choices from daily life.
Choice Hunt accesses buried memories.

recommendations.condition: Actor is working on a scene but is not sure which obligation to address first
recommendations.recommendation: Use the chronology of obligations: identify which obligation is most impelling for the character's behavior in that scene. If unclear, start with Time-and-Place obligation as it builds an environment that takes the actor off the stage and creates public privacy.

sources.summary: Chronology of obligations: most impelling determines where to start.
Time-and-Place is good default when chronology unclear; creates environment and public privacy.

recommendations.condition: No single person in the actor's life matches all the obligation requirements for a character relationship
recommendations.recommendation: Use a conglomerate choice: combine two or more people from your life who, together, produce the needed feelings. Work for the conglomerate through Sense Memory, Endowments, or Imaginary Monologue.

sources.summary: Conglomerate choice: combine several people who together produce the needed feeling when no single person matches.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to select a choice approach for a scene but isn't sure which to use
recommendations.recommendation: Check Available Stimulus first—it is the most accessible. If the environment already contains elements that fulfill the obligation, use them. Only proceed to other choice approaches if Available Stimulus doesn't do the job. Select approach based on what won't interfere with behavioral demands of the scene.

sources.summary: Available Stimulus should be first consideration when preparing to work.

recommendations.condition: Actor has many lines and moves rapidly through dialogue; sensory choice work interferes with the behavioral demands of the scene
recommendations.recommendation: Use Evocative Words as an inner monologue under the lines—find the cluster of words from a relevant personal experience or belief structure that evokes the needed state. Or combine with a previously established place (Sense Memory) to sustain the emotional foundation.

sources.summary: Evocative Words particularly useful when rapid dialogue makes sensory work impractical.

recommendations.condition: The relationship between the actors needs to have a depth, history, or emotional intensity that doesn't yet exist between them
recommendations.recommendation: Do a Believability improvisation together: start with actual truths from both lives, selectively emphasize those that parallel the material's needs, and add untrue elements that create the needed relationship reality. Mix until truth and untruth are indistinguishable.

sources.summary: Believability is particularly important as a collaborative tool for building shared relational reality.

recommendations.condition: The actor needs the scene partner to feel like a specific person from the actor's own life who produces the required emotional relationship
recommendations.recommendation: Use Endowments: start with the real actress and ask sensory questions that explore similarities between her and the imaginary person, then begin sensorially molding—changing colors, shapes, hairline, voice quality—until the conglomerate stimulus of real + imaginary produces the desired relationship reality.

sources.summary: Endowing other actor with features of someone else: start with similarities, use contrasts, endow sensorially.

recommendations.condition: The character requires physical attitudes, primal impulses, or a BEING state that no other choice approach has been able to produce
recommendations.recommendation: Use Externals—Animals. Find an animal that seems to parallel the character's essential nature, and begin the eight-element approach technique (overall rhythm, leading center, secondary center, limitations, weight/balance/power centers, isolations, corresponding/contrasting rhythms, mannerisms). Spend significant time with the animal before attempting translation to human behavior. Morris: 'The experience takes over the entire body and piques impulses that were not there just a few moments ago.'

sources.summary: Externals animals: six purposes; piques primal elements; transforms physical instrument.

recommendations.condition: The scene requires a deeply charged relational reality between two characters, but the actual actors don't yet have that dynamic between them
recommendations.recommendation: Use Imaginary Dialogues: both actors identify their respective obligations, each selects an imaginary person from personal life who parallels the scene's relationship dynamic, then both do Imaginary Monologues simultaneously out loud—relating what they say to each other—until the stimulated life can be carried directly into the scene.

sources.summary: Imaginary Dialogues: each actor selects imaginary person who parallels scene dynamics; done out loud to each other.

recommendations.condition: Selective Emphasis is needed but the actor isn't sure how to apply it to a specific choice approach
recommendations.recommendation: With Available Stimulus: isolate the specific mannerism or feature of the other actor that produces the right response. With Evocative Words: choose the most impacting phrases. With Imaginary Monologues: pick the right person and the right things to say to them. With Sense Memory: direct questions toward the sensory areas that will produce the needed emotion. With Personal Realities: focus only on the real event or aspect of current life that moves in the needed direction.

sources.summary: Selective Emphasis applied across Available Stimulus, Evocative Words, Believability, Imaginary Monologues, Inner Monologues, Affective Memory, Personal Realities.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to feel specific physical attitudes (swagger, authority, vulnerability) that don't arise naturally but would arise from a specific garment or object
recommendations.recommendation: Use Objects that Come into Contact with the Body. If the real object can be used on stage, make it Available Stimulus. If not, systematically sense-memorize the object by working with the real thing, asking detailed sensory questions about its weight, texture, temperature, sound, and pressure—then recreate it sensorially when on stage.

sources.summary: Objects that Contact Body: boots example; sensorially create object if unavailable.

recommendations.condition: The actor has lost contact with organic impulse life during the scene—is on automatic pilot, leadership, or result-consciousness
recommendations.recommendation: Switch immediately to Moment-to-Moment Inner Monologue. Begin including between the words what is actually going on inside—do not try to feel something, just report what is there. Allow whatever is actually present to express itself through the dialogue.

sources.summary: Moment-to-Moment Inner Monologue: antidote for leadership; include what is going on inside between the words of the play.

recommendations.condition: The character's behavior and inner life can be identified as coming from a specific dominant personality facet (e.g., a victim, a warrior, a free spirit, a moralist, a satanic energy)
recommendations.recommendation: Use Sub-Personalities. Identify the sub-personality archetype controlling the character. Study your own expression of that sub-personality—when does it take over? What stimulates it? Then use that knowledge to call it forth during the scene. This approach can address multiple obligations with a single choice.

sources.summary: Sub-Personalities: identify character's controlling sub-personality; elicit same in yourself; one choice for entire play.

recommendations.condition: The scene requires attraction, desire, terror, or curiosity toward someone or something the actor cannot physically touch or access
recommendations.recommendation: Use Sensory Speculation: ask sensory questions speculatively—'What would it feel like to touch her face?...Where in my fingertips would I feel that?...'—responding in the appropriate sense as if experiencing it now. The speculative sensory engagement will actively stimulate the required feelings.

sources.summary: Sensory Speculation: speculatively explore what it would feel like; goes beyond wonderment; excites sensory apparatus.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to fulfill a time-and-place obligation where the actual stage environment is inadequate or wrong for the scene
recommendations.recommendation: Use Creative Visualization: close the eyes, guide yourself into the specific environment, support with all five senses, allow the unconscious to produce thoughts and impulses. Then open the eyes and carry that life into the scene. Can also be done with open eyes through the 'inner eye' once you become facile with the process.

sources.summary: Creative Visualization: excellent for time-and-place obligation; carryover is usually substantial.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to access a personality facet (aggression, sexuality, fear, moralism, free spirit, victimhood) that is suppressed or disowned
recommendations.recommendation: Identify the sub-personality. Use Personal Voice Dialogue: speak to it directly from an objective, ego-state place of consciousness. Physically move to accommodate it. If unresponsive, identify and create the conditions that naturally call it forth in life. With practice and repetition, disowned sub-personalities gain confidence and come forth more easily.

sources.summary: Disowning: antidote is conscious re-contact; repeat until sub-personality gains confidence and comes forth.

recommendations.condition: Actor is experiencing result-consciousness or awareness of being on stage—the acting consciousness is too close to the surface
recommendations.recommendation: The Eleventh Level of Consciousness is a byproduct of deep craft involvement—it cannot be forced. Return to the craft: deepen the choice approach work, use Moment-to-Moment Inner Monologue to anchor in the present reality, and allow the consciousness level to recede as involvement with the reality of the play increases. The more the here-and-now reality is believed, the farther back the awareness of acting is pushed.

sources.summary: Eleventh Level of Consciousness evolves with the work—byproduct of craft proficiency; cannot be created by pushing a button.
